The model learns residual edge perturbations to an unbiased TSP cost using implicit differentiation. A smoothed Held–Karp layer enforces approximate degree constraints to correct structure, while a certificate-guided sharpening mechanism biases the connected distribution toward tour-like (Hamiltonian) structures.
AI.result: Experiments show that C2TSP achieves strong tour quality after decoding, comparable to or better than existing methods, while also maintaining an interpretable latent structure. Ablation studies demonstrate that both edge perturbation learning and certificate-guided sharpening contribute to improved tour costs and more Hamiltonian-like latent distributions.
AI.conclusion: Learning directly in a structurally meaningful latent space for TSP, instantiated via a rooted 1-tree Gibbs family with structural correction (Held–Karp) and sharpening, can provide both competitive decoding performance and insight into the Hamiltonian structure actually learned by the model. This suggests that combinatorially structured latent representations are a promising direction for learning-based combinatorial optimization.

title: Belief-reality separation lives in routing over a shared value slot in language models
summary: Capable language models hold what a character believes apart from what is true: told "Anna believes the cup is blue; in reality it is red," they answer blue about Anna and red about the world. Where in the computation does that separation live? We show it rests on two separable mechanisms at two positions. A generic value slot binds the attributed value. A router at the query position selects which frame, the character's belief or reality, a query reads out. Two routes fill the slot: an asserted belief, whose value the text supplies, binds in directly; a derived belief, whose value must be inferred from what the character could see, arrives by a visibility-gated lookback. A subspace trained on either route steers the other, and only the derived route depends on described visibility. The slot itself carries no belief-reality tag: intervening on it moves a reality readout as strongly as a belief one. The separation lives instead in a dissociated pair of routing subspaces, which flip a query between frames without injecting the donor's value. These results hold across three architectures, on stimuli de-confounded against theory-of-mind-benchmark shortcuts; the behavior itself emerges between 3B and 7B across five model families. This paper develops the single belief-reality axis in depth; a companion paper shows the same slot-and-router format is shared across the other non-actual contexts a sentence can open (counterfactual, fictional, temporal).

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how language models internally separate what a character believes from what is actually true, finding specific mechanisms (a value slot and routing subspaces) that support belief vs. reality representations across models and architectures.
AI.motivation: Large language models can correctly answer questions about agents’ beliefs even when those beliefs differ from reality, which suggests some internal representation of multiple "frames" (belief, reality, etc.). However, it is unclear where and how these distinct frames are implemented mechanistically inside the model, and whether this behavior is robust across architectures and scales or depends on superficial dataset shortcuts.
AI.method: The authors probe and intervene in transformer models using tailored stimuli that explicitly separate a character’s belief from reality (e.g., “Anna believes the cup is blue; in reality it is red”). They identify and characterize a "value slot" that stores the relevant value (e.g., color) and "routers" at query positions that determine whether the model reads out the character’s belief frame or the reality frame. They further distinguish two routes that can populate the belief slot: direct textual assertions of belief vs. beliefs derived via visibility-based inference, studying how these are gated and how trained subspaces on one route can steer the other. They run these analyses across three different architectures and five model families, controlling for theory-of-mind benchmark shortcuts, and use subspace interventions and visibility manipulations to test causal roles.
AI.result: They find that: (1) there exists a generic value slot that binds the attributed value (e.g., blue vs. red) without encoding whether it belongs to belief or reality; (2) a separate routing mechanism at query positions selects which frame (belief vs. reality) to read from; (3) there are two distinct routes that can fill the belief slot—direct assertion and visibility-gated derivation—where only the latter depends on described visibility; (4) subspaces trained on either route can steer the other, showing shared structure; (5) intervening on the value slot affects both belief and reality readouts equally, indicating that belief vs. reality distinction is not stored in the slot; (6) instead, the separation is implemented by a dissociated pair of routing subspaces that can flip queries between frames without transferring value content; and (7) these phenomena are consistent across three architectures and emerge reliably between 3B and 7B parameters across five model families using de-confounded stimuli.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the belief–reality distinction in language models is implemented via a generic value slot plus specialized routing subspaces rather than via separate content-bearing representations for beliefs and reality. The slot stores content agnostically, while routing subspaces determine which contextual frame is read out. This architecture is robust across model types and scales and supports both asserted and derived beliefs, with only the derived path depending on visibility information. The work suggests a common slot-and-router format for handling non-actual contexts, with a companion paper extending the same mechanism to counterfactual, fictional, and temporal frames.

title: The Emerging Paradigm of Geospatial Foundation Models: From Pre-Training to Agentic Reasoning
comment: 18 pages, 4 figures. To appear in Lecture Notes in Computer Science (LNCS)
summary: The analysis of satellite and aerial imagery has entered a new era with the advent of foundation models. This paper describes the concept of Geospatial Foundation Models (GeoFMs), which are artificial intelligence/machine learning (AI/ML) models pre-trained on massive geospatial datasets through varied methodologies. We first articulate the core paradigm shift that GeoFMs enable: a separation of duties, where large-scale model providers perform the computationally intensive pretraining, allowing domain experts to rapidly fine-tune or prompt these models for specific, mission-critical tasks. This approach democratizes access to state-of-the-art AI/ML while maintaining the security and confidentiality of the downstream task. We then explore the novel capabilities unlocked by different types of GeoFMs, distinguishing between the finetunable vision models produced by self-supervised techniques like masked auto-encoding, and the vision-language models produced by contrastive learning which enable zero-shot tasks like open-vocabulary image analysis. Next, we discuss the practical considerations for operationalizing GeoFMs, from performance-cost analysis to the broader MLOps ecosystem. To that end, we introduce a taxonomy of model adaptation strategies and propose a framework for domain experts to select the most cost-effective adaptation approach for their particular mission set. Finally, we present a forward-looking vision of Agentic Geospatial Reasoning, where Large Language Models act as intelligent orchestrators, leveraging GeoFMs as tools to answer high-level user queries in natural language and automate complex analytical workflows, moving the field from perception to cognition.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and defines Geospatial Foundation Models (GeoFMs) for satellite and aerial imagery, explains their types and capabilities, provides guidance on how to adapt and deploy them cost-effectively, and outlines a future where LLMs orchestrate GeoFMs for higher-level geospatial reasoning.
AI.motivation: Satellite and aerial imagery analysis increasingly relies on large AI/ML models, but training such models is computationally expensive and technically demanding for most domain experts. There is a need to separate the heavy pretraining from downstream application work, democratize access to powerful models, ensure security for sensitive tasks, and clarify how to practically adopt and adapt these models in real missions.
AI.method: The paper conceptually defines GeoFMs and categorizes them into self-supervised finetunable vision models and contrastive vision-language models. It analyzes their capabilities (e.g., finetuning vs zero-shot), provides a taxonomy of adaptation strategies, and proposes a decision framework for choosing cost-effective adaptation paths. It also sketches an architecture where LLMs act as agents that orchestrate GeoFMs for complex workflows.
AI.result: The main results are a clear conceptual framework for understanding GeoFMs, a distinction between different GeoFM types and their capabilities, a taxonomy of model adaptation strategies coupled with cost-performance considerations, and an architectural vision for integrating GeoFMs with LLM-based agents for geospatial reasoning.
AI.conclusion: GeoFMs mark a paradigm shift in geospatial AI by enabling a separation of duties between large-scale model providers and domain experts, thereby democratizing access to powerful models while preserving confidentiality. Different GeoFM types unlock new capabilities from finetuning to zero-shot analysis. With appropriate adaptation strategies and MLOps support, GeoFMs can be operationalized effectively, and, combined with LLM-based agents, can move geospatial analysis from low-level perception toward higher-level cognitive reasoning and automated workflows.

title: Hybrid Continual Learning for Low-Resource Australian Aboriginal Language Identification
summary: Language identification is an important step toward integrating endangered Australian Aboriginal languages (AALs) into speech technologies supporting language revitalisation and digital inclusion. However, extreme data scarcity limits model performance. Transfer learning from high-resource languages shows promise but often suffers from catastrophic forgetting when adapting to new languages. Continual learning (CL) can mitigate this issue, though it remains challenging with very limited data. To address this, we propose two hybrid continual learning methods: Replay Augmented Elastic Weight Consolidation and Constraint Guided Knowledge Distillation to adapt pretrained speech models for AAL identification while preserving previously learned knowledge. Experiments on Warlpiri, Dalabon and Dharawal show that the proposed methods outperform fine-tuning and existing CL baselines, improving adaptation to multiple AALs while maintaining performance on previously learnt high-resource languages.

AI.tldr: They improve language identification for endangered Australian Aboriginal languages by using two hybrid continual learning methods that adapt pretrained speech models without forgetting high‑resource languages.
AI.motivation: Endangered Australian Aboriginal languages are underrepresented in speech technologies, yet language identification is crucial for revitalisation and digital inclusion. Existing transfer learning approaches from high‑resource languages work but suffer from catastrophic forgetting when adapting to new low‑resource languages, and standard continual learning is hard under extreme data scarcity. The paper aims to create methods that can adapt to multiple AALs while preserving performance on previously learned high‑resource languages.
AI.method: They start from pretrained speech models and introduce two hybrid continual learning strategies tailored to low‑resource AALs: (1) Replay Augmented Elastic Weight Consolidation, which combines parameter regularisation (EWC) with replaying stored examples to prevent forgetting; and (2) Constraint Guided Knowledge Distillation, which distills knowledge from earlier model states under explicit constraints to preserve prior knowledge while learning new AALs. These methods are applied for language identification across both high‑resource languages and multiple AALs.
AI.result: On three Australian Aboriginal languages—Warlpiri, Dalabon and Dharawal—the proposed hybrid continual learning methods outperform simple fine‑tuning and existing continual learning baselines. They yield better adaptation to the new AALs while maintaining or improving performance on the previously learned high‑resource languages, demonstrating reduced catastrophic forgetting under severe data scarcity.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid continual learning approaches that combine replay, regularisation, and constrained knowledge distillation are effective for adapting pretrained speech models to endangered, low‑resource languages. These methods improve language identification for multiple Australian Aboriginal languages while preserving performance on high‑resource languages, offering a promising direction for speech technology that supports language revitalisation and digital inclusion in low‑resource settings.

title: Cost-Governed RAG: Unified Per-Tenant Cost Attribution Across Retrieval and Generation in Multi-Tenant LLM Systems
summary: Enterprise Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) deployments face a critical governance gap: while LLM generation cost is metered per token, the retrieval layer - vector memory, similarity compute, and embedding API calls - remains an unattributed shared cost, enabling invisible cross-subsidization among tenants. We present Cost-Governed RAG, an architecture that integrates a codebook-oblivious vector index (TurboVec) with a multi-tenant LLM governance gateway, creating a unified observability stack where embedding, retrieval, and generation costs are jointly attributable per tenant. The architecture exploits TurboVec's deterministic, closed-form memory formula to enable near-exact per-tenant retrieval cost calculation - a property unavailable in graph-based indexes with non-linear memory overhead. Deployed on Snowpark Container Services within a cloud data platform's governance boundary, the system achieves 99.96% end-to-end cost attribution accuracy across 100 simulated tenants (10M vectors, log-normal size distribution) with telemetry overhead below 0.04% of query latency. The architecture reduces retrieval infrastructure cost by 3.1-9.0x compared to managed vector database services under the pricing assumptions detailed in Section IV. We formalize a three-layer cost model and demonstrate that codebook-oblivious quantization enables deterministic per-tenant cost attribution while also removing the shared-codebook leakage surface present in trained quantizers - the latter observation being exploratory and subject to the limitations described in Section VII.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a cost-governed RAG architecture that makes retrieval and embedding costs precisely attributable per tenant, achieving accurate multi-tenant cost governance and cheaper infrastructure than managed vector DBs.
AI.motivation: In enterprise RAG systems, LLM generation costs are easily metered per token, but the retrieval layer (vector storage, similarity search compute, embedding calls) is typically a shared, unattributed cost. This leads to invisible cross-subsidization between tenants and weak governance, as heavy users can consume disproportionate retrieval resources without corresponding chargeback or accountability. Existing vector index structures, especially graph-based ones, lack deterministic, closed-form memory and cost models, making fine-grained attribution difficult or impossible. The authors want a design where every part of the RAG stack—embedding, retrieval, and generation—can be observed, metered, and charged per tenant within standard enterprise governance boundaries.
AI.method: The paper introduces Cost-Governed RAG, which combines a codebook-oblivious vector index called TurboVec with a multi-tenant LLM governance gateway. TurboVec provides a deterministic closed-form formula for memory usage, allowing near-exact calculation of per-tenant retrieval costs (storage and similarity search) without needing tenant-specific codebooks. The system is deployed on Snowpark Container Services within a cloud data platform’s governance boundary, integrating telemetry from embedding, retrieval, and generation. The authors define a three-layer cost model (embedding, retrieval infrastructure, and LLM generation) and simulate 100 tenants with 10 million vectors following a log-normal size distribution. They compare cost and attribution accuracy against graph-based or managed vector database alternatives and assess telemetry overhead on query latency. They also analyze how codebook-oblivious quantization avoids the shared-codebook leakage issues present in trained quantizers.
AI.result: In experiments with 100 simulated tenants and 10 million vectors, the architecture achieves 99.96% end-to-end cost attribution accuracy, meaning almost all retrieval and embedding resource usage can be correctly attributed to individual tenants. Telemetry overhead is minimal, adding less than 0.04% to query latency. Compared to managed vector database services, the proposed approach reduces retrieval infrastructure costs by a factor of 3.1 to 9.0, under specified pricing assumptions. The authors also show that deterministic memory modeling is enabled by codebook-oblivious quantization and argue that this design eliminates the shared-codebook leakage surface found in trained quantizers, though this security claim is exploratory and limited by assumptions discussed in Section VII.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that precise, per-tenant cost governance for enterprise RAG systems is achievable when the retrieval layer is redesigned around deterministic, codebook-oblivious indexing (TurboVec) and integrated with a multi-tenant LLM governance gateway. This combination enables a unified observability stack where embedding, retrieval, and generation costs are jointly metered with high accuracy, transforming retrieval from a shared, opaque cost center into an attributable, controllable resource. The approach not only significantly lowers retrieval infrastructure costs relative to managed vector databases but also offers a cleaner security posture by removing shared-codebook leakage surfaces. However, the authors caution that some of the security and leakage-resistance claims are preliminary and must be validated under broader threat models and deployment conditions.

title: Evaluating Nonuniform Dependability Across Response Conditions: A Conditional Generalizability Framework Illustrated in Automated Essay Scoring
summary: Aggregate reliability estimates can obscure heterogeneity in measurement-design burden across response conditions, so a single G- or D-study may mischaracterize a design's adequacy for particular strata. This study introduces a conditional generalizability framework with three components. First, automated scoring configurations -- the encoder architectures and scoring-head families admissible within a fixed pipeline -- are treated as a universe of admissible measurement conditions rather than incidental modeling choices. Second, analytical D-study projections are compared with empirical configuration sweeps over a finite scoring pool, yielding two estimands of design adequacy whose agreement or divergence diagnoses the realized configuration universe. Third, evidence is conditioned on entropy-defined response strata, treating entropy as an operational stratification variable, not a construct claim about writing quality. Whereas recent generalizability-theory extensions address AI-generated item variants on the response side, this framework addresses the analogous scoring-side problem: AI-mediated scoring configurations. Demonstrated with automated essay scoring of timed L2 writing, the realized design was dependable in aggregate (Phi approx 0.76). Re-estimated within entropy strata, dependability stayed high but declined modestly and robustly (Phi = 0.88, 0.87, 0.84) -- a gradient implying different decision-study requirements, the highest-entropy stratum requiring the most crossed conditions. The framework offers a portable workflow for evaluating nonuniform dependability.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a framework to examine how reliably automated scoring systems perform across different types of responses, showing that overall reliability can hide important differences between response strata defined by entropy.
AI.motivation: Traditional generalizability or decision studies in assessment report a single reliability estimate, which can mask how measurement error and design burden differ across subgroups of responses (e.g., easier vs harder-to-score essays). With the rise of AI-based scoring, there is a need to understand not just item-side variability (AI-generated items) but also scoring-side variability (different model configurations), and to ensure that designs are adequate for all response strata, not just on average.
AI.method: The authors develop a conditional generalizability framework with three key ideas: (1) treat automated scoring configurations—combinations of encoder architectures and scoring heads within a fixed NLP pipeline—as a formal universe of admissible measurement conditions; (2) estimate design adequacy in two ways: using analytical D-study projections and by empirically sweeping over a finite pool of scoring configurations, then compare these estimands to diagnose how well the realized pool represents the intended universe; and (3) condition all evidence on response strata defined by entropy, using entropy as an operational stratification variable reflecting how inherently uncertain or complex a response is for the scoring system, without interpreting it as a direct measure of writing quality. The framework is then instantiated in an automated essay scoring application for timed L2 writing.
AI.result: In the empirical demonstration, the overall dependability coefficient (Phi) of the automated essay scoring design is about 0.76, indicating acceptable reliability in aggregate. However, when the analysis is repeated within entropy-defined strata of responses, dependability remains high but shows a systematic decline across strata, with Phi values of 0.88, 0.87, and 0.84 from lowest to highest entropy. This gradient reveals that higher-entropy (harder-to-score) responses would require more crossed measurement conditions—such as additional scoring configurations—to achieve equivalent decision accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The conditional generalizability framework reveals nonuniform dependability that aggregate estimates would miss and provides a portable workflow for assessment designers to evaluate how well their automated scoring designs perform for different response strata. By explicitly treating model configurations as measurement conditions and using entropy-based conditioning, practitioners can better diagnose when and where more complex or intensive designs are needed to support fair and accurate decisions across all responses.

title: A Threshold Exceedance Framework for CBRN Uplift Evaluation in Frontier Language Models
summary: As frontier language models advance, policymakers and model developers need methods for assessing whether model access materially increases a non-expert actor's ability to plan high-consequence Chemical, Biological, Radiological, or Nuclear (CBRN) misuse relative to public tools alone. Existing CBRN evaluations differ in non-expert definitions, threat scope, baselines, scoring rubrics, and decision rules, making results difficult to compare across studies. We introduce a Threshold Exceedance Criteria (TEC) framework that decomposes an uplift study into independently executable components: determining non-expert participant eligibility, defining the CBRN threat scope for the study, and statistically estimating material uplift. We then operationalize the TEC framework in a large-scale empirical study using a design that determines two forms of uplift: generative (where a model assists plan creation from scratch) and revisionist (where a model assists refinement of an existing plan). The study produced attack plans across the CBRN domains, which we evaluated through subject-matter-expert review to estimate generative and revisionist uplift. Applying the framework, our empirical study revealed domain heterogeneity: under this controlled pre-release evaluation, model-assisted plans sometimes received expert-equivalent instructional ratings, but confirmed material uplift was limited to the radiological domain. These findings informed mitigation and deployment-governance decisions rather than characterizing deployed model behavior. We conclude with methodological lessons for future CBRN uplift evaluations, emphasizing prespecified criteria, explicit baselines, separation of generative and revisionist estimates, and careful distinction between preliminary screening signals and confirmed risk determinations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a standardized framework (TEC) to rigorously assess whether access to frontier language models materially improves non-experts’ ability to plan high-impact CBRN attacks, and demonstrates its use in a large empirical study finding limited but real uplift in the radiological domain.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of how language models might enable chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear misuse are inconsistent and hard to compare because they differ in who they treat as a non-expert, which threats they cover, what baseline tools they compare against, and how uplift is scored and judged. Policymakers and developers need a more principled, comparable way to decide if a model release materially increases misuse risk and to guide mitigation and deployment decisions.
AI.method: The authors introduce the Threshold Exceedance Criteria (TEC) framework, which breaks an uplift study into three separable components: (1) defining and screening for non-expert participants; (2) explicitly specifying the CBRN threat scope; and (3) statistically estimating whether model access leads to “material uplift” beyond public baselines. They then implement this framework in a large-scale controlled experiment with human participants working on CBRN attack-planning tasks. Two conditions are tested: generative uplift (participants create plans from scratch with model help) and revisionist uplift (participants refine pre-existing plans with model help). Plans produced across CBRN domains are evaluated and scored by domain subject-matter experts, and the results are analyzed to determine whether TEC thresholds for material uplift are exceeded.
AI.result: In the empirical implementation, the study generated and refined attack plans spanning chemical, biological, radiological, and nuclear domains. Expert raters sometimes judged model-assisted plans to have instructional quality comparable to expert-written plans, but statistically confirmed material uplift—according to the TEC thresholds—was observed only in the radiological domain. Other domains showed weaker or less consistent evidence that access to the model significantly improved non-experts’ planning capability relative to public tools alone.
AI.conclusion: The TEC framework provides a structured, modular methodology for CBRN uplift evaluation that improves clarity and comparability across studies. The authors’ empirical results show domain-specific risk, with meaningful uplift confirmed only for radiological misuse in their pre-release test, and they use these findings to shape mitigations and deployment governance rather than to describe deployed model behavior. They recommend future work adopt prespecified decision criteria, clearly defined baselines, separate reporting of generative vs. revisionist uplift, and a clear distinction between early screening indicators and robustly confirmed risk, to better support policy and release decisions around frontier language models.

title: Agentic systems for breast cancer treatment recommendations
comment: Under peer review. Source code available at: https://github.com/GRUPOMED4U/breast_cancer_agents_paper
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly being explored for clinical decision support, but their reliability in complex oncology treatment planning remains unclear. We evaluated agentic LLM systems for breast cancer treatment recommendation generation using 72 real clinical cases across stages I to IV and 1,147 case-specific rubrics generated through Asymmetric Information Rubric Generation (AIRG), in which the rubric generator had access to real clinical decisions unavailable to the evaluated models. Seven pipelines were compared, including single-LLM baselines, tool-augmented systems, and multi-agent architectures with fact checking and autonomous subagent spawning. The best-performing configuration, Claude Opus 4.8 with the D&C+SA pipeline, achieved a global score of 0.594 $\pm$ 0.025. Tool use and increased agent autonomy had mixed effects, improving performance in some settings but degrading it in others. Performance varied by clinical domain and disease stage, and oncologist-led error analysis revealed persistent clinically relevant failures, including incorrect or missing recommendations, flawed justifications, citation errors, outdated claims, and overconfidence. These findings suggest that agentic LLM systems can generate clinically relevant breast cancer recommendations, but remain insufficient for unsupervised clinical use.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well different large language model (LLM) agent pipelines can recommend breast cancer treatments and finds they are helpful but not reliable enough for unsupervised clinical use.
AI.motivation: LLMs are being considered for clinical decision support, including oncology, but their reliability in complex, high‑stakes treatment planning is uncertain. Breast cancer management involves nuanced, stage‑specific, and rapidly evolving standards of care, so it is important to rigorously assess whether LLMs and more advanced agentic pipelines (with tools, multiple agents, and autonomy) can safely and accurately generate treatment recommendations in realistic scenarios.
AI.method: The authors tested several LLM-based pipelines on 72 real breast cancer cases spanning stages I–IV. They used Asymmetric Information Rubric Generation (AIRG), where one model with access to the true clinical decisions created detailed, case-specific evaluation rubrics (1,147 in total), while the evaluated systems had no access to these ground-truth decisions. Seven different pipelines were compared: simple single-LLM baselines, tool-augmented systems, and multi-agent architectures featuring fact-checking and autonomous subagent spawning. Performance was scored against the AIRG rubrics to obtain global and domain-specific metrics, and oncologists conducted error analysis of the generated recommendations.
AI.result: Among the evaluated systems, the best-performing configuration was Claude Opus 4.8 using a "D&C+SA" (divide-and-conquer plus subagent) pipeline, achieving a global score of 0.594 ± 0.025 on the AIRG metrics. Tool use and greater agent autonomy did not uniformly improve results: they helped in some clinical domains and case types but worsened performance in others. Performance also varied by disease stage and domain. Expert review identified recurring clinically important errors, such as missing or incorrect treatment recommendations, flawed reasoning, citation and referencing mistakes, outdated medical claims, and unwarranted overconfidence in incorrect answers.
AI.conclusion: Agentic LLM systems can often generate plausible and clinically relevant breast cancer treatment recommendations, but current performance levels and persistent, clinically meaningful errors make them unsafe for unsupervised clinical deployment. The mixed impact of tools and autonomy suggests that more careful design, evaluation, and guardrails are needed before such systems can be trusted in real-world oncology decision support.

title: Good Benchmarks
summary: Good tasks are correct, solvable, verifiable, well-specified, and hard for interesting reasons. The best tasks describe a real problem an experienced practitioner would recognize, in language a practitioner would use, with tests that verify the outcome rather than the approach.

AI.tldr: Defines characteristics of high-quality tasks in a problem-setting or evaluation context, emphasizing realism, clarity, correctness, and outcome-based verification.
AI.motivation: To formalize what makes a task or benchmark genuinely useful and meaningful, especially in practical or applied settings where existing tasks may be artificial, underspecified, or misaligned with real practitioner needs.
AI.method: Conceptual/criteria-based: proposes a set of qualitative properties (correct, solvable, verifiable, well-specified, and hard for interesting reasons) and describes them in terms of how they relate to real-world practitioner problems and outcome-based testing.
AI.result: Provides a concise definition and checklist-style characterization of “good tasks” that can guide the design and evaluation of tasks, benchmarks, or exercises, focusing attention on realistic language, practitioner relevance, and verification by outcomes instead of methods.
AI.conclusion: Tasks are most valuable when they mirror real practitioner problems, are clearly and precisely stated, admit solutions that can be checked, and are challenging in a non-arbitrary way, with evaluations based on whether the solution works rather than how it is obtained.

title: Beyond Parallel Tracking: Interactive Multi-Feature Fusion Drives Semantic Reconstruction from Non-invasive Brain Recordings
summary: Continuous semantic reconstruction from non-invasive neural recordings remains limited by the representational mismatch between semantic feature spaces and neural coding patterns, which severely impedes cross-modal alignment between high-noise neural signals and target semantic features. Prior semantic decoders have predominantly relied on static lexical representations or dynamic contextualized representations in isolation. This single-dimension approach inevitably leads to severe information loss, as it fails to account for the human brain's capacity to integrate stable word attributes and dynamic contexts simultaneously.To bridge this gap, this study introduces a multi-feature fusion framework for non-invasive semantic reconstruction, systematically benchmarking two integration approaches: linear Naive Concatenation and non-linear Multi-Head Cross-Attention. Within this framework, our approach complements static lexical representations (W2V) with dynamic contextual representations (GPT) via an interactive gating mechanism to facilitate cooperative processing during language comprehension.Evaluated through extensive semantic reconstruction and text generation experiments, our framework reveals a robust performance hierarchy: Cross-Att > Concat > GPT > W2V. Crucially, the non-linear cross-attention fusion method achieves state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating that neural language decoding benefits from simulating the collaborative modulation between contextual information and core lexical attributes rather than depending on isolated individual features, while also offering a viable non-invasive brain-to-text decoding method.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a multi-feature fusion framework that combines static (W2V) and dynamic (GPT) semantic representations using concatenation and cross-attention to improve non-invasive brain-to-text decoding, with cross-attention achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Non-invasive neural recordings are noisy and show a representational mismatch with existing semantic feature spaces, limiting accurate continuous semantic reconstruction. Prior decoders use either static or dynamic word representations alone, which loses information and ignores how the brain integrates both stable lexical properties and dynamic context. The authors aim to create a representation that better matches neural coding by jointly modeling these complementary semantic aspects.
AI.method: The authors design a multi-feature fusion framework that integrates static word embeddings (Word2Vec) and dynamic contextual embeddings (from GPT). They benchmark two integration strategies: (1) linear Naive Concatenation of W2V and GPT features, and (2) a non-linear Multi-Head Cross-Attention module that allows interactive gating and cooperative processing between the two feature types. These fused semantic representations are aligned with non-invasive neural signals for semantic reconstruction and downstream text generation tasks.
AI.result: Experiments on semantic reconstruction and brain-to-text generation show a clear performance ordering of models: Cross-Attention fusion outperforms Concatenation, which in turn outperforms using GPT alone, which outperforms W2V alone. The cross-attention fusion method attains state-of-the-art decoding accuracy for non-invasive neural data in the tested setting.
AI.conclusion: Combining static lexical and dynamic contextual semantic representations yields better alignment with brain activity than using either alone. Non-linear cross-attention fusion most effectively captures the cooperative modulation between contextual information and core lexical attributes, leading to state-of-the-art performance in non-invasive neural language decoding and offering a promising path for brain-to-text systems.

title: Rethinking the Evaluation of Harness Evolution for Agents
summary: We revisit the evaluation of automatic harness evolution for LLM agents. Existing harness evolution methods use unit test cases to search for harness configurations and then report final performance on the same public benchmark. This protocol raises two fundamental concerns. First, harness evolution is itself an iterative search procedure that repeatedly evaluates and revises candidate harnesses using task feedback. As in agentic test-time scaling, it should therefore be compared with simple task-level search baselines under matched feedback and inference budgets to determine whether its gains arise from improved harness design or from additional search alone. Second, because the search and the final evaluation share the same benchmark, the reported gains risk overfitting to that specific task set. To address these concerns, we conduct an extensive evaluation comparing harness evolution with simple test-time scaling and discovery baselines under comparable feedback and inference budgets, and also evaluate evolved harnesses on held-out tasks to assess whether the discovered improvements generalize. Experiments on Terminal-Bench 2.1 with GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus 4.6 show that automatic harness evolution does not consistently outperform simple test-time scaling methods and exhibits limited generalization. Our results raise important questions about the effectiveness of automatic harness evolution and highlight the need for fairer evaluation protocols and benchmarks for automatic harness design. Our code is available at https://github.com/rethinking-harness-evolution.

AI.tldr: The paper reevaluates automatic harness evolution for LLM agents and finds that, under fair comparisons and held-out tests, it does not consistently beat simple test-time scaling and generalizes poorly.
AI.motivation: Existing work claims that evolving prompt/tool harnesses for LLM agents improves performance, but typically (1) compares against weak or unmatched baselines that do not receive the same test-time feedback/inference budget, and (2) tunes and evaluates on the same benchmark, risking overfitting. The authors want to know whether harness evolution truly helps beyond just doing more search at test time, and whether the discovered harnesses transfer to new tasks.
AI.method: They systematically compare automatic harness evolution methods to simple task-level test-time scaling and discovery baselines while tightly controlling feedback and inference budgets. They treat harness evolution as a search process and match it with equally powerful but simpler search procedures. They then evaluate the resulting harnesses both on the benchmark used during search (Terminal-Bench 2.1) and on held-out tasks to test generalization. Experiments are run with GPT-5.4 and Claude Opus 4.6.
AI.result: On Terminal-Bench 2.1, automatic harness evolution does not reliably outperform simpler test-time scaling approaches when all methods are given comparable feedback and inference budgets. Moreover, harnesses evolved on the benchmark show limited gains on held-out tasks, indicating weak generalization and suggesting overfitting to the search benchmark.
AI.conclusion: The purported advantages of automatic harness evolution for LLM agents are overstated under current evaluation practices. When evaluated fairly, its benefits over simple test-time scaling are inconsistent and do not generalize well. The authors argue that the community needs more rigorous, budget-matched baselines and new benchmarks and protocols for assessing automatic harness design. They also release code for reproducibility and further study.

title: The Capacity of Thought: Benchmarking Llama 3.2 in Semantic fMRI Neural Language Decoding and Improving the Huth Encoding-Model Baseline
comment: 7 pages, 2 tables, 2 figures. Preprint. NLP 244: Advanced Machine Learning for Natural Language Processing report, UC Santa Cruz
summary: Decoding continuous language from fMRI signals remains a core challenge in non-invasive brain-computer interface research. We present two complementary investigations. First, we improve the Huth et al. ridge regression encoding pipeline through expanded voxel selection (10K->15K), substitution of GPT-2 medium for GPT-1 as the beam-search proposal model, and GPU-accelerated bootstrap training, achieving mean METEOR = 0.149 and BLEU-1 = 0.200 across three held-out narratives for subject UTS03 -- an 11% relative METEOR gain over our replication baseline. Second, we introduce fMRIFlamingo, which maps BOLD activity to a frozen Llama-3.2-1B with trainable gated cross-attention layers via a learned brain tokenizer and a Perceiver Resampler. Despite achieving 42.86% Top-1 accuracy on a 1-in-100 ranking task, well above chance, a blind control ablation with zeroed fMRI inputs yields near-identical scores, revealing that apparent decoding success is driven primarily by the frozen language prior rather than by neural input. These results demonstrate that high-capacity language models do not inherently improve fMRI decoding and can actively obscure failures without rigorous blind-control evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well continuous language can be decoded from fMRI, improving a classic regression baseline and testing a new large-language-model-based decoder, and finds that stronger language models can give misleadingly good scores without actually using brain signals.
AI.motivation: Non-invasive brain-computer interfaces want to decode rich, continuous language from fMRI, but performance is modest and recent work often assumes that plugging in larger language models will improve decoding. There is concern that strong language priors might appear to decode brain activity while actually ignoring it, so the authors aim to both improve a standard baseline and critically test LLM-based methods with rigorous controls.
AI.method: 1) Baseline enhancement: They refine the Huth et al. ridge-regression encoding/decoding pipeline by increasing the number of selected voxels from 10K to 15K, replacing GPT-1 with GPT-2 medium as the model that proposes candidate sentences during beam search, and speeding training via GPU-accelerated bootstrapping. They evaluate METEOR and BLEU-1 on held-out narrative data for subject UTS03. 2) LLM-based decoder: They build fMRIFlamingo, which maps BOLD fMRI signals into the input space of a frozen Llama-3.2-1B language model using a learned brain tokenizer plus a Perceiver Resampler and gated cross-attention layers. Only the cross-modal components are trainable, while the LLM is frozen. They test language decoding performance and additionally perform a blind control where the fMRI inputs are zeroed out to see how much decoding depends on true neural information.
AI.result: The improved ridge-regression based pipeline obtains mean METEOR = 0.149 and BLEU-1 = 0.200 across three held-out narratives for subject UTS03, which is an 11% relative improvement in METEOR over their replication of the original baseline. The fMRIFlamingo model appears to perform well on a 1-in-100 sentence ranking task, reaching 42.86% Top-1 accuracy, which is far above chance. However, when they run a control experiment that zeros out the fMRI inputs, the model’s ranking accuracy remains almost unchanged, indicating that the model’s apparent success is not driven by actual brain data.
AI.conclusion: Incremental engineering on classic regression-based pipelines can yield measurable gains in language decoding from fMRI, but simply coupling fMRI with a powerful frozen large language model is not sufficient to obtain genuine neural decoding. High-capacity language models can produce seemingly strong decoding metrics based mostly on their internal language priors rather than on fMRI signals, and this can mask failures unless strict blind-control tests (such as zero-input ablations) are used. Therefore, future work must include rigorous controls to verify that decoding performance truly reflects information extracted from brain activity.

title: On-Device Deep Research at 4B: Exposure Bounds Faithfulness, Retrieval Bounds Coverage
summary: On-device research agents search a corpus, read sources, and write a cited brief on a personal laptop. Whether their citations are faithful, and at what cost, is unmeasured for a deployable small model. This study fixes one 4B generator on a 24 GB laptop and asks what makes its citations faithful. It separates two quantities usually reported as one number. Cited claim faithfulness asks whether the cited source supports the claim. Trustworthy coverage asks whether the agent also cites the right sources. The study crosses how much of each source the generator sees, 400 against 1500 characters, with the quality of the sources supplied, gold papers against retrieved papers. Two levers fall out, and they act on different outcomes. Exposure sets faithfulness. More of each source lifts faithfulness from 0.45 to 0.58 on retrieved sources and from 0.37 to 0.58 on gold sources, and the two settings converge, so faithfulness is bound by exposure, not by whether the source is correct. The exposure lift is robust to a second, independent judge; the exact convergence is tight under the primary judge and only approximate under the second. Retrieval sets coverage. Trustworthy coverage stays near 0.22 on retrieved sources at any exposure, because recall is held near 0.40, so exposure cannot fix which sources are cited. The extra exposure costs about 235 output tokens. The practical recipe is to raise per source exposure first, cheaply, and then treat retrieval recall as the only remaining lever.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how to make citations from an on-device small language model more faithful and well-covered, showing that giving the model more text from each source improves faithfulness, while retrieval quality determines coverage.
AI.motivation: There is rising interest in running retrieval-augmented research assistants locally on small hardware, but we lack measurements of how faithful their citations are and what practical levers (input length, retrieval quality) actually improve faithfulness and coverage under realistic constraints.
AI.method: Using a fixed 4B-parameter generator running on a 24 GB laptop, the authors systematically vary (1) per-source exposure length (400 vs. 1500 characters) and (2) the quality of supplied sources (gold, i.e., ground-truth relevant, vs. retrieved papers). They then separately measure cited-claim faithfulness (does the cited source truly support the generated claim?) and trustworthy coverage (does the agent cite the right sources overall), with evaluation by a primary and an independent secondary judge.
AI.result: Increasing per-source exposure substantially raises cited-claim faithfulness for both retrieved and gold sources (e.g., from about 0.45 to 0.58 on retrieved and 0.37 to 0.58 on gold), and performance on gold vs. retrieved sources converges, indicating faithfulness is constrained mainly by how much of the source the model can read, not by whether the source is itself perfect. However, trustworthy coverage remains around 0.22 when using retrieved sources regardless of exposure, because retrieval recall is capped near 0.40. The added exposure incurs a cost of about 235 additional output tokens.
AI.conclusion: Two distinct levers control different aspects of citation quality for on-device small models: per-source exposure length determines cited-claim faithfulness, while retrieval recall determines trustworthy coverage. In practice, one should first cheaply increase how much of each source the model sees to maximize faithfulness, and then focus optimization efforts on improving retrieval recall, since exposure alone cannot fix which sources are cited.

title: CityBehavEx: A Scalable and Empirically Validated LLM-Assisted Urban Simulation Platform
summary: Recent LLM-based multi-agent urban simulators can generate semantically rich city routines, but they remain costly to scale and are often weakly validated against empirical mobility patterns. We present CityBehavEx, an interactive LLM-assisted urban simulation platform that scales to city-size populations, exposes agent behavior for inspection, supports empirical validation, and generates mobility patterns that better match real-world spatial, temporal, and semantic distributions. Instead of invoking large language models for every agent action, CityBehavEx combines established human mobility models with fine-tuned cross-encoders that estimate semantic alignment between agent profiles, schedules, and activity transitions. This design enables large-scale simulations, as demonstrated in a case study of 100,000 agents over 75 days in under one hour on a single consumer GPU. The platform allows users to define simulation regions, launch experiments, inspect trajectories and activity traces, debug unrealistic behaviors, and validate generated routines against real-world mobility, time-use, and semantic metrics.

AI.tldr: CityBehavEx is an LLM-assisted but scalable urban simulation platform that produces city-scale mobility routines whose spatial, temporal, and semantic patterns more closely match real-world data.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent urban simulators create rich semantic behaviors but are expensive to scale and are rarely well-validated against real mobility data, limiting their practical use for realistic, large-scale city simulations.
AI.method: CityBehavEx avoids calling LLMs for every agent step by combining classical human mobility models with fine-tuned cross-encoder models that score semantic consistency between agent profiles, schedules, and activity transitions. The system is implemented as an interactive platform where users can configure regions and experiments, run large simulations, and inspect and debug agent trajectories and routines.
AI.result: In a case study, CityBehavEx scales to a simulation of 100,000 agents over 75 days in less than one hour on a single consumer GPU, and the generated mobility patterns better match real-world spatial, temporal, and semantic distributions compared to prior LLM-heavy simulators.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging learned semantic alignment models on top of traditional mobility models, CityBehavEx achieves both scalability and empirical realism, while providing tools for inspection, debugging, and validation of urban agent behaviors against real-world metrics.

title: How Many Tasks Are Enough for Agent Benchmark Decisions? A Replay Analysis of Public LLM Agent Benchmarks
comment: KDD 2026 Workshop Agentic AI Evaluation and Trustworthiness
summary: Agent benchmarks often compare two agents after all tasks have run, but costly evaluations make partial runs tempting. A task fraction alone does not show whether a partial run supports the same pairwise conclusion as the completed benchmark. We study this question by replaying completed public task-level records from SWE-bench, AppWorld, and tau-bench. A partial budget counts as enough only when it supports the completed benchmark's decision, covers required task groups, and leaves no more than a target fraction of comparisons unresolved. The required task fraction varies sharply. At the strict 0 percentage point threshold on a 5 percentage point budget grid, AppWorld first meets all targets at 15 percent, tau-bench at 25 percent, and SWE-bench Verified at 90 percent; SWE-bench Lite does not meet all targets by 95 percent under the primary coverage rule. Partial-evaluation reports should state how much one agent must outperform another, how tasks are selected, what coverage rule is required, what decision rule is used, and how many comparisons may remain unresolved.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes when partial benchmark runs between AI agents are reliable enough to preserve the same winner as full evaluations, using data from SWE-bench, AppWorld, and tau-bench.
AI.motivation: Benchmarking agents is expensive, so practitioners often want to stop early and use partial task results, but it is unclear how many tasks are needed for partial results to match the conclusions of full benchmarks.
AI.method: The authors replay full, public, task-level benchmark logs from SWE-bench, AppWorld, and tau-bench, simulating partial evaluations by subsampling tasks. They define a partial budget as sufficient only if it yields the same pairwise ranking as the full benchmark, satisfies benchmark-specific coverage rules over task groups, and leaves at most a given fraction of pairwise comparisons unresolved. They then measure, for various target thresholds, what fraction of tasks is needed for each benchmark to meet these criteria.
AI.result: The fraction of tasks required to safely use partial results differs sharply across benchmarks. At a strict 0 percentage-point win-margin threshold and a 5 percentage-point budget grid, AppWorld first meets all requirements at 15% of tasks, tau-bench at 25%, SWE-bench Verified only at 90%, and SWE-bench Lite still fails to meet all targets even at 95% under the main coverage rule.
AI.conclusion: For reliable partial evaluations, one must specify (i) the performance margin required between agents, (ii) how tasks are sampled, (iii) coverage rules over task groups, (iv) the decision rule for declaring a winner, and (v) the allowed fraction of unresolved comparisons. Different benchmarks have very different sample-efficiency profiles, so partial runs cannot be treated uniformly and must be carefully designed and reported.

title: Token Reduction Is Not Cost Reduction
summary:
Context-reduction layers for API-based coding agents, including command-output compressors, retrieval rankers, and payload-optimizing proxies, are usually evaluated by how much text they remove. We ask instead: when does reducing retrieved context or tool output lower the actual billed cost of a coding agent without reducing task success or lengthening its trajectory?
  Our primary evidence is a pre-specified, hash-frozen, paired campaign of 2,908 provider-billed Claude Code runs, of which 2,848 were analyzed, covering 103 tasks, seven repositories, and three models. The campaign compared a baseline with two generations of hook-based compression and an API-boundary proxy, within a broader measured program of roughly 5,500 billed executions.
  Three findings emerge. First, prompt-cache traffic dominated cost composition. Cache creation and reads accounted for about 87% of reconstructed four-component cost, or about 80% of the actual bill, with an 8.7% dollar-weighted residual that retained telemetry could not attribute. On Haiku 4.5, this residual scaled with thinking effort.
  Second, tool-output reduction did not reliably predict billed-cost reduction. An arm that removed 38% of estimated raw tool-output tokens had 6.8% higher paired cost (95% CI: +2.8% to +11.3%), while per-task reduction was only weakly associated with cost change (Pearson r = 0.15, CI crossing zero).
  Third, compression can harm task completion by removing action-critical evidence. In a small single-shot study on SWE-bench-derived Go tasks, compression reduced patch application from 27/40 to 15/40 by corrupting verbatim edit anchors, and the compressed grounded arm produced fewer solves at higher observed cost per solve.
  We propose a layered evidence standard centered on success-adjusted billed cost rather than token reduction alone.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically studies when compressing context or tool output in API-based coding agents actually reduces billed cost without hurting performance, finding that token reduction alone is a poor proxy and that caching and task success dominate effective cost.
AI.motivation: Existing work on context-reduction layers (compressors, rankers, proxies) typically optimizes for or reports how many tokens they remove, assuming fewer tokens means lower cost and similar performance. For coding agents that use tools and retrieval, the true billed cost involves caches, tool calls, and thinking steps, and aggressive compression can harm task completion. The authors want to reframe evaluation from raw token reduction to a more realistic, success-adjusted notion of cost, grounded in real provider bills.
AI.method: They run a pre-registered, hash-frozen paired experimental campaign using Claude Code across multiple repositories and models. They compare a baseline system to variants with hook-based compression and an API-boundary proxy, measuring actual provider-billed costs, cache usage, and performance across 2,908 executions (2,848 analyzed) spanning 103 tasks, 7 repos, and 3 models. They decompose cost into cache-related components, analyze correlations between tool-output token reduction and billed cost, and run a focused single-shot study on SWE-bench-derived Go tasks to see how compression affects patch application and success rates.
AI.result: 1) Prompt-cache operations dominate monetary cost: about 87% of reconstructed cost (≈80% of the bill) comes from cache creation and reads, with a residual tied to thinking effort on Haiku 4.5. 2) Reducing tool-output tokens does not reliably reduce billed cost: an arm that cut estimated raw tool-output tokens by 38% actually increased cost by 6.8% on average, and per-task token savings were only weakly correlated with cost changes (r ≈ 0.15). 3) Compression can degrade task success: in SWE-bench-style Go tasks, compression broke verbatim anchors needed for patch application, dropping successful patch applications from 27/40 to 15/40 and yielding fewer solves at a higher cost per solve.
AI.conclusion: Token removal, especially from tool outputs, is an unreliable proxy for economic efficiency and can harm completion rates in coding agents. Because caching and model thinking behavior drive most of the bill, and because aggressive compression can delete critical evidence, evaluation of context-reduction layers should use a layered evidence standard centered on success-adjusted billed cost, not just token counts. Designers should verify that compression improves or at least preserves both success and actual billed cost under realistic usage patterns.

title: PM-Bench: Evaluating Prospective Memory in LLM Agents
comment: Published as a conference paper at COLM 2026
summary: A significant challenge in agentic AI is prospective memory: the ability to execute an intention at a specific future cue or state while other activities are ongoing. We introduce PM-Bench, a text-based benchmark for measuring prospective memory capabilities in modern LLM agents. Inspired by the Virtual Week paradigm from cognitive science, PM-Bench evaluates how well LLM agents maintain user intentions, execute delayed intentions, and monitor latent environment changes. Over the course of a simulated seven-day week, agents must continue an ongoing activity while deciding whether any deferred task is due. We compare eight state-of-the-art LLMs on PM-Bench under eight different agent configurations. PM-Bench proves challenging across all settings: the best method, a GPT-5.4 agent, reaches only 65.1\% F1 score under our evaluation. Furthermore, no single strategy for improving prospective memory dominates across models. We release PM-Bench as a controlled testbed for diagnosing these failures and developing training or inference-time interventions that support reliable prospective behavior.

AI.tldr: They propose PM-Bench, a text-based benchmark to test whether LLM agents can remember and execute delayed intentions while doing other tasks, showing current models perform poorly and offering the benchmark as a tool to study and improve this ability.
AI.motivation: Agentic AI systems often need to remember to carry out future tasks when certain conditions are met, while simultaneously handling other activities. This human-like ability, called prospective memory, is underexplored and poorly measured in LLM agents. Existing benchmarks mainly test immediate reasoning or memory, not long-horizon, delayed intentions amidst distractions. The authors want a controlled way to quantify and compare how well different LLM agents can maintain and execute such future intentions.
AI.method: They build PM-Bench, a text-based benchmark inspired by the Virtual Week task from cognitive science. In a simulated seven-day schedule, an LLM agent performs an ongoing activity while having to track multiple deferred tasks and detect when they become due, including monitoring latent changes in the environment. The benchmark measures three key components: maintaining user intentions, executing delayed intentions at the right cue or time, and tracking relevant context changes. They then evaluate eight state-of-the-art LLMs, each tested under eight different agent configurations/strategies, and compute performance metrics such as F1 score.
AI.result: Across all models and configurations, PM-Bench is difficult: the best-performing setup—an agent built on GPT-5.4—achieves only a 65.1% F1 score, indicating frequent failures either to remember or to correctly time future actions. Different architectural or prompting strategies for improving prospective memory help in some settings but not others, and no single approach consistently improves performance across all models.
AI.conclusion: Prospective memory remains a major weakness for current LLM agents, even for strong models and with various agentic configurations. PM-Bench offers a standardized, cognitively inspired environment to expose and analyze these weaknesses. The authors position PM-Bench as a diagnostic and development tool for designing training schemes and inference-time interventions that could lead to more reliable, future-oriented behavior in agentic AI systems.

title: We Hebben Een Serieus Translatie: Modeling Intercomprehension as Probabilistic Inference
summary: Intercomprehension refers to partial intelligibility of an unfamiliar language (L2) by a speaker of a related language (L1). How is this zero-shot cross-language comprehension possible? In this work, we extend past work on algorithmic models of noisy-channel inference to model intercomprehension in a Bayesian framework. The model uses an LM in L1 only for scoring latent hypotheses about the translations of observed L2 utterances, and a general-purpose noise model to infer a mapping between L2 and L1 words based on either form-based similarity or symbolic rules. We then conduct a human behavioral experiment, eliciting inferences for utterances in Dutch, Italian, and Ukrainian from speakers of English, Spanish, and Russian, respectively. Our full model shows a closer alignment to the distribution of human intercomprehension performance than ablations, and also compares favorably to zero-shot prompting of much larger models. These results provide a cognitively plausible computational model of intercomprehension, and highlight the flexible inferences made by comprehenders under wide uncertainty in real-world cross-language scenarios. We share our code publicly.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a Bayesian noisy-channel model that explains how people can partially understand related foreign languages (intercomprehension) using only their native-language knowledge plus a general noise mapping between languages.
AI.motivation: To explain how speakers can often partially understand an unfamiliar but related language (zero-shot cross-language comprehension) using only knowledge of their native language, and to build a computational model that is both cognitively plausible and empirically aligned with human behavior in such scenarios.
AI.method: They extend noisy-channel inference to a Bayesian framework for intercomprehension. The model uses a language model in the listener’s L1 to score latent hypotheses about the L1 translations of L2 utterances. A general-purpose noise model infers a mapping from L2 words to L1 words based either on surface-form similarity or symbolic rules (e.g., regular correspondences). They then run a behavioral experiment: English, Spanish, and Russian speakers interpret utterances in Dutch, Italian, and Ukrainian, respectively. They compare the full model with ablated versions and with zero-shot prompting of large language models.
AI.result: The full Bayesian noisy-channel model better matches the distribution of human intercomprehension behavior than its ablations and performs favorably relative to zero-shot prompted large models, despite using only an L1 language model and a general noise model instead of explicit L2 training.
AI.conclusion: Intercomprehension can be modeled as Bayesian noisy-channel inference that leverages an L1 language model plus an inferred noisy mapping from L2 to L1. This provides a cognitively plausible account of how people understand related unfamiliar languages and underscores the flexibility of human inference under high uncertainty in cross-language communication. The authors also release their code for further research.

title: Critic Experience Bank: Self-Evolving Step-Level Confidence Estimation for LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents act in external environments where each action changes the state that later decisions condition on, and where a single wrong step can waste interaction budget or trigger irreversible side effects long before the final failure is observed. Reliable deployment therefore requires \emph{step-level confidence estimation}: a calibrated probability that each proposed action is productive, available \emph{before} the action is executed. Existing LLM confidence estimators are designed to score a response from the given prompt, but agent confidence also depends on execution consequences: whether similar actions in similar situations actually advanced the task after the environment responded. We introduce the \method (\methodshort), a self-evolving critic framework in which an LLM critic accumulates evidence from its own past judgments and their observed consequences. After each trajectory, a hindsight LLM that sees the full execution feedback votes on whether each step was productive. The resulting pseudo-labels populate a memory bank from which related productive and unproductive experiences are retrieved into the critic's prompt whenever a similar step recurs. \methodshort requires no training and uses no ground truth step labels. Across three agent benchmarks and three critic backbones, \methodshort attains the best calibration (ECE and Brier) and ranking (AUC) in every dataset--critic combination, reducing ECE by up to $54\%$ relative to the strongest training-free baseline.

AI.tldr: They propose a training-free, self-improving confidence estimator for LLM agents that predicts, before acting, how likely each step is to be productive, using hindsight feedback from past trajectories.
AI.motivation: LLM agents operate sequentially in environments where each action changes future context and a single mistake can be costly or irreversible. Current confidence estimators score final responses to static prompts and ignore whether similar past actions actually helped after environment feedback. There is a need for step-level, well-calibrated confidence over actions that accounts for execution consequences without relying on ground-truth step annotations or extra training.
AI.method: They introduce a self-evolving critic framework (\method/\methodshort). After an agent completes a trajectory, a hindsight LLM that sees the full execution and environment feedback labels each step as productive or unproductive. These pseudo-labels are stored in a memory bank. When the agent faces a new step, the critic LLM retrieves similar past steps and their labels into its prompt and outputs a calibrated probability that the proposed action is productive. The system is training-free, uses no ground-truth step labels, and improves over time as more labeled trajectories accumulate.
AI.result: On three agent benchmarks and using three different critic backbones, \methodshort consistently achieves the best calibration (lower Expected Calibration Error and Brier score) and ranking quality (higher AUC) among training-free baselines. It reduces ECE by up to 54% compared with the strongest alternative, indicating more reliable step-level confidence estimates across all tested dataset–critic combinations.
AI.conclusion: A memory-augmented, hindsight-labeled, training-free critic can provide well-calibrated, step-level confidence estimates for LLM agents by leveraging their own past experience. This approach outperforms existing training-free methods across multiple tasks and models, suggesting a practical path toward safer and more reliable deployment of LLM agents in sequential decision-making environments.

title: Entropy in Semantic Memory Navigation in Blind and Sighted Individuals: The Effect of Visual Experience
summary: Embodied accounts of semantic memory highlight the role of sensorimotor systems in acquiring and storing knowledge. Congenitally blind populations offer a critical test bed for these assumptions, providing an opportunity to assess whether conceptual grounding requires visual experience. In this study, we assessed semantic memory navigation differences between blind and sighted individuals using a property listing task with concrete and abstract concepts. We computed semantic entropy, an embedding-based natural language processing metric that captures the predictability of retrieval. Generalized linear mixed models revealed distinct navigation patterns across groups: while sighted individuals showed higher entropy for abstract than concrete concepts, blind participants did not. Instead, blind individuals exhibited higher entropy for visually salient concrete concepts (e.g., penguin). These results underscore the role of visual experience in the organization and dynamic navigation of semantic memory.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how congenital blindness affects the organization and navigation of semantic memory, using semantic entropy from a property listing task to compare blind and sighted individuals’ conceptual representations of concrete and abstract words.
AI.motivation: Embodied theories of semantic memory propose that conceptual knowledge is grounded in sensorimotor systems, especially vision. Congenitally blind individuals provide a strong test of whether visual experience is necessary for building concepts and for dynamically navigating semantic space. The study aims to clarify how the lack of visual input shapes semantic organization, particularly for concrete vs. abstract concepts and for visually salient items.
AI.method: Participants who were congenitally blind and sighted controls performed a property listing task for both concrete and abstract concepts. Their verbal responses were analyzed with an embedding-based NLP approach to compute semantic entropy, quantifying the predictability/diversity of retrieved properties for each concept. Generalized linear mixed models were used to compare entropy patterns across groups and concept types (including visually salient concrete items).
AI.result: Sighted participants showed higher semantic entropy (more diverse, less predictable properties) for abstract concepts than for concrete ones, consistent with the idea that abstract concepts are navigated more variably. In contrast, blind participants did not show this abstract–concrete entropy difference; instead, they showed higher entropy specifically for visually salient concrete concepts (e.g., penguin). This indicates that concepts strongly tied to vision in the sighted population are represented and accessed more variably by blind individuals.
AI.conclusion: The findings support a role of visual experience in shaping both the structure and dynamic navigation of semantic memory. While conceptual knowledge can clearly develop without vision, the absence of visual experience alters how concepts—especially visually salient concrete ones—are organized and accessed, posing a challenge to fully amodal accounts and refining embodied theories of semantic representation.

title: Isolation as a First-Class Principle for LLM-Agent System Safety: Concepts, Taxonomy, Challenges and Future Directions
summary: The capability of LLM agents to function as the ``brain'' of a system fundamentally expands the scope of analysis beyond a standalone model. Consequently, safety is no longer only about input--output content alignment. It also concerns system behavior and real-world execution outcomes. However, the current literature is fragmented across attack types, applications, and benchmarks. This makes it hard to explain why failures such as prompt injection, tool misuse, and memory poisoning often share the same structural cause, and how they spread through an agent workflow. In this survey, we treat isolation as a first-class principle for LLM-agent system safety. By isolation, we refer to the separation of user inputs, tool access, execution channels, inter-agent communication, and environment-originated context. We organize the literature with a boundary-centric taxonomy of five boundaries: user-agent, agent-tool, agent-execution, agent-agent, and system-environment. This view helps identify where the loss of isolation first occurs, how compromise propagates across boundaries, and which defenses are most relevant at each interface. We also summarize cross-boundary failure paths, discuss open challenges, and outline a research agenda for isolation-by-construction in future agent systems.

AI.tldr: Survey of LLM-agent safety centered on isolation as the key organizing principle, using a boundary-based taxonomy to explain failures and defenses.
AI.motivation: Existing safety work on LLM agents is fragmented by attack type and application, obscuring the shared structural causes behind failures like prompt injection, tool misuse, and memory poisoning, and how these failures propagate through complex agent workflows. A clearer, unifying framework is needed to reason about system-level safety and real-world execution risks.
AI.method: Conceptual and survey-based: the authors review and reorganize the literature on LLM-agent safety around the principle of isolation. They define and analyze five key system boundaries—user-agent, agent-tool, agent-execution, agent-agent, and system-environment—and map known attack types, vulnerabilities, and defenses onto these boundaries. They also trace how compromises cross boundaries and synthesize cross-boundary failure paths.
AI.result: A boundary-centric taxonomy for LLM-agent safety is proposed, centered on isolation between distinct interfaces (user inputs, tools, execution channels, agents, and environment context). The survey shows that diverse failures such as prompt injection, tool misuse, and memory poisoning can be understood as violations of specific boundaries, and it connects each type of boundary failure to relevant defensive strategies. Additionally, it identifies typical patterns by which an initial loss of isolation escalates into broader system compromise.
AI.conclusion: Viewing LLM-agent systems through the lens of isolation and explicit boundaries provides a unifying explanation for many known failure modes and clarifies where to apply defenses. The authors advocate treating isolation as a foundational design principle, encourage work on isolation-by-construction architectures for agents, and highlight open challenges and research directions for building safer, more robust LLM-agent systems.

title: Comparing Semantic Navigation in Humans and Large Language Models using Natural Language Processing
summary: Semantic memory retrieval can be conceptualized as navigation through conceptual space. We compared semantic search dynamics between humans and three large language models (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Claude-Sonnet-4.5) using verbal fluency data. By applying trajectory-based NLP metrics to the items generated by 82 human participants and LLM output across eight temperature settings, we quantified three complementary dimensions: entropy (step size predictability), distance to next (successive semantic steps), and distance to centroid (global dispersion). Humans exhibited higher entropy, larger semantic steps and broader dispersion than all LLMs, indicating more variable and exploratory search. Temperature tuning produced only partial alignments, as individual metrics matched between humans and LLMs at specific settings, but no configuration reproduced the complete human profile (in all dimensions). These findings suggest that human semantic search implements a distinctive balance between local exploitation and global exploration that current model architectures fail to reproduce.

AI.tldr: The paper compares how humans and large language models search through semantic memory during a verbal fluency task, showing that humans explore concepts more variably and broadly than current LLMs, even after temperature tuning.
AI.motivation: To understand whether large language models retrieve and organize semantic knowledge in ways similar to humans, particularly in terms of how they search through conceptual space during verbal fluency tasks.
AI.method: Collected verbal fluency data from 82 human participants and generated comparable category lists from three LLMs (GPT-4o, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Claude-Sonnet-4.5) across eight temperature settings. Applied trajectory-based NLP metrics to each produced sequence, quantifying three dimensions of semantic search: entropy (predictability of step size), distance to next item (local semantic jumps), and distance to centroid (overall dispersion in semantic space). Compared distributions of these metrics between humans and models and examined how temperature affected alignment with human patterns.
