AI.result: Humans showed consistently higher entropy, larger average semantic step sizes, and greater global dispersion in conceptual space than all tested LLMs. Adjusting model temperature changed model behavior and sometimes matched humans on individual metrics at specific temperatures, but no temperature setting yielded a model profile that simultaneously matched humans on all three dimensions. LLMs remained more locally constrained and less exploratory overall.
AI.conclusion: Human semantic search during verbal fluency tasks reflects a distinct balance between local exploitation and global exploration that current LLM architectures, even when temperature-tuned, do not capture. This indicates that present-day LLMs implement qualitatively different semantic navigation dynamics than humans, suggesting the need for new mechanisms or architectures if we wish to more faithfully approximate human-like semantic memory search.

title: Accepted Prefixes Are Not All You Need: A Negative Result on PEFT-Based Block-Diffusion Drafting
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive language model inference by using a cheap drafter to propose multiple future tokens and a target model to verify them. A common design goal is therefore to improve draft quality while reducing auxiliary parameters and systems overhead. We study a negative result for this direction through PEFT-BD, a same-backbone speculative decoding method in which a LoRA-like adapter acts as a block-diffusion drafter for an autoregressive verifier. PEFT-BD is motivated by several attractive properties: it avoids tokenizer mismatch, avoids loading a separate draft model, adds only a small number of trainable parameters, and uses a BD3LM-style denoising objective to propose a block of tokens in parallel. Despite these advantages, PEFT-BD does not yield a practical speedup in our Qwen3-0.6B experiments. Although the method obtains nontrivial accepted prefixes, profiling shows that each speculative step requires an adapter-enabled full-backbone draft pass followed by an adapter-disabled full-backbone verification pass. Thus, the drafter is parameter-efficient but not compute-efficient. Our results isolate a simple but important condition for successful speculative decoding: the drafter must be substantially cheaper to execute than the verifier. Longer accepted prefixes alone cannot compensate when draft computation remains verifier-scale.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates a speculative decoding method (PEFT-BD) that reuses the same backbone with lightweight adapters as the drafter and finds that, despite good draft quality, it gives no practical speedup because the draft computation is as expensive as verification.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding aims to speed up autoregressive language models by using a cheaper model to propose multiple tokens and a larger model to verify them. Designers want higher draft quality with minimal extra parameters and system complexity. The authors investigate whether a parameter-efficient, same-backbone drafter using lightweight adapters can achieve these goals without needing a separate draft model, thus simplifying deployment and reducing memory overhead.
AI.method: They propose PEFT-BD, where a LoRA-style adapter is attached to the same backbone as the main autoregressive model and trained with a BD3LM-style denoising objective to generate blocks of tokens in parallel. During inference, decoding alternates between an adapter-enabled draft pass that proposes multi-token blocks and an adapter-disabled pass where the base model verifies these proposals. They implement and evaluate this on Qwen3-0.6B, measuring accepted-prefix lengths, speed, and profiling compute usage per speculative step.
AI.result: PEFT-BD achieves nontrivial accepted prefix lengths, meaning the drafter proposes blocks that the verifier often accepts. However, timing and profiling show no practical speedup in inference. Each speculative step still requires two full passes through the backbone: one with the adapter for drafting and one without for verification. Consequently, even though the drafter has few extra parameters, its computational cost remains comparable to the verifier, negating performance gains.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that parameter-efficient, same-backbone speculative decoding like PEFT-BD is not compute-efficient and therefore fails to deliver practical speedups. It identifies a key requirement for successful speculative decoding: the drafter must be significantly cheaper to execute than the verifier, not just parameter-light or high quality. Long accepted prefixes cannot overcome the overhead when the draft computation operates at essentially the same compute scale as the verifier.

title: Fine-Tuned Multi-Agent Framework for Detecting OCEAN in Life Narratives
summary: Accurately assessing personality from text is challenging because traits are latent, context-dependent, and often subtly expressed across long narratives. Large language models (LLMs) offer new opportunities by processing extensive textual contexts, but pretraining of these models can induce latent "personality-like" biases, making single-model inferences inconsistent. We propose a fine-tuned multi-agent framework for detecting OCEAN personality traits, in which sub-agents are conditioned to adopt high, low, or neutral perspectives for each trait through masked language modeling (MLM) and psychometric supervision. A judge LLM aggregates and compares sub-agent outputs to generate final trait predictions, capturing multiple complementary perspectives while mitigating individual model biases. We evaluate the framework on life narrative dataset through quantitative and qualitative experiments, including baselines, ablations, and inference quality analyses. Our approach offers a scalable and interpretable method for text-based personality inference, highlighting the benefits of multi-agent reasoning grounded in psychometric supervision.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a multi-agent, fine-tuned LLM framework to infer OCEAN personality traits from long texts by comparing outputs from agents conditioned to different personality levels, improving robustness and interpretability over single-model approaches.
AI.motivation: Personality traits are latent, context-dependent, and expressed subtly across long narratives, making them hard to infer reliably from text. While LLMs can process long contexts, their pretraining can embed implicit personality-like biases, leading to unstable and inconsistent personality assessments when using a single model. The authors aim to develop a more reliable, scalable, and interpretable method for text-based personality inference that reduces these biases and aligns more closely with psychometric theory.
AI.method: The authors fine-tune a set of sub-agent LLMs for each OCEAN trait, conditioning them to adopt high, low, or neutral perspectives using a combination of masked language modeling (MLM) and supervision grounded in psychometric signals. Each sub-agent reads the same life-narrative text but interprets it through its assigned trait perspective. A separate judge LLM then aggregates, compares, and reasons over the outputs of these sub-agents to produce final personality trait predictions. The framework is evaluated via quantitative metrics, ablation studies, baselines, and qualitative analyses of inference quality on a life narrative dataset.
AI.result: The multi-agent framework produces more consistent and interpretable personality trait inferences than single-model baselines. Experiments on a life narrative dataset show that conditioning sub-agents on high/low/neutral perspectives and using a judge LLM improves prediction quality, robustness to model biases, and explanatory richness. Ablation studies indicate that both the psychometric supervision and the multi-perspective aggregation contribute substantially to performance gains.
AI.conclusion: The proposed fine-tuned multi-agent LLM framework offers a scalable and interpretable solution for inferring OCEAN personality traits from long texts. By explicitly modeling multiple personality perspectives and aggregating them via a judge LLM, the approach mitigates personality-like biases from pretraining and enhances inference reliability. The work underscores the value of multi-agent reasoning and psychometric grounding for sensitive, latent psychological attribute prediction from text.

title: EVOQUANT: Self-Evolving Verifier-Guided Strategy Optimization for Robust Quantitative Trading
summary: Quantitative strategy optimization remains largely manual, requiring domain experts to identify weak signals, tune risk-control rules, and repeatedly validate iterative revisions. Large language models can accelerate this process, but directly relying on them to rewrite trading strategies often introduces hallucinated edits, strategy drift, and backtest overfitting. We propose EVOQUANT, a self-Evolving Verifier-guided framework for strategy Optimization in Quantitative trading. Our method utilizes LLMs to deeply diagnose performance bottlenecks, generates semantically controlled candidate edits, selects the best strategy through a multi-stage verification pipeline, and distills optimization experience into reusable knowledge for continual self-improvement. We evaluate our method using seven representative strategies: four from the A-share market and three from the Crypto market. Experimental results show that our method significantly improves the Sharpe ratio across all tested strategies: the average test Sharpe increases from -0.298 to 0.538, and the best-performing strategy achieves a 199% relative improvement. Ablation studies and stress tests under stricter conditions further validate the effectiveness and robustness of the framework. Overall, this work transforms quantitative strategy optimization from costly manual trial and error into an automated and verifiable iterative paradigm, offering a new path for applying large language models to financial strategy research.

AI.tldr: EVOQUANT is a framework that uses large language models with a verifier-guided, multi-stage pipeline to automatically optimize quantitative trading strategies, significantly improving Sharpe ratios while avoiding common LLM pitfalls like hallucinations and overfitting.
AI.motivation: Quantitative trading strategy optimization is still a largely manual, expert-driven process. Human quants must locate weak signals, tune risk controls, and iteratively test modifications, which is time-consuming and expensive. While large language models could speed up this workflow, naively letting them rewrite strategies leads to hallucinated changes, strategy drift from the original design, and overfitting to backtests. There is a need for an automated, reliable, and verifiable way to leverage LLMs for strategy optimization without these drawbacks.
AI.method: The authors propose EVOQUANT, a self-evolving, verifier-guided optimization framework. It uses LLMs to: (1) diagnose performance bottlenecks of existing strategies, (2) generate candidate edits under semantic constraints to control how much and in what way the strategy is modified, and (3) pass these candidates through a multi-stage verification pipeline that selects the best-performing, most robust strategies. The framework also distills successful optimization patterns into reusable knowledge, enabling the system to improve its future optimization behavior over time.
AI.result: On seven representative strategies (four from the A-share market and three from the crypto market), EVOQUANT substantially improves performance. The average test Sharpe ratio rises from -0.298 to 0.538, and the best strategy attains a 199% relative Sharpe improvement. Additional ablation experiments and stricter-condition stress tests support that each component contributes to performance and that the framework is robust under tougher evaluation settings.
AI.conclusion: EVOQUANT turns quantitative strategy optimization from a labor-intensive, manual trial-and-error process into an automated, iterative, and verifiable paradigm. It demonstrates that carefully constrained and verifier-guided use of LLMs can meaningfully advance financial strategy research by improving returns while mitigating hallucinations, strategy drift, and overfitting, and it establishes a reusable knowledge base for continual self-improvement.

title: RCWT: Measuring Task-Budget Displacement from Coordination Content in LLM Calls
summary: Multi-agent and memory-augmented LLM systems often place coordination content, shared state, prior discussion, tool outputs, summaries, and role instructions, inside the same finite prompt used for the current task. This creates a practical allocation problem: every token spent on coordination is unavailable to task instructions or evidence when a call is assembled under a fixed context budget. We introduce the Roundtable Context Window Test (RCWT), a controlled protocol for measuring this task-budget displacement effect. RCWT varies coordination content while controlling total budget, position order, task family, and scoring. In the main context-dependent recall task at $W=4096$, three commercial models remain near baseline through moderate overhead and then degrade sharply once residual reference evidence falls to a few hundred tokens. Window-scaling summaries are consistent with a task-specific residual-budget interpretation rather than a fixed percentage threshold, but we treat this as descriptive evidence rather than a universal law. To test whether the fixed-budget cliff persists when task evidence remains intact, we add an intact-task ablation: the full task/reference block is kept present while coordination tokens increase by expanding total prompt length. In that setting, all tested calls return every scored field correctly across GPT-4.1-mini, Claude Haiku 4.5, and Gemini 2.5 Flash up to a 95\% coordination ratio. This ablation narrows the claim: the main RCWT cliff is best read as task-budget displacement, not as proof that coordination volume alone causes semantic interference in the original open-ended task. RCWT is therefore a measurement primitive for context-allocation budgeting, not a complete theory of multi-agent benefit or session-level coordination.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how putting coordination content (like multi-agent instructions and shared state) into the same limited context window as the actual task hurts performance, and introduces a controlled benchmark (RCWT) to quantify how much task-relevant budget is needed before performance collapses.
AI.motivation: In multi-agent and memory-augmented LLM setups, coordination text, summaries, and tool outputs often share a finite context window with the actual task instructions and evidence. Because every token spent on coordination reduces the remaining budget for task-relevant content, practitioners lack a principled way to understand or budget this trade-off. The authors want a controlled, model-agnostic way to measure when increasing coordination overhead begins to significantly displace task content and degrade performance.
AI.method: They design the Roundtable Context Window Test (RCWT), a protocol that systematically varies the amount of coordination content in the prompt while holding total context budget, content ordering, task family, and scoring rules fixed. In the main setup, they use a context-dependent recall task with a 4096-token budget, gradually increasing coordination tokens so that fewer tokens remain for task evidence. They then analyze model performance as a function of residual task tokens. To disentangle displacement from raw volume effects, they run an ablation where the full task evidence is kept intact and total prompt length is expanded to accommodate additional coordination tokens, testing several commercial LLMs under very high coordination-to-task ratios.
AI.result: In the fixed-budget RCWT, three commercial models perform close to baseline under modest coordination overhead but show a sharp performance drop once the remaining task evidence shrinks to only a few hundred tokens. Summary analyses suggest that the performance cliff depends more on the absolute residual task budget than on a fixed percentage of the context window, although this is not claimed as a universal rule. In the intact-task ablation, where task evidence is not displaced, GPT-4.1-mini, Claude Haiku 4.5, and Gemini 2.5 Flash answer all scored fields correctly even when coordination tokens make up as much as 95% of the total prompt.
AI.conclusion: The key degradation in the main RCWT comes from task-budget displacement—too few tokens left for task evidence—rather than from coordination volume itself causing semantic interference. RCWT should thus be viewed as a measurement tool for studying and budgeting context allocation in multi-component prompts, not as a general theory about when or how multi-agent coordination helps. It provides a way to empirically determine how much task-relevant budget must be preserved to avoid the performance cliff in context-limited LLM applications.

title: Do We Really Need Transformers for Global Spatial Information Extraction in Traffic Forecasting?
comment: 15 pages, 6 figures. Source code available at https://github.com/uuesti/U-Trans
summary: Existing traffic forecasting models commonly focus on extracting spatial dependencies, particularly global spatial information, which characterizes the representations obtained through interactions between each individual node and all nodes across the traffic network. However, the underlying mechanism by which such global information is modeled and extracted remains insufficiently investigated. Whether global information must be extracted by high-degree-of-freedom adaptive attention or can be captured by a simple global aggregation operator remains unclear. For this purpose, we design a controlled ablation framework that replaces only the spatial mixing module to test attention-based global interaction. Across six traffic benchmarks, uniform full-range mixing and standard spatial attention each achieve lower MAE on three datasets, with only a 0.14% difference in mean MAE, while the former reduces node-scale spatial mixing complexity from O(N2) to O(N). Mechanism analysis further decomposes spatial attention into a row-uniform global background and a non-uniform residual. The residual shows dataset-dependent marginal value, suggesting that spatial attention should be justified by stable gains beyond a row-uniform global background. The corresponding source code is publicly available at: https://github.com/uuesti/U-Trans

AI.tldr: The paper questions whether complex attention-based spatial mixing is truly necessary for traffic forecasting and shows that simple global aggregation can match its performance at much lower complexity.
AI.motivation: Many recent traffic forecasting models use attention or other complex mechanisms to capture global spatial dependencies across the entire traffic network, but it is unclear whether such high-degree-of-freedom mechanisms are actually required or whether simpler operators could suffice. The authors aim to rigorously probe the real contribution and mechanism of attention-based global spatial interaction.
AI.method: They design a controlled ablation framework for traffic forecasting models where only the spatial mixing module is changed while keeping all other components fixed. They compare attention-based global interaction with a uniform full-range (row-uniform) global mixing operator across six standard traffic benchmarks. They also analytically decompose spatial attention into a row-uniform global background term and a non-uniform residual, then study the empirical value of this residual across datasets.
AI.result: On six traffic forecasting benchmarks, the simple uniform full-range mixing and the standard spatial attention each achieve lower MAE on three datasets, with an average MAE difference of only 0.14%, while the uniform mixing reduces spatial mixing complexity from O(N^2) for attention to O(N) in terms of the number of nodes. Mechanism analysis shows that most of the effect of spatial attention can be attributed to a row-uniform global background, with the non-uniform residual providing only small, dataset-dependent benefits.
AI.conclusion: Complex attention-based spatial mixing does not consistently outperform a much simpler global aggregation operator in traffic forecasting, and its marginal gains, when present, are highly dataset-dependent. Therefore, the use of spatial attention in this context should be justified by clear, stable improvements beyond what can be achieved with a row-uniform global background, especially given its higher computational complexity.

title: Fin-Analyst at FinMMEval 2026 Task 3: A Live Hybrid Trading Agent with LLM Specialists and Rule-Based Signals
comment: 14 pages, 7 tables, 1 figure. CLEF 2026 FinMMEval Task 3 Working Notes
summary: Large language model (LLM) trading agents show promising performance in equity markets, yet remain narrowly focused on US equities with little evidence from live deployment. We present Fin-Analyst, a hybrid agent for FinMMEval 2026 Task 3: an eight-specialist LLM pipeline over news, SEC filings, fundamentals, analyst forecasts, technical indicators, and social sentiment, aggregated by a Meta-Agent for Tesla (TSLA), and a lightweight rule based three-signal vote for Bitcoin (BTC). On the final official leaderboard (accessed 2026-07-05), Fin-Analyst ranks first of all agents on TSLA with a +13.51% return, +28.33 points over Buy-and-Hold (Sharpe 4.10, 88% win rate), while the BTC vote ends flat yet well above a sharply falling baseline. Relative to the interim performance, the asset ranking reversed, indicating that short live windows yield volatility-sensitive rankings. Ablation identifies event-driven 8-K disclosures as the most influential TSLA signal. Error analysis shows that the memoryless agents repeat wrong calls for days at a time, and that the fixed-threshold BTC rules lost money by trading on noise in a sideways market while the LLM pipeline gained under similar conditions, motivating a memory-aware, LLM-based successor for both assets.

AI.tldr: Fin-Analyst is a hybrid LLM-based trading system that ranked first on TSLA in FinMMEval 2026, highlighting the value of event-driven filings and exposing limitations of memoryless, rule-based agents, especially in volatile and sideways markets.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM trading agents are mostly limited to US equities, largely backtested rather than live, and rarely combine diverse data sources systematically. The authors aim to build and evaluate a more realistic, multi-signal LLM trading framework in a live competition setting for both an equity (TSLA) and a crypto asset (BTC), and to understand which signals and design choices actually drive performance and robustness.
AI.method: They design Fin-Analyst, a hybrid agent system. For TSLA, it is an eight-specialist LLM pipeline, where separate specialist agents analyze distinct information streams—news, SEC filings (including 8-Ks), company fundamentals, analyst forecasts, technical indicators, and social sentiment. Their outputs are then combined by a higher-level Meta-Agent that makes the final TSLA trading decision. For BTC, they use a simpler rule-based mechanism that aggregates three technical or sentiment signals into a voting scheme. They evaluate performance on the FinMMEval 2026 Task 3 live leaderboard, perform ablations to identify which information channels matter most, and conduct error analysis on trading patterns and failure modes.
AI.result: On the official FinMMEval 2026 leaderboard, Fin-Analyst ranks first among all TSLA agents, achieving a 13.51% absolute return and outperforming Buy-and-Hold by 28.33 points, with a Sharpe ratio of 4.10 and an 88% win rate. The BTC rule-based vote system finishes roughly flat but still meaningfully ahead of a strongly negative BTC baseline. Between interim and final evaluations, the relative performance ordering of TSLA and BTC components flips, indicating that short evaluation windows create volatility-driven ranking instability. Ablation experiments reveal that event-driven SEC 8-K disclosures are the most impactful TSLA signal. Error analysis finds that stateless (memoryless) decision-making leads to repeated mispredictions over several days, and that fixed trading thresholds on BTC caused overtrading on noise in a sideways market, while the LLM-based TSLA pipeline handled similar conditions better.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that a multi-specialist, LLM-centric trading architecture can perform strongly in a live setting, particularly on an individual equity like TSLA, and that event-driven regulatory filings are especially valuable among the information sources. However, both memoryless architectures and rigid rule-based trading rules show systematic weaknesses: they can persist in erroneous positions and overreact to noise in low-trend markets. These findings motivate next-generation, memory-aware, fully LLM-driven agents for both equities and crypto, with adaptive thresholds and richer state tracking to reduce repeated errors and improve robustness to market regimes.

title: Function-Aware Fill-in-the-Middle as Mid-Training for Coding Agent Foundation Models
summary: Coding agents must integrate external tool returns into ongoing reasoning - a capability that standard left-to-right pretraining on code exposes only in its forward direction. We observe that the action-observation-continuation loop of a coding agent is structurally isomorphic to a function call site, where a caller binds arguments, a callee returns a value computed elsewhere, and downstream code consumes that value. This conditioning structure exists at internet scale in ordinary code. We exploit it through function-aware fill-in-the-middle (FIM) mid-training: a self-supervised objective that masks functions selected via program dependency graph analysis and a complexity-inferability double criterion. We mid-train Qwen2.5-Coder-Instruct (7B/14B) and Qwen3-8B on a 2.6B-token decontaminated corpus drawn from 968 GitHub repositories, then apply existing agentic post-training pipelines. Mid-training improves SWE-Bench-Verified by +2.8/+3.0 at 7B/14B and by +3.2 on Qwen3-8B; SWE-Bench-Lite gains are +3.7/+4.0/+5.4 on the same models. The improvement holds across two post-training pipelines (R2E-Gym, SWE-Smith) and on a non-Qwen2.5 base (Qwen3-8B with SWE-Lego). Beyond in-domain gains, mid-training also mitigates the capability erosion that agentic post-training otherwise inflicts on non-agent coding (e.g., LiveCodeBench) and non-coding tool-use benchmarks (tau-bench, BFCL): although the mid-training corpus contains Python code only, the function-call inductive bias survives post-training and yields consistent gains.

AI.tldr: They propose a new function-aware fill-in-the-middle (FIM) mid-training objective for code models that teaches them to better integrate external tool outputs, leading to improved performance of coding agents and preserving general coding/tool-use abilities after agentic post-training.
AI.motivation: Coding agents must repeatedly incorporate external tool outputs (e.g., tests, compilers, search results) into their ongoing reasoning. Standard left-to-right pretraining on code mainly exposes models to such patterns only in the forward direction, giving weak inductive bias for the action–observation–continuation loop required in tool-using agents. The authors note that this interaction pattern is structurally similar to function calls in code, which are ubiquitous in open-source repositories. They aim to leverage this natural structure to improve agentic coding performance and avoid degradation of general coding and tool-use ability caused by agentic post-training.
AI.method: They define a function-aware fill-in-the-middle (FIM) mid-training objective. Using program dependency graph analysis and a combined complexity–inferability criterion, they select functions in real code that are both nontrivial and predictable from surrounding context. These functions are then masked, and the model is trained to fill them in, mirroring the pattern: call site (arguments) → external computation (masked function body) → downstream code that consumes the returned value. They mid-train several base models—Qwen2.5-Coder-Instruct (7B/14B) and Qwen3-8B—on a 2.6B-token, decontaminated Python corpus from 968 GitHub repos, then apply standard agentic post-training pipelines like R2E-Gym, SWE-Smith, and SWE-Lego.
AI.result: Mid-training yields consistent gains on software-engineering agent benchmarks. On SWE-Bench-Verified, performance improves by +2.8/+3.0 points for Qwen2.5-Coder-Instruct 7B/14B and +3.2 points for Qwen3-8B; on SWE-Bench-Lite, gains are +3.7/+4.0/+5.4 respectively. These improvements hold across multiple post-training pipelines (R2E-Gym, SWE-Smith, SWE-Lego) and across model families. Beyond the primary task, mid-training also mitigates the loss of non-agentic coding performance and non-coding tool-use capabilities (e.g., LiveCodeBench, tau-bench, BFCL) that are otherwise degraded by agentic post-training, even though the mid-training data is exclusively Python.
AI.conclusion: Function-aware FIM mid-training successfully encodes a function-call-style inductive bias that aligns well with the action–observation–continuation loop of coding agents. This leads to stronger agentic coding performance and greater robustness of general coding and tool-use skills after agentic post-training. The results suggest that exploiting naturally occurring function-call structure at scale is an effective, domain-general way to teach models to integrate external tool returns into ongoing reasoning, with benefits that transfer beyond Python and beyond coding tasks.

title: FinResearchBench II: A Deep Research Benchmark with Consensus-Derived Gold Rubrics for Distinguishing Financial Report Quality
summary: Deep research agents are increasingly used to produce long-form financial reports, yet large-scale evaluation remains bottlenecked by the need for human experts to define and execute high-quality rubrics. We address this problem by proposing a scalable pipeline for generating high-quality rubrics without human experts in the final loop. We build a financial deep research benchmark from 104 real-world user queries and automatically synthesize 14,450 query-specific candidate rubrics from model-generated reports. To justify removing human experts from rubric execution, we compare rubric judgments from three human experts with those from a three-LLM judge panel on a sampled subset, and show that LLM-based evaluation is sufficiently consistent with human evaluation to replace it for large-scale rubric screening, including 98.67\% label-level agreement on jointly unanimous items. We then derive consensus-derived gold rubrics through two filters: a strict consistency filter, which keeps a rubric only if the three LLM judges unanimously agree on every report under the same query, and a distinguishability filter, which keeps a rubric only if it assigns at least one majority-yes and at least one majority-no label across the evaluated systems. This process retains 3,687 consistency-passed rubrics, of which 2,600 remain distinguishable and form the final set of consensus-derived gold rubrics. Using this final rubric set, we obtain clearly differentiated rankings across 10 deep research systems, with item-level pass rates ranging from 58.58\% to 22.23\%. More broadly, because the pipeline removes human-expert execution from rubric generation and evaluation, it is naturally scalable for benchmark evaluation, automatic system comparison, and future studies of evaluation-driven system improvement.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an automated, scalable pipeline to generate and validate high-quality evaluation rubrics for long-form financial research agents using LLM-based judges instead of human experts.
AI.motivation: Evaluating long-form financial research produced by deep research agents requires detailed, query-specific rubrics. Building and executing these rubrics at scale is expensive and bottlenecked by the need for domain experts. The authors aim to remove this bottleneck by automating rubric generation and execution while maintaining human-level reliability, thus enabling scalable benchmarking and comparison of financial research systems.
AI.method: The authors construct a benchmark from 104 real-world financial user queries. For each query, they use model-generated research reports to automatically synthesize 14,450 candidate, query-specific rubrics. They then evaluate these rubrics using both human experts and a panel of three LLM judges on a sampled subset to measure agreement and justify replacing humans with LLMs. Next, they apply a two-stage filtering process on the candidate rubrics using only LLM judgments: (1) a strict consistency filter that retains only rubrics where all three LLM judges unanimously agree on labels for every report under a given query, and (2) a distinguishability filter that retains only rubrics that yield at least one majority-yes and one majority-no label across evaluated systems. The rubrics passing both filters constitute the final consensus-derived gold rubric set, which is then used to evaluate and rank 10 deep research systems.
AI.result: The authors find that a three-LLM judge panel achieves high consistency with human expert judgments, including 98.67% label-level agreement on items where both humans and LLMs are unanimously aligned, supporting the replacement of humans with LLMs for large-scale rubric screening. Out of 14,450 initial candidate rubrics, 3,687 pass the strict consistency filter, and 2,600 of these also pass the distinguishability filter to become the final consensus-derived gold rubrics. Using this rubric set to assess 10 deep research systems yields clearly separated performance rankings, with item-level pass rates from 58.58% down to 22.23%, demonstrating that the benchmark is discriminative.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based panels can reliably execute rubric-based evaluations for long-form financial research, closely matching human experts and enabling removal of humans from the rubric execution loop. The proposed pipeline automatically generates, screens, and refines a large set of high-quality, query-specific gold rubrics that effectively distinguish system performance. This makes benchmark construction and large-scale system comparison far more scalable and supports future research on evaluation-driven improvement of financial deep research agents.

title: From Observation to Insight: Mechanistic World Models and the Quest for Autonomous Discovery
summary: Recent advances in foundation models have transformed AI for Science, enabling remarkably accurate predictive performance across domains ranging from protein folding to weather forecasting. Yet prediction alone does not constitute scientific discovery. Scientific understanding depends on uncovering the reusable explanatory mechanisms that generate observations, whereas contemporary machine learning remains fundamentally organised around predictive mappings rather than explanatory structure. In this paper, we argue that scientific discovery is fundamentally a problem of knowledge organisation. To this end, we introduce Mechanistic World Models, a new design paradigm that places reusable mechanisms at the centre of representation, computation and learning. Drawing on insights from the philosophy of science, we derive the computational capabilities required for discovery, identify the design principles and inductive pressures that encourage explanatory knowledge to emerge, and formalise the anatomy of a mechanism-centric world model. Finally, we show how diverse research directions including mechanistic interpretability, causal representation learning, equation discovery and modular architectures capture complementary ingredients of this paradigm while lacking a unified framework. We propose Mechanistic World Models as a conceptual foundation and computational blueprint for moving AI beyond predictive forecasting towards autonomous scientific discovery.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Mechanistic World Models as a unified paradigm to move AI from mere prediction to explanatory, mechanism-based scientific discovery.
AI.motivation: Foundation models achieve impressive predictive performance in scientific domains (e.g., protein folding, weather), but they largely learn black-box predictive mappings rather than explicit, reusable explanatory mechanisms. Scientific discovery, however, requires structured, interpretable knowledge about the mechanisms that generate observations. There is currently no unified framework that organises existing efforts (mechanistic interpretability, causal representation learning, equation discovery, modular architectures) around such mechanisms.
AI.method: The authors conceptually analyse what scientific discovery requires from AI systems, drawing on philosophy of science to characterise explanatory mechanisms and their computational counterparts. They define the required capabilities of a mechanism-centric world model and derive design principles and inductive biases that favour the emergence of reusable mechanisms. They then formalise the "anatomy" of Mechanistic World Models as a blueprint and map existing research strands onto this framework to show how they capture partial aspects of it.
AI.result: The paper yields a formal and conceptual specification of Mechanistic World Models: how mechanisms should be represented, composed, and learned; what inductive pressures encourage mechanism discovery; and how these differ from standard predictive models. It organises disparate research directions—mechanistic interpretability, causal representation learning, equation discovery, modular architectures—under a single conceptual lens, clarifying their roles and gaps with respect to autonomous scientific discovery.
AI.conclusion: Scientific discovery should be framed as a problem of knowledge organisation around reusable explanatory mechanisms, not merely prediction. Mechanistic World Models provide a conceptual foundation and computational blueprint for AI systems that can uncover such mechanisms. By adopting mechanism-centric representations, computations, and learning objectives, future AI systems can progress from high-accuracy forecasting to genuine autonomous scientific discovery, integrating and extending existing work on interpretability, causality, equations, and modularity.

title: A Shared Subcircuit Lets LLMs Count Down Across Tasks
summary: Writing a sentence of exactly twelve words; ending a DNA sequence at the right codon; formatting an ASCII table. These are all tasks that language models can do that requires tracking how many tokens remain before a target. In this work, we identify in Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct a general mechanism for performing these tasks: a "countdown subcircuit" that compares the current position to a goal length and estimates the time remaining until then. We first isolate a countdown subcircuit in a controlled setting, in which the model is tasked with writing a fixed-length sentence ending in a specified word. We then investigate the geometry of the representations used by the subcircuit, and find that the subcircuit uses an identical motif previously identified in a frontier LLM on a separate task, thus suggesting that this motif is shared across models. Finally, we use unsupervised probing on a natural language dataset to find a variety of other tasks where this subcircuit is used, including tasks where the goal length is inferred from context rather than explicitly stated. Our work suggests that reverse-engineering subcircuits allows us to understand how behaviors generalize from a single example to many different tasks and even models.

AI.tldr: They find and analyze a shared “countdown” mechanism in Llama-3.1-70B that tracks remaining tokens until a target length, showing it generalizes across tasks and models.
AI.motivation: Many language-model behaviors require precise control over output length or position (e.g., fixed-length sentences, DNA codon alignment, table formatting), but it is unclear how models internally track remaining tokens and generalize this capability across diverse tasks and models. The authors want to reverse-engineer the internal subcircuits that implement such countdown-like behavior to better understand generalization and shared mechanisms in LLMs.
AI.method: (1) Choose a controlled task where the model must write a fixed-length sentence ending in a specific word. (2) Use mechanistic interpretability techniques to isolate a subcircuit in Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct that compares current position to a goal length and encodes “time remaining.” (3) Analyze the geometry of the internal representations used by this subcircuit and compare them to motifs previously found in other frontier LLMs. (4) Apply unsupervised probing on natural-language data to detect when and where this countdown subcircuit is activated in the wild, including cases where the goal length is implicit from context.
AI.result: They identify a concrete countdown subcircuit in Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct that tracks how many tokens remain until a target length. The subcircuit’s representational motif closely matches one previously found in a different frontier LLM on a distinct task, indicating that this mechanism is shared across models. Using unsupervised probes on natural data, they find that the same subcircuit is reused in multiple other tasks that require positional tracking, including tasks where the target length is not explicitly given but inferred from context.
AI.conclusion: A specific, reusable countdown subcircuit underlies a wide range of length- and position-sensitive behaviors in Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct, and its representational motif appears shared with other large models. Reverse-engineering such subcircuits illuminates how models can generalize a mechanism discovered in one controlled task to many different real-world tasks and even across architectures, suggesting that understanding these motifs is key to explaining LLM generalization and behavior.

title: TRACE: An Operational Reasoning Schema for Auditable Agentic Commitments
summary: This paper defines TRACE (Typed Reasoning And Commitment Evidence): a typed, versioned schema for recording reasoning traces, a reference procedure for writing records against it, and one operating discipline, no durable state change without a record. The paper argues in three layers that reasoning is not in the language model: the autoregressive mechanism natively computes association; chain-of-thought and reinforcement learning inherit its limits; and the formal constructs of reasoning theory, from Socratic procedure to Pearl's ladder, are absent as machinery. The schema answers the absence with fields and tests: the TraceRecord and its causal specialization, an eight-stage reference writer, a gate-first measurement regime, the TRACE-Bench protocol, and the consumers, memory admission, plan gating, temporal regret, and verdict reuse, whose more auditable decisions are the measure of the record. A record-consumer contract states what a record guarantees and what a consumer must honor in return, making the schema an operational interface rather than a passive document. Two worked examples run in the main text: a music-lessons argument traced from sentence to typed verdict, separating association, intervention, and prescription; and a flood search-and-rescue vignette in which a predictive world model reports confident plan success that its own support and out-of-distribution scores contradict, so the record defers the commitment, requests a bounded observation, revises append-only, and clears a different branch. The vignette is illustrative, not empirical; closed-loop evaluation is left to future work, so the contribution is the schema and its contract, not a performance claim. Appendices carry the full schema, writer algorithms and cost model, clinical and policy illustrations, the benchmark protocol, convergence metrics, and usage scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TRACE, a typed, versioned schema and operating discipline for recording explicit reasoning traces outside the language model, arguing that LMs do not natively perform true reasoning, and providing a contract between records and downstream consumers to enable auditable, testable use of reasoning in applications.
AI.motivation: Current language models mainly perform associative prediction rather than structured reasoning, yet are often deployed in high-stakes settings that require explicit, inspectable, and testable reasoning. Existing techniques like chain-of-thought and reinforcement learning are built on top of the same autoregressive mechanism and inherit its limitations, lacking formal constructs from reasoning theory. There is a need for an external, typed representation and operational discipline that can capture reasoning steps, commitments, and their justifications in a way that downstream systems can reliably interpret and audit.
AI.method: The authors (1) argue, in three conceptual layers, that reasoning is not internal to LMs: they are fundamentally associative predictors; CoT/RL do not overcome this; and formal reasoning machinery (e.g., interventions, counterfactuals, Socratic procedures) is absent. They then (2) design TRACE: a typed, versioned schema with specialized record types (TraceRecord and a causal variant), and an eight-stage reference procedure for writing reasoning records. (3) They define an operational discipline of "no durable state change without a record" and a record-consumer contract specifying what guarantees a record must provide and what consumers must do in return. (4) They add a measurement and benchmarking regime (gate-first measurement, TRACE-Bench protocol, convergence metrics) and define several record consumers (e.g., memory admission, plan gating, temporal regret, verdict reuse). (5) They illustrate TRACE through two detailed, non-empirical case studies: a music-lesson argument and a flood search-and-rescue planning vignette.
AI.result: The paper yields a concrete schema (TRACE) and associated tooling: a typed, versioned TraceRecord format and causal extension; an eight-stage writer algorithm with tests; a discipline tying durable actions to recorded reasoning; and a formal contract between records and consumers. It also provides benchmark and evaluation scaffolding (TRACE-Bench, convergence metrics, cost models) and multiple usage scenarios and domain illustrations (clinical, policy, planning). The worked examples show how TRACE can separate association from intervention and prescription, surface conflicts between predictive confidence and support/OOD scores, and manage deferral, bounded observation, and append-only revision of commitments.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that reasoning should not be assumed to reside inside language models; instead, it should be externalized into explicit, typed traces governed by an operational discipline and a contract with downstream consumers. TRACE provides such an interface, enabling more auditable and testable use of LMs in decision-making workflows. The main contribution is this schema and interaction model, not empirical performance gains; closed-loop empirical evaluation is left for future work.

title: LakeQuest: A Three-Domain Benchmark for Grounded Question Answering across Data Lakes
comment: 24 pages, 4 figures, 18 tables. Accepted at the Conference on Language Modeling (COLM) 2026
summary: While modern question answering (QA) systems excel on clean, schema-aligned corpora, real-world knowledge is rarely so neatly packaged. Answering questions over enterprise and scientific data lakes requires systems to navigate heterogeneous, weakly structured collections of tables, passages, and linked metadata. Current benchmarks abstract away this noisy discovery process, failing to evaluate end-to-end performance. To bridge this gap, we introduce LakeQuest, a human-validated benchmark of 9,846 QA pairs designed to evaluate the end-to-end retrieve-and-synthesize pipeline over realistic data lakes. LakeQuest spans three diverse domains (AI/ML metadata, retail banking, and multimodal biomedical drug information) and pairs every question with exact, modality-aware evidence pointers. By isolating source discovery from cross-modal synthesis, LakeQuest exposes critical failure modes in modern QA systems. Our baseline evaluations, including standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and agentic tool-use methods, reveal that high-quality retrieval does not guarantee correct reasoning. Systems consistently struggle with relation chaining in metadata graphs, policy grounding in bank ledgers, and joint tabular QA in biomedical contexts, highlighting the need for robust discovery and faithful cross-file composition mechanisms in future agentic QA systems.

AI.tldr: LakeQuest is a benchmark to test end-to-end question answering over messy, real-world data lakes rather than clean datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing QA benchmarks assume clean, well-structured, and schema-aligned data sources, which do not reflect real-world enterprise and scientific data lakes that are heterogeneous, weakly structured, and spread across multiple modalities. As a result, current systems are not properly evaluated on the difficult step of discovering and integrating relevant evidence from noisy, cross-modal sources.
AI.method: The authors construct LakeQuest, a human-validated benchmark containing 9,846 QA pairs drawn from three realistic domains: AI/ML metadata, retail banking, and multimodal biomedical drug information. Each question is paired with precise, modality-aware evidence pointers (e.g., to specific tables, passages, or graph elements). The benchmark design separates the evaluation of source discovery (retrieval over the lake) from cross-modal synthesis (reasoning and answer generation). They run baseline experiments using standard Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipelines and agentic tool-use approaches over this dataset.
AI.result: Baseline systems, including RAG and agent-based tool-use methods, show that even when retrieval quality is high, systems frequently fail at reasoning and synthesis. They struggle with relation chaining in metadata graphs, grounding answers in banking policy and ledger data, and performing multi-table, multimodal QA in biomedical scenarios. This demonstrates significant gaps in current QA methods when faced with realistic data lakes.
AI.conclusion: LakeQuest exposes key weaknesses in modern QA systems operating over heterogeneous data lakes, showing that accurate retrieval alone is insufficient for reliable answers. The benchmark highlights the need for improved mechanisms for robust source discovery, faithful cross-file and cross-modal composition, and more capable reasoning over complex, weakly structured data in future agentic QA systems.

title: The Model Knows Your Project, Not You: Measuring Recognition in LLMs with NameRank
summary: What a frontier model recalls about a person or tool from its own weights -- before any retrieval step -- often shapes the first description a human sees, making that parametric corpus presence a measurement problem. Citations explain about a third of whether a model recognizes a researcher; we target the residual and build NameRank, a [0,1] recognition score: each of 4,685 entities in 54 cohorts is probed with one open-ended question across 36 models, and an independent judge returns a binary verdict against a curated gold -- did the model state a specific, non-guessable fact about this exact entity? -- so hallucination, context echo, and guesses earn nothing. Synthetic-null entities hold the floor near zero, and verdicts track the entity, not the model. One thesis organizes the findings: recognition is paid to named, indexable artifacts, not to credentials or titles. Every Olympic-style credential sits below a working-researcher baseline, because no named artifact ships with the medal, yet the ranking inverts at the marquee tier, where Nobel, Turing, and Fields laureates saturate the panel. For independent creators the tool out-ranks its maker, and the credential that does propagate is a named method or awarded paper. Being one of many named contributors to a celebrated artifact, by contrast, earns almost nothing -- the authors listed on a flagship model report or system card sit near the recognition floor -- because recognition attaches to the artifact's own distinctive name, not to the roster behind it. No bibliometric predicts recognition well; top-density institutions out-recognize peers at matched citations; and on 258 news events recognition loads on peak salience, not persistence. A self-report probe shows introspection reads a corpus prior, not its own knowledge.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces NameRank, a metric for how frontier language models “recognize” people and tools from their internal weights alone, showing that models mainly recognize named artifacts (methods, tools, award-winning papers) rather than credentials, titles, or most individual contributors.
AI.motivation: To understand and quantify what large language models recall about specific people or tools from their internal parameters before any retrieval, since this first recall shapes user impressions and raises measurement, fairness, and attribution concerns.
AI.method: The authors construct a dataset of 4,685 entities across 54 cohorts (researchers, award-winners, tools, etc.) and query 36 frontier models with a single open-ended prompt per entity. An independent judge, using a curated gold standard, assigns a binary judgment: did the model produce at least one specific, non-guessable fact about that exact entity? They exclude hallucinations, context echoes, and guesses. They include synthetic-null entities as controls and compare recognition patterns to citations, institutional affiliation, news event characteristics, and other bibliometrics. They also probe models’ self-reports about their own knowledge.
AI.result: They define NameRank, a [0,1] recognition score, and find: (1) recognition scores are stable at the entity level across models; (2) synthetic-null entities stay near zero, validating the metric; (3) recognition follows named, indexable artifacts (methods, tools, specific papers) more than credentials or titles; (4) Olympic-level credentials (e.g., major competitions) underperform baseline working researchers in recognition, but marquee awards (Nobel, Turing, Fields) yield very high recognition; (5) for independent creators, their tools or methods are more recognized than the creators themselves; (6) authors listed on major model reports or system cards get almost no recognition relative to the artifact; (7) standard bibliometrics do not explain recognition well, and elite institutions have higher recognition even at matched citation levels; (8) for news events, recognition ties to peak salience rather than persistence; (9) introspective self-reports of what the model “knows” track corpus priors rather than actual internal knowledge.
AI.conclusion: Frontier models’ parametric “memory” of people and tools is governed primarily by the presence of named, indexable artifacts rather than individual credentials or contributions. Recognition attaches to distinctive names (methods, tools, landmark papers, marquee awards) and systematically undervalues many contributors, making standard bibliometrics poor predictors of recognition. This has implications for fairness, attribution, and how we interpret models’ apparent knowledge and self-knowledge.

title: QUBO-Optimized Evidence Selection for Retrieval-Augmented Question Answering with Unconventional Solvers
summary: Retrieval-augmented question answering depends on selecting evidence passages that jointly support answer generation. However, many RAG pipelines rely on top-\(k\) ranking, where passages are selected mainly by individual relevance scores, even though multi-hop questions often require complementary evidence satisfying multiple information requirements. Recent LLM-based selectors address this by treating retrieval as set selection, but using an LLM for this intermediate stage can be costly and difficult to scale. In this work, we formulate evidence selection as a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) problem. Given a question, candidate passages, and decomposed information requirements, our method constructs an energy function that balances relevance, requirement coverage, support strength, redundancy, complementarity, and compactness. Low-energy solutions correspond to compact evidence subsets that cover the needed requirements while avoiding unnecessary or repetitive context. The selected passages are then passed to a downstream language model for answer generation, separating combinatorial evidence selection from semantic answer generation. We evaluate the proposed QUBO selector on HotpotQA and compare it with LLM-based set selectors and non-LLM baselines including BM25, relevance top-\(k\), maximal marginal relevance, hybrid lexical--semantic ranking, greedy coverage, and random selection. The QUBO selector achieves competitive exact-match and token-F1 performance relative to LLM-based selectors while providing a solver-compatible formulation for structured evidence selection. These results suggest that multi-hop evidence selection can be cast as discrete optimization, opening a path toward RAG pipelines where LLMs are reserved for semantic processing and answer generation, while context selection is handled by Ising/QUBO-compatible solvers.

AI.tldr: They propose a QUBO-based evidence selector for RAG that finds small sets of passages that jointly satisfy multi-hop information needs, achieving LLM-level performance without using an LLM for selection.
AI.motivation: Existing RAG systems usually pick the top-k individually relevant passages, which is suboptimal for multi-hop QA where multiple complementary pieces of evidence must be combined. LLM-based set selectors can model this joint selection but are expensive and hard to scale. The authors want a scalable, structured, optimization-based way to jointly select evidence passages that best satisfy all information requirements for a question.
AI.method: They formulate evidence selection as a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) problem. Given a question, a set of candidate passages, and decomposed information requirements, they define a QUBO energy function over binary selection variables, encoding multiple criteria: passage relevance, coverage of all requirements, strength of support, redundancy penalties, complementarity among passages, and compactness (small subset size). Minimizing this energy yields a subset of passages that jointly cover the requirements while avoiding unnecessary or repetitive context. This QUBO solution is then used as the context for a downstream language model that handles answer generation, cleanly separating discrete combinatorial selection from semantic reasoning.
AI.result: On HotpotQA, their QUBO-based selector is compared against several baselines: BM25, top-k ranking by relevance, maximal marginal relevance, hybrid lexical–semantic ranking, greedy coverage, random selection, and LLM-based set selectors. The QUBO selector attains competitive exact-match and token-level F1 scores relative to LLM-based selectors and outperforms or is on par with non-LLM baselines, while being compatible with generic QUBO/Ising solvers.
AI.conclusion: Casting multi-hop evidence selection as a QUBO problem provides a structured, optimization-based alternative to LLM-based selectors. It can achieve competitive QA performance while enabling RAG pipelines where LLMs are used mainly for semantic understanding and answer generation, and context selection is offloaded to scalable, solver-based discrete optimization methods, potentially including specialized hardware for Ising/QUBO problems.

title: Evidence-Grounded AI for Musculoskeletal Care
summary: Musculoskeletal diseases are among the leading causes of disability worldwide and create the greatest global need for rehabilitation. Because recovery, remodelling and degeneration often unfold over months to years, musculoskeletal care requires longitudinal management that repeatedly integrates evolving patient evidence, external medical knowledge and stage-specific functional goals. In routine practice, this evidence is fragmented across visits, departments and hospital systems, limiting individualized, evidence-based care. Here we report OrthoPilot, a clinical artificial intelligence system powered by a large language model that integrates hospital data streams with authoritative external knowledge for continuous musculoskeletal management. OrthoPilot autonomously retrieves real-time imaging, laboratory, pathology and order data and converts evolving patient states into evidence-based decisions from admission diagnosis to rehabilitation planning. We established a specialist-validated benchmark from real-world electronic health records spanning 1,000 disease codes. In a reader study across the complete care pathway, OrthoPilot was compared with 81 orthopaedic physicians and surpassed experts with 25 years of experience in diagnostic reasoning, clinical decision-making and management planning. It also outperformed all evaluated intelligent systems across 60 external clinical centres. In a prospective study of 1,870 complex cases, OrthoPilot increased full-chain management success by 10.6%. During an 8-month randomised deployment involving 8,240 inpatients, it increased cumulative cases per bed by 9.7% and improved patient-reported access to health information. These results move clinical AI from predicting isolated events toward executing longitudinal management across complete musculoskeletal care pathways.

AI.tldr: The paper presents OrthoPilot, a large-language-model-based clinical AI system that integrates hospital data and external knowledge to manage musculoskeletal care longitudinally, showing superior performance to human experts and existing AI systems and improving real-world clinical outcomes.
AI.motivation: Musculoskeletal diseases are a leading cause of disability and demand long-term, stage-specific management. However, patient data are fragmented across visits, departments, and hospital systems, making it difficult to deliver individualized, evidence-based, and continuous care. Existing AI systems usually focus on predicting isolated events rather than managing the entire care pathway over time. The authors aim to address these gaps by creating an AI system that can integrate diverse, evolving clinical data and external knowledge to support end-to-end musculoskeletal care.
AI.method: The authors develop OrthoPilot, a clinical AI system powered by a large language model. OrthoPilot autonomously retrieves real-time multimodal hospital data (imaging, laboratory, pathology, orders) and combines it with authoritative external medical knowledge to model evolving patient states and generate evidence-based decisions from admission through rehabilitation. They create a specialist-validated benchmark based on real-world electronic health records covering 1,000 disease codes to evaluate the system. Performance is assessed in a reader study against 81 orthopedic physicians (including very senior experts), in comparisons with other intelligent systems across 60 external clinical centers, and in prospective and randomized deployment studies measuring management success and operational outcomes.
AI.result: In the reader study, OrthoPilot surpassed orthopedic experts with up to 25 years of experience in diagnostic reasoning, clinical decision-making, and management planning. It outperformed all other evaluated intelligent systems across 60 external clinical centers. In a prospective study of 1,870 complex cases, OrthoPilot increased full-chain (end-to-end) management success by 10.6%. In an 8-month randomized deployment involving 8,240 inpatients, it increased cumulative cases per bed by 9.7% and improved patients’ perceived access to health information.
AI.conclusion: OrthoPilot demonstrates that a large-language-model-based clinical AI system can move beyond single-task prediction to execute longitudinal, end-to-end management of musculoskeletal patients across the entire care pathway. By integrating real-time multimodal hospital data with external medical knowledge, the system can outperform experienced clinicians and existing AI tools, improve care management success, and enhance hospital throughput and patient information access. This work suggests a paradigm shift toward continuously managing complex, chronic conditions with AI in real-world clinical settings.

title: Evaluating Health Misinformation in Low-Resource Languages: Integrating Small Language Models with a Culturally-Sensitive Responsible NLP Framework (Bangla as a Case Study)
summary: Artificial Intelligence (AI) technologies, while serving as a foundational enabler for modern social media and digital health services, exert a bivalent effect by simultaneously acting as a combatant against and a spread vector for misinformation. A prevalent challenge in mitigating this issue arises in non-English contexts and low socioeconomic classes, where limited data hinders the training of AI models for effective detection. Consequently, culturally and linguistically diverse (CALD) communities struggle to access trustworthy health information through AI-driven tools. Current AI tools underperform due to a lack of training data and are largely unable to consider language nuances and traditions in non-English contexts. This research addresses these gaps by proposing a CALD-friendly AI-based health misinformation detector and providing a dashboard for medical professionals to analyse this misinformation, a critical step toward mitigating a growing concern among CALD populations. To this end, we conduct a series of experiments using a Bangla-translated health misinformation dataset to evaluate the performance of various Small Language Models (SLMs). SLMs are particularly relevant in this context given the frequent underperformance of Large Language Models (LLMs), which often stems from insufficient domain-specific knowledge and the prohibitive costs of resource-intensive fine-tuning. The results demonstrate that Phi-4 is the superior model, achieving an ideal balance between precision and recall in claim extraction. Then, to mitigate the limitations of SLMs, we design and test a novel health misinformation detection framework grounded in Responsible Natural Language Processing (NLP), which incorporates cultural sensitivity, potential for harm, and communication quality, thereby providing a holistic lens for evaluating misinformation in low-resource languages.

AI.tldr: The paper develops and evaluates an AI-based framework and dashboard to detect health misinformation for culturally and linguistically diverse (CALD) communities, focusing on low-resource languages (Bangla) and responsible, culturally sensitive NLP.
AI.motivation: AI is both a tool to fight and a vector to spread misinformation, especially on social media and in digital health services. Non-English and low-socioeconomic communities are particularly vulnerable because there is limited training data in their languages, so current AI models perform poorly and ignore local linguistic nuances and cultural traditions. CALD communities thus lack reliable AI-driven access to trustworthy health information. There is a need for AI systems that can effectively detect health misinformation in low-resource languages, reflect cultural context, and help medical professionals understand and address misinformation trends.
AI.method: The authors translate an existing health misinformation dataset into Bangla and use it to run a series of experiments comparing multiple Small Language Models (SLMs) for claim extraction and misinformation detection. They focus on evaluating precision and recall trade-offs across models, identifying the best-performing SLM (Phi-4). Building on the observed SLM limitations, they design a novel health misinformation detection framework based on principles of Responsible NLP that explicitly encodes dimensions such as cultural sensitivity, potential harm, and communication quality. They integrate this framework into an AI-based detector and a dashboard intended for use by medical professionals to analyse and visualise misinformation patterns in CALD contexts.
AI.result: Experimental results on the Bangla-translated dataset show that among the evaluated SLMs, Phi-4 provides the best balance between precision and recall for claim extraction, making it the most suitable backbone model in this setting. The responsible NLP-based framework extends beyond raw model performance by introducing additional evaluation dimensions (cultural sensitivity, harm potential, and communication quality), demonstrating that health misinformation in low-resource languages can be assessed more holistically than with standard accuracy or F1 metrics alone.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that small, carefully selected language models—specifically Phi-4—can outperform or be more practical than large models for health misinformation detection in low-resource, non-English contexts, especially when combined with a responsible NLP framework. By integrating cultural sensitivity, harm assessment, and communication quality, the proposed CALD-friendly detector and accompanying dashboard provide a more holistic and practical tool for medical professionals to identify and understand health misinformation affecting CALD communities. This approach represents a key step toward safer, more equitable AI-driven health information ecosystems for underrepresented languages and populations.

title: Vertical Standardisation for High-Risk AI Systems under the EU AI Act: A Domain-Specific Framework for Algorithmic Hiring
summary: According to the recent European legislation, high-risk AI systems will have to adapt in order to comply with requirements related to specific areas, like risk management, data quality and governance, logging and traceability, technical documentation, transparency, human oversight, and accuracy, as outlined in the European Artificial Intelligence (AI) Act. As the standardisation process for AI is expected to remain iterative and, so far, there are no European standards on AI fully covering the challenges of algorithmic hiring, we propose specific standardisation-oriented recommendations related to the relevant AI areas specified by the European Commission. For each of these areas, we set the context by describing the requirements that AI systems in high-risk domains, and especially in recruitment, should fulfil, as well as the activities that should be carried out to ensure their appropriate use and desired performance, in line with the requirements deriving from the AI Act. Unlike existing horizontal approaches to AI governance and standardisation, this paper contributes a vertical, domain-specific framework for algorithmic hiring, and especially ranking-based recruitment systems, by mapping the requirements of the AI Act to concrete standardisation recommendations, focusing on lifecycle discrimination risks, fairness-aware data governance, explainability, human oversight, and post-deployment monitoring in recruitment systems. Even though our recommendations were informed by the outcomes of the European project FINDHR, they are not tied to the project's technical artefacts and could be implemented using alternative methods, tools, or governance mechanisms.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes domain-specific, standardisation-focused recommendations to help algorithmic hiring systems comply with the EU AI Act’s high‑risk requirements.
AI.motivation: High-risk AI systems, including algorithmic hiring tools, must comply with the new European AI Act, which sets requirements on risk management, data governance, transparency, human oversight, and accuracy. Current and emerging AI standards are generic (horizontal) and do not fully address the particular risks and needs of recruitment systems, such as ranking-based candidate selection and discrimination risks. There is a need for a concrete, domain-specific framework that translates the AI Act’s abstract obligations into actionable guidance for algorithmic hiring.
AI.method: The authors interpret the AI Act provisions that apply to high-risk AI systems and specialise them to the context of recruitment and ranking-based hiring tools. For each key requirement area identified by the European Commission—risk management, data quality and governance, logging and traceability, technical documentation, transparency, human oversight, and accuracy—they (1) describe what high‑risk recruitment systems should fulfil, and (2) derive concrete, standardisation-oriented recommendations. They structure these recommendations around lifecycle discrimination risks, fairness-aware data governance, explainability, human oversight, and post-deployment monitoring, informed by insights from the European FINDHR project but formulated in a technology-agnostic way.
AI.result: The outcome is a vertical, domain-specific framework that maps high-level AI Act obligations to concrete standardisation recommendations tailored to algorithmic hiring, particularly ranking-based recruitment systems. The framework specifies what processes, documentation, governance practices, and technical controls should be in place across the AI lifecycle (from data collection to post-deployment monitoring) to address discrimination risks, ensure fairness-aware data governance, provide explainability and transparency, enable effective human oversight, and support ongoing monitoring and logging in recruitment contexts.
AI.conclusion: Generic AI governance and standardisation approaches are insufficient for the particular risks and regulatory demands of algorithmic hiring under the EU AI Act. A domain-specific, vertical framework is needed to operationalise the Act’s requirements in recruitment, especially for ranking-based hiring systems. The authors’ proposed recommendations fill this gap by offering a standardisation-ready blueprint, independent of any specific technical solution, that can guide developers, deployers, and standardisation bodies in aligning algorithmic hiring systems with the AI Act’s high-risk requirements.

title: Policy-Conditioned Constrained Decoding for Column-Level Access Control in Text-to-SQL
summary: Text-to-SQL is increasingly deployed across trust boundaries between data providers and users. Such deployment must balance three competing requirements: policy compliance, answer coverage, and bounded cost. Existing approaches typically decide refusal based on which columns a query mentions and enforce it stochastically. Whether a query is compliant, however, depends not only on which columns appear but on how they are used, and stochastic enforcement cannot deterministically rule out violations. We formalize this requirement as a column-use policy over semantic use: output, filter condition, and aggregation argument. We integrate the policy by aligning each role with grammar productions tracked by the decoder. The resulting system, PCC-SQL, applies a per-token logits mask that deterministically eliminates single-query column-use violations on the supported SQL fragment in a single decoding pass. Across three benchmarks and three open-source models, PCC-SQL achieves 0% Leakage Rate and Coverage up to 88.7% on Spider-CU, while staying within +10% tokens of direct prompting. We additionally assess semantic alignment with execution accuracy.

AI.tldr: They propose PCC-SQL, a decoding-time control method for Text-to-SQL systems that enforces fine-grained column-use policies (on output, filters, and aggregations) via grammar-aligned logits masking, achieving zero policy leakage with high query coverage and low overhead.
AI.motivation: Text-to-SQL is increasingly used across organizational or trust boundaries where data providers must enforce access policies. Existing policy-enforcing methods typically look only at which columns are mentioned and often rely on stochastic (probabilistic) refusal or filtering, so they cannot guarantee that a generated SQL query won’t violate policies. However, policy compliance depends on how columns are used semantically (e.g., as outputs, filters, or aggregation arguments), not just on their presence. There is a need for a deterministic, fine-grained enforcement mechanism that works during generation, preserves answer coverage, and keeps computational cost bounded.
AI.method: They define a formal column-use policy at the level of semantic roles: whether a column may be used in SELECT output, in WHERE/HAVING filter predicates, or as an aggregation argument. They then align these roles with specific grammar productions of a constrained SQL decoder. During decoding, they track which grammar production is active for each token and apply a per-token logits mask that removes any token choices (columns) that would violate the column-use policy for that particular semantic role. This is done in a single decoding pass over a specified SQL fragment, effectively integrating policy enforcement into the model’s beam/greedy search without post-hoc filtering.
AI.result: Their system, PCC-SQL, deterministically eliminates single-query column-use policy violations on the supported SQL subset, achieving a Leakage Rate of 0% on three Text-to-SQL benchmarks and three open-source language models. On the Spider-CU benchmark, it reaches up to 88.7% coverage (fraction of user requests that can still be answered under policy) while increasing token usage by at most 10% compared to direct prompting without policy controls. They additionally evaluate how well the enforced outputs align semantically by measuring execution accuracy of the generated SQL queries.
AI.conclusion: Column-use policies can be enforced deterministically and efficiently in Text-to-SQL systems by integrating fine-grained semantic roles into the decoding process. PCC-SQL demonstrates that grammar-aligned per-token logits masking can achieve zero policy leakage while maintaining high coverage and modest computational overhead, providing a practical approach for deploying Text-to-SQL models across trust boundaries with strong policy guarantees.

title: Agentic Service-Oriented Computing: A Manifesto for the Next Frontier of Service-Oriented Computing
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Web Services (ICWS); Corresponding author: Prof. Amin Beheshti; DOI 10.1109/ICWS72778.2026.00163
summary: The rapid emergence of LLM-powered autonomous and semi-autonomous agents is reshaping software systems from static, request-response components into goal-directed, adaptive, and tool-using computational actors. As these agents move from isolated cognitive prototypes into complex distributed workflows, they confront challenges that the Service-Oriented Computing community has studied for more than two decades: composition, interoperability, quality of service, lifecycle management, governance, security, and trust. Yet much of today's agentic AI ecosystem is developing these foundations ad hoc, without the engineering rigour required for dependable enterprise and societal deployment.
