This paper introduces Agentic Service-Oriented Computing (ASOC) as a new research and practice area concerned with engineering agents as services, orchestrating services through autonomous and semi-autonomous agents, and governing ecosystems of agents and services under constraints of trust, cybersecurity, compliance, performance, and accountability. We articulate six foundational principles of ASOC (harness-ability, composability, lifecycle engineering, trustworthiness by design, goal-driven orchestration, and observability/accountability) and organise a five-dimensional research agenda spanning: (i) agentic services foundations and lifecycle engineering; (ii) composition, orchestration, and interoperability; (iii) governance, observability, and accountability; (iv) security, trust, and risk management; and (v) evaluation, certification, and Agentic QoS. We argue that the Services Computing community is especially well positioned to provide the conceptual and engineering spine for this emerging field, transforming agentic AI from fragmented demonstrations into dependable, service-based systems worthy of human and organisational trust.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Agentic Service-Oriented Computing (ASOC) as a framework to systematically engineer LLM-based autonomous and semi-autonomous agents as dependable services within complex workflows.
AI.motivation: LLM-powered agents are rapidly evolving from simple request-response tools into complex, goal-directed components in distributed systems. However, their current development is largely ad hoc, lacking the mature engineering principles that Service-Oriented Computing (SOC) has developed for composition, interoperability, quality of service, lifecycle management, security, and trust. There is a need to bring SOC rigor to agentic AI to enable trustworthy enterprise and societal deployment.
AI.method: The authors conceptually define a new area, Agentic Service-Oriented Computing (ASOC), and structure it via six foundational principles: harness-ability, composability, lifecycle engineering, trustworthiness by design, goal-driven orchestration, and observability/accountability. They then organize a five-dimensional research agenda that covers foundations and lifecycle, composition and interoperability, governance and accountability, security and risk, and evaluation/certification of agentic quality of service.
AI.result: The primary result is a conceptual framework and terminology for ASOC, including clearly articulated principles and a structured, five-dimensional research agenda to guide future work by the Services Computing community and related fields.
AI.conclusion: By leveraging the established concepts and engineering discipline of Service-Oriented Computing, the ASOC framework can transform current fragmented, prototype-level agentic AI into dependable, service-based systems suitable for organizations and society, making them trustworthy, governable, and robust in real-world deployments.

title: Beyond Binary Detection: A Multi-Dimensional Taxonomy of Cancer Misinformation on Reddit
summary: Cancer-related discussions on social media provide an important space for information exchange and peer support, but also facilitate the spread of misinformation that may influence prevention, screening, and treatment decisions. Existing research on cancer misinformation often relies on narrow definitions, small-scale datasets, or binary labeling frameworks. We introduce a multi-dimensional taxonomy for characterizing cancer misinformation in Reddit discussions of breast, lung, colon, and prostate cancer. The taxonomy captures seven dimensions, including misinformation presence, information type, risk level, stance, and topical focus. Using expert-annotated data, we evaluate multiple large language models (LLMs) for scalable misinformation annotation and analyze cancer misinformation across Reddit communities. Our results show that cancer-related misinformation constitutes approximately 6\% of Reddit cancer discussions, with substantial variation across communities and misinformation topics. Few-shot prompting substantially improves classification performance, particularly for nuanced taxonomy dimensions. We additionally identify recurring misinformation narratives centered on unsupported treatments, distrust of conventional medicine, and misleading claims about diagnosis and screening. Our taxonomy, dataset, and findings provide a foundation for multi-dimensional modeling of online cancer misinformation.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a multi-dimensional taxonomy of cancer misinformation on Reddit, evaluates LLMs to label it, and analyzes how often and in what forms misinformation appears across cancer communities.
AI.motivation: Cancer discussions on social media mix valuable support with misinformation that can impact health decisions. Prior work has narrow definitions, small datasets, and simplistic binary labels, which are insufficient to capture the complexity and nuance of real-world cancer misinformation. The authors aim to provide a richer framework and scalable tools to systematically characterize this misinformation landscape.
AI.method: The authors construct a seven-dimensional taxonomy for cancer misinformation in Reddit posts about breast, lung, colon, and prostate cancer, encompassing dimensions such as whether misinformation is present, information type, risk, stance, and topical focus. They then create an expert-annotated dataset following this taxonomy, and test multiple large language models with different prompting strategies, including few-shot prompting, to automatically annotate posts at scale. Finally, they apply the best-performing configurations to analyze prevalence and patterns of misinformation across different Reddit cancer communities and topics.
AI.result: They find that about 6% of Reddit cancer discussions contain misinformation, though the rate varies by community and topic. Few-shot prompting significantly boosts LLM performance, especially for subtle, nuanced taxonomy dimensions beyond simple true/false judgments. Their analysis surfaces recurring misinformation narratives, particularly around unsupported or alternative treatments, distrust in conventional medicine, and misleading claims about diagnosis and screening practices.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that cancer misinformation on Reddit, while not dominant in volume, is nontrivial and heterogeneous across communities and topics. Their seven-dimensional taxonomy, expert-labeled dataset, and LLM evaluation results offer a foundation for more nuanced, multi-dimensional modeling of online cancer misinformation and for building scalable monitoring and intervention systems.

title: Atomic Units of X: The Compression Layer of Intelligence
summary: This paper proposes a theoretical framework for understanding intelligence as a process of atomic compression and compositional reuse. We argue that cognitive, biological, computational, and organizational systems achieve scalable intelligence by decomposing complex phenomena into reusable atomic units that can be recombined into higher-order structures. Drawing on evidence from cognitive science, information theory, evolutionary biology, software engineering, medicine, legal reasoning, education, music, and artificial intelligence, the paper develops the concept of atomic units as fundamental compression layers that support efficiency, transfer, interpretability, and evolvability. The central contribution is the Compression Calculus, a formal framework for comparing surface-level representations with atomic representations and for describing how compression gains compound across abstraction layers. We introduce the Compounding Cascade thesis, according to which each additional layer of abstraction multiplicatively increases representational efficiency rather than merely adding incremental savings. The paper further argues that contemporary AI systems often operate at suboptimal levels of representation, relying on token-level processing or document-level retrieval rather than stable, concept-level atomic structures. In this view, large language models are best understood not as complete knowledge architectures, but as dynamic fusion engines capable of navigating, sequencing, and recombining atomic units. The framework provides a foundation for designing self-evolving knowledge systems that can discover, refine, and compose new primitives over time. By reframing intelligence as compression through compositional abstraction, the paper offers a unifying perspective on expertise, knowledge representation, explainable AI, and the future architecture of adaptive intelligent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a general theory of intelligence as compression via reusable atomic concepts and shows how layered abstraction yields multiplicative efficiency gains, offering a framework (Compression Calculus and Compounding Cascade) for analyzing and designing more scalable, interpretable, and self-evolving intelligent systems.
AI.motivation: Current understandings of intelligence across domains (cognitive, biological, computational, organizational) are fragmented and often focus on surface-level representations (tokens, documents, local rules) rather than deeper conceptual structures. There is a need for a unifying theory that explains how systems achieve scalability, generalization, and evolvability through abstraction and reuse, and that clarifies why many contemporary AI systems feel powerful yet structurally suboptimal. The authors aim to supply such a theory grounded in compression and compositionality.
AI.method: The authors construct a theoretical framework rather than an empirical model. They synthesize evidence and examples from multiple fields—cognitive science, information theory, evolutionary biology, software engineering, medicine, law, education, music, and AI—to motivate the existence of atomic, reusable units of representation. They then formalize this in the "Compression Calculus," which defines and compares surface-level vs atomic-level representations, quantifies compression gains across abstraction layers, and introduces the "Compounding Cascade" thesis to model multiplicative gains. Conceptual analysis and cross-domain analogies are the primary methods.
AI.result: The paper yields: (1) a precise notion of atomic units as compression layers that support efficiency, transfer, interpretability, and evolvability; (2) the Compression Calculus, a formal way to reason about representational choices and compression gains across layers; (3) the Compounding Cascade thesis, stating that each new abstraction layer multiplies, rather than simply adds, representational efficiency; and (4) an analysis of contemporary AI that argues they underutilize concept-level atomic structures, instead working mostly at token or document levels.
AI.conclusion: Intelligence is best understood as a process of compression via compositional abstraction: systems become more powerful when they discover, stabilize, and reuse atomic units of representation that can be recombined into higher-order structures. Each additional abstraction layer yields multiplicative compression benefits, explaining the scalability of expert knowledge and complex intelligent behavior. Large language models should therefore be viewed as fusion engines operating over such atomic structures, not as complete knowledge architectures. This framework suggests design principles for future AI and knowledge systems that are more interpretable, transferable, and self-evolving by focusing on discovering, refining, and composing atomic primitives.

title: Ring-Zero: Scaling Zero RL to a Trillion Parameters for Emergent Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards without human-annotated data, often referred to as zero RL, has emerged as a powerful paradigm for eliciting chain-of-thought reasoning. However, due to computational constraints, existing studies are largely restricted to small models, leaving the training dynamics and emergent capabilities at a large scale unexplored. To meaningfully explore this frontier, we aim to elicit high-quality reasoning behaviors from the model. However, we find that naive scaling often suffers from poor readability, token redundancy, and a lack of adaptive reasoning depth. To address these challenges, we present a stable and efficient training pipeline, incorporating algorithmic and system optimizations such as clipped importance sampling, training-inference ratio correction, and mixed-precision control. Our experiments offer three key findings that validate the "bitter lesson" of scaling: (1) scaling to 1T parameters significantly enhances sample efficiency and performance ceilings; (2) the training process progresses sequentially through an initial discovery phase followed by a sharpening phase; and (3) the model spontaneously develops advanced cognitive behaviors, including anthropomorphism, structured formatting, self-verification, parallel reasoning, and context anxiety, rendering hand-crafted heuristics redundant. Evaluated on seven mathematical benchmarks, Ring-2.5-1T-Zero achieves competitive performance. Additionally, to assess CoT quality beyond final-answer correctness, we propose a structured evaluation framework across three dimensions: comprehensibility, reproducibility, and efficiency, where our model demonstrates clear advantages in producing structured and concise reasoning traces. By sharing our observed emergent phenomena, we hope to provide the community with deeper insights into scaling behaviors, particularly at the 1-trillion scale.

AI.tldr: The paper scales zero-shot reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards to a 1T-parameter model, proposes a stable and efficient training pipeline, documents emergent reasoning behaviors, and introduces a framework to evaluate chain-of-thought (CoT) quality beyond accuracy.
AI.motivation: Existing zero RL work for chain-of-thought reasoning is limited to small models due to computational cost, so we lack understanding of training dynamics and emergent capabilities at trillion-parameter scale. Naively scaling leads to unreadable, redundant, and non-adaptive reasoning traces. The authors want to both make large-scale zero RL feasible and extract high-quality, interpretable reasoning behaviors from very large models, while systematically characterizing what emerges during scaling.
AI.method: They build a stable and efficient zero RL training pipeline for CoT reasoning that does not require human-labeled data but uses verifiable rewards. The pipeline includes algorithmic and systems optimizations such as clipped importance sampling to stabilize off-policy updates, training-inference ratio correction to balance compute, and mixed-precision control for efficiency. They train a 1T-parameter model, Ring-2.5-1T-Zero, and analyze its learning dynamics (discovery vs. sharpening phases) and emergent behaviors. They also design a structured evaluation framework for chain-of-thought quality along three axes: comprehensibility, reproducibility, and efficiency.
AI.result: Scaling zero RL to 1T parameters substantially improves sample efficiency and raises performance ceilings on seven mathematical reasoning benchmarks, where Ring-2.5-1T-Zero attains competitive results. Training dynamics show a sequential progression: the model first discovers useful reasoning patterns, then sharpens and refines them. The trained model exhibits emergent advanced behaviors such as anthropomorphic dialog, structured formatting of reasoning, self-verification of steps, parallel reasoning paths, and context anxiety, obviating the need for many hand-crafted prompting heuristics. Under their new evaluation framework, the model produces reasoning traces that are more structured, concise, and higher quality than baselines.
AI.conclusion: The study supports the scaling hypothesis (the "bitter lesson"): pushing zero RL to trillion-parameter models with proper algorithmic and systems design leads to better sample efficiency, higher performance, and rich emergent reasoning behaviors. Their training pipeline makes such large-scale zero RL practical and stable. The observed cognitive-like behaviors suggest that many manually designed prompting tricks become unnecessary at scale. Their proposed CoT evaluation framework provides a more nuanced way to measure reasoning quality than final-answer accuracy, and the empirical findings offer guidance for future work on large-scale reasoning models.

title: A Learning-Rate-Gated Failure of GRPO in a Small Language and Vision-Language Model Web Agent: A Controlled Null and Its Mechanism
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) in particular, is now run routinely on a supervised checkpoint in the hope of producing a stronger agent. We ask whether it adds skill to a small language and vision-language model web agent at the 4B to 8B scale, or whether it mostly reshapes behavior the supervised model already has. Across a control grid of 18 runs that varies learning rate, KL weight, seed, initialization, and clipping, no configuration credibly improves the success rate of a strong supervised baseline on tasks the agent has largely mastered. On the text track, moderate to high learning rates make it credibly worse. The null holds under paired testing, 25 evaluation seeds, 6 training seeds, changes to the recipe, both text and Set-of-Marks screenshot observations, and scaling the backbone to 8B; the credible harm is a text-track finding and is only nominal under Set-of-Marks. To show that the null reflects the setting and not a broken pipeline, we run the identical harness, reward, and recipe on tasks whose reward is reachable by sampling, and there the success rate rises by 22 points with a paired interval that excludes zero. GRPO therefore helps only when there is headroom to climb, meaning the sampled policy already succeeds more often than the greedy one. We then explain the failure. A middle learning rate degrades the agent and a high one collapses it, and the two regimes form a double dissociation: grafting localizes the degrade regime to the attention and MLP blocks, while the collapse regime cannot be traced to any single group, and the embedding change that dominates the weight movement is causally inert. At 4B, effective rank in the late layers tracks capability in both directions; at 8B the two come apart. This coupling is specific to the smaller model, so we report it as scale-dependent.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates whether GRPO-style reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards actually improves small (4B–8B) web agents beyond an already-strong supervised baseline, and finds that it generally does not, and can even hurt, unless there is clear performance headroom.
AI.motivation: GRPO and related verifiable-RL methods are now commonly run on top of supervised checkpoints with the expectation that they will make agents stronger. However, this assumption has not been rigorously tested for small language and vision-language web agents that already do well on their training tasks. The authors want to know if RL is truly adding new capabilities or merely reshaping behavior, and under what conditions it helps or harms.
AI.method: They take small (4B and 8B) language and vision-language web agents initialized from strong supervised checkpoints and apply GRPO across a thorough control grid of 18 runs, varying hyperparameters such as learning rate, KL penalty weight, random seeds, initialization, and clipping. They evaluate on tasks where the supervised agent is already strong, using extensive paired testing with multiple evaluation and training seeds, and on both text-only and Set-of-Marks screenshot observation modes. To validate the pipeline, they re-use the exact same training harness, reward function, and RL recipe on a different set of tasks where rewards are easier to reach via sampling and there is clear headroom. They also perform mechanistic analyses: grafting experiments that swap parts of networks, tracking effective rank in later layers, and comparing behavior across scales (4B vs 8B).
AI.result: On tasks largely mastered by the supervised baseline, no hyperparameter configuration produces a credible improvement in success rate; on text-only tasks, moderate to high learning rates reliably reduce performance. These null or harmful effects persist across multiple robustness checks (paired testing, many seeds, different recipes, both observation formats, and scaling to 8B). In contrast, on tasks where random sampling can already reach reasonably high rewards, the same GRPO setup improves success rate by 22 percentage points with statistically significant gains. Mechanistically, they identify two RL regimes: a middle learning rate that degrades performance and a high learning rate that collapses the model. Grafting shows the degrade regime is localized to attention and MLP blocks, whereas the collapse regime is distributed and not attributable to a single component; large changes in embeddings dominate weight motion but are not causally relevant. At 4B, effective rank in late layers correlates with capability changes, but this relationship breaks down at 8B.
AI.conclusion: GRPO-based reinforcement learning on top of a strong supervised small web agent generally does not add new skill when the tasks are already largely solved; it mainly reshapes behavior and can even be harmful, particularly at higher learning rates on text tasks. GRPO is beneficial only when there is clear performance headroom—specifically when the sampled policy already outperforms the greedy policy—indicating that RL’s usefulness is contingent on the initial policy’s exploration capacity. The failure modes differ by learning-rate regime and reflect complex, sometimes scale-dependent internal changes; at smaller scale, effective rank is more tightly coupled to capability than at 8B. Overall, the work cautions against defaulting to RL fine-tuning on saturated tasks and highlights the importance of task headroom and model scale when applying GRPO to web agents.

title: WikiSTAR: A System for Shedding Light on the Hidden History of Scientific Wikipedia Articles
summary: Wikipedia plays a key role in shaping public understanding of science, and its openly accessible revision history is a unique record of how scientific knowledge evolves over time. Yet scientifically meaningful revisions are obscured by the sheer volume of routine edits, leaving each article's scientific history hidden. We present WikiSTAR (Scientific Tracking of Article Revisions), an interactive system for exploring scientifically meaningful changes across an article's revision history. Using an LLM classifier with an expert-designed multi-label taxonomy, WikiSTAR first tags edit types such as the addition of technical terms, new research findings, and changes in scientific narrative. Then, through interactive views, an article's full revision history can be traced at any granularity - from aggregate trends that reveal when and in which sections scientific content was added or refined, down to individual edits - showing how scientific knowledge develops at a scale previously impossible. In a user study, experts from three domains found that WikiSTAR surfaced new patterns and research questions and enabled previously impractical analyses. We release our system, code and a human-annotated benchmark.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces WikiSTAR, an interactive system that uses an LLM-based classifier and expert taxonomy to identify and visualize scientifically meaningful edits in Wikipedia article revision histories.
AI.motivation: Wikipedia strongly shapes public understanding of science, but its massive revision logs mix minor routine edits with substantial scientific changes. This makes it hard for researchers and experts to track how scientific knowledge on Wikipedia evolves, identify when key findings or conceptual shifts appeared, or analyze patterns of scientific content development over time. There is a need for tools that distinguish scientifically meaningful edits from non-scientific noise and present this information in a usable way.
AI.method: The authors design WikiSTAR, which combines an expert-created multi-label taxonomy of scientific edit types with a large language model classifier that tags each revision according to categories such as additions of technical terms, new research findings, or narrative changes. They then build interactive visual interfaces that aggregate and display these labeled edits across an article’s entire revision history, enabling exploration at different granularities: overall temporal trends, section-level evolution, and drill-down to individual edits. They also conduct a user study with domain experts from three fields to evaluate usefulness.
AI.result: WikiSTAR successfully classifies and visualizes scientifically meaningful edits across Wikipedia article histories, enabling users to see when and where scientific content is added, modified, or refined. In a user study with experts from three domains, participants discovered new patterns and research questions and performed analyses that they reported would have been impractical or impossible using existing tools. The authors also provide an open-source implementation and a human-annotated benchmark dataset for future research and evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The system demonstrates that combining LLM-based edit classification with a structured taxonomy and interactive visualization can reveal the hidden scientific evolution of Wikipedia articles. WikiSTAR makes it feasible to study how scientific knowledge is constructed, updated, and debated on Wikipedia at multiple scales, opening new avenues for research on science communication and knowledge dynamics. The released code and benchmark support further work on understanding and improving the representation of science on collaborative platforms.

title: Internet of Agentic Things: Networked AI Agents for Closed-Loop IoT Orchestration
summary: The paper introduces the Internet of Agentic Things (IoAT), an architectural framework that integrates agentic AI, IoT, cyber-physical systems, Physical AI, edge computing, and digital twins into a unified closed-loop orchestration framework. The proposed architecture consists of cloud, edge/fog, and physical IoT layers connected through autonomous AI agents that perceive, reason, coordinate, and actuate across distributed cyber-physical environments. The paper formalizes IoAT as a coupled workflow-control problem with nested strategic and tactical decision making using a hylomorphic dynamic programming framework that links agentic planning with physical execution. Smart-building orchestration is presented as a representative use case, and key research challenges related to safety, security, governance, resilience, and trustworthy deployment are discussed.

AI.tldr: Proposes the Internet of Agentic Things (IoAT), a unified architecture where autonomous AI agents orchestrate IoT, cyber-physical systems, edge/cloud computing, and digital twins in a closed loop.
AI.motivation: Current IoT and cyber-physical deployments are fragmented, with limited autonomy and weak integration between AI decision-making, physical devices, and digital twins. There is a need for a principled architecture and formal model to coordinate distributed intelligent agents that can plan and act safely and reliably across physical and virtual layers.
AI.method: Define a multi-layer IoAT architecture (cloud, edge/fog, physical IoT) connected via autonomous AI agents that perceive, reason, coordinate, and actuate. Formalize the orchestration as a coupled workflow-control problem with nested strategic (long-term) and tactical (short-term) decision making, using a hylomorphic dynamic programming framework to link abstract agentic planning with concrete physical execution. Illustrate the framework via a smart-building orchestration use case and analyze key challenges.
AI.result: Provides an architectural blueprint and formalization of IoAT, demonstrating conceptually how agentic AI can coordinate distributed cyber-physical resources in a smart-building scenario. Identifies critical open research problems around safety, security, governance, resilience, and trustworthiness, rather than reporting empirical performance results.
AI.conclusion: IoAT offers a structured way to integrate agentic AI with IoT, edge/cloud, cyber-physical systems, and digital twins in a closed loop, enabling more autonomous and coordinated operation of physical environments. Realizing this vision will require further work on safety, security, governance, resilience, and trustworthy deployment before large-scale adoption is feasible.

title: Language Identification with Succinct Machine-Independent Traces
summary: Motivated by the power of large language models, there has been renewed interest in the Gold-Angluin model of language identification in the limit, with an eye toward variants of the model that might overcome the negative results for its original formulation. Recent papers on this question have proposed looking at computational traces and annotations of training strings as a source of additional power for a learner, reflecting empirical regularities such as the way that commented source code is easier to learn from than arbitrary source code, and text annotated with algorithmically generated chain-of-thought tokens can be easier to learn from than the raw text itself. This recent work has shown positive results for language identification in the presence of such computational traces, but the traces in these positive results come from explicit automata-theoretic machine models that generate the language, where the underlying vocabulary of tokens for the traces is very large. In this paper, we address two fundamental issues left open by this line of work: can we achieve positive results with traces that use only a small alphabet, and can we define traces directly from the language itself, without requiring an underlying machine model that generates it? We establish positive results for both of these questions: for an arbitrary collection of languages, we show how to define computational traces that enable identification in the limit, using an alphabet of tokens that is linear in the size of the alphabet that the languages are defined over, and independent of any other properties of the languages.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to use small-alphabet computational traces, defined directly from languages rather than machine models, to make identification in the limit possible for arbitrary language families.
AI.motivation: Classical Gold-Angluin language identification in the limit has strong negative results: many language classes cannot be learned from positive data alone. Inspired by the empirical success of large language models and the usefulness of annotated data (e.g., comments in source code, chain-of-thought explanations), recent theory work augments the learning model with computational traces attached to training strings, showing that such annotations can restore learnability. However, existing positive results rely on traces produced by explicit automata-theoretic generators and use very large trace alphabets. The paper is motivated by two gaps: (1) whether learnability can still be achieved when the trace alphabet is small, and (2) whether traces can be defined intrinsically from the language itself, without assuming an underlying machine model that generates it.
AI.method: The authors work in the Gold-Angluin framework of identification in the limit with positive data, extended to include computational traces attached to sample strings. They abstract away from specific automata and instead design a generic trace scheme that can be associated with any language in an arbitrary collection. They construct traces using a token alphabet whose size is only linear in the base symbol alphabet of the languages, not in other complexity measures. Conceptually, they define a mapping from strings to traces such that the pair (string, trace) contains enough structured information for a learner to uniquely identify the target language in the limit. They then prove, via learning-theoretic arguments, that under this trace scheme every language in the collection becomes identifiable in the limit.
AI.result: For any collection of languages over some finite alphabet, the authors exhibit a way to assign computational traces to strings so that a learner can identify the target language in the limit. Crucially, the trace alphabet required has size only linear in the size of the original symbol alphabet and does not depend on other properties of the languages (such as complexity or cardinality of the collection). Moreover, the traces are defined directly from the languages themselves and do not rely on specifying or using an explicit underlying machine model that generates those languages.
AI.conclusion: The paper shows that the negative results for language identification in the limit can be overcome in a robust way by augmenting examples with appropriately designed computational traces. These traces can be (i) defined intrinsically from the languages, without appealing to explicit automata or machine models, and (ii) implemented using a small alphabet whose size scales only linearly with the base symbol alphabet. This establishes that the additional informational power needed for positive learnability results can be provided in a compact and model-independent form, aligning better with how annotations and explanations appear in practical machine learning settings.

title: MaxSAT-Based Feedback for Guiding Vision-Language Models in Sudoku
comment: Accepted at the 25th EPIA Conference on Artificial Intelligence, EPIA 2026. 16 pages, 1 figure, and 1 table
summary: Vision--Language Models (VLMs) have recently demonstrated promising performance on structured visual reasoning tasks, including grid-based puzzles. However, despite strong perceptual capabilities, these models lack explicit mechanisms for enforcing logical consistency and frequently generate assignments that violate underlying constraints. In this paper, we propose a neuro-symbolic approach that integrates formal constraint reasoning into the VLM solving process via a Maximum Satisfiability (MaxSAT) oracle. Rather than computing solutions directly, the symbolic component acts as a consistency validator and refinement engine. Candidate placements generated by the VLM are encoded as soft clauses in a partial MaxSAT formulation, while Sudoku constraints remain hard clauses. When inconsistencies arise, the MaxSAT solver identifies a largest mutually consistent subset of assignments, which is then translated into structured textual and visual feedback to guide subsequent refinements. We evaluate our approach on a Sudoku dataset across multiple open-source and closed-access VLMs. Results show that MaxSAT-based feedback improves logical consistency and increases the number of solved instances, particularly in full-board refinement mode. These findings demonstrate that symbolic optimisation can enhance the reliability of vision-language reasoning.

AI.tldr: They integrate a MaxSAT-based symbolic reasoning module with vision–language models to solve Sudoku more consistently.
AI.motivation: Vision–language models can perceive puzzle grids but often give logically inconsistent or constraint-violating answers because they lack explicit symbolic reasoning. The authors want a way to enforce logical consistency without discarding the strengths of VLMs.
AI.method: They build a neuro-symbolic framework where the VLM proposes candidate Sudoku cell assignments. These candidates are encoded as soft clauses in a partial MaxSAT problem, while Sudoku rules are encoded as hard clauses. A MaxSAT solver finds the largest subset of VLM assignments consistent with all Sudoku constraints. The differences between the VLM proposal and the consistent subset are converted into structured textual and visual feedback that is fed back to the VLM for iterative refinement (including a full-board refinement mode).
AI.result: Across several open- and closed-source VLMs on a Sudoku dataset, adding MaxSAT-based feedback leads to more logically consistent board states and a higher proportion of fully solved puzzles, with the largest gains observed when using the full-board refinement strategy.
AI.conclusion: Coupling VLMs with a MaxSAT oracle that acts as a consistency checker and refinement guide improves the reliability of structured visual reasoning. Symbolic optimisation can effectively complement neural perception and language capabilities, especially in constraint-heavy tasks like Sudoku.

title: Translation as a Computationally Efficient Bridge: Feasibility of English BERT for Low-Resource Languages
summary: BERT models have revolutionised Natural Language Processing (NLP) through their ability to process unstructured text across diverse domains. However, developing high-quality BERT models for non-English languages remains challenging due to limited annotated data and high computational demands. Translating non-English data into English and fine-tuning existing English BERT models offers a resource-efficient alternative, yet few studies have structurally compared translation-based fine-tuning with native-language BERT performance across tasks and languages. This study provides such a comparison, evaluating the feasibility of translation-based fine-tuning across six NLP tasks: Sentiment Analysis, Hate Speech Detection, Question Answering, Named Entity Recognition, Part-of-Speech Tagging, and Natural Language Inference, using datasets translated from Bulgarian, Chinese, Dutch, Italian, and Russian. Across all settings, the translation-based approach was comparable or superior in 53.3 percent of cases. Gains were most frequent in Question Answering, Part-of-Speech Tagging, and Natural Language Inference, while performance declines were common in Named Entity Recognition and Hate Speech Detection. The results show that translation-based fine-tuning is most effective for tasks relying on syntactic or structural patterns and for languages typologically close to English, such as Dutch, but less effective for token-level or culturally nuanced tasks, particularly in Chinese. Overall, this study demonstrates that translation-based fine-tuning offers a scalable, resource-efficient, and empirically validated path for extending NLP to low-resource languages while advancing linguistic inclusivity and sustainability in artificial intelligence.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether translating non-English text into English and fine-tuning English BERT models can match or surpass native-language BERT models across multiple NLP tasks and languages, finding that translation-based fine-tuning is often competitive and particularly effective for syntactic tasks and languages close to English.
AI.motivation: Building strong BERT models for non-English languages is difficult due to limited labeled data and high computational costs. While using machine translation plus English BERT is an attractive, cheaper alternative, there is little systematic evidence on when this strategy works well or poorly. The authors aim to fill this gap by providing a structured, multi-task, multi-language comparison.
AI.method: The authors translate datasets from five non-English languages (Bulgarian, Chinese, Dutch, Italian, and Russian) into English and fine-tune existing English BERT models on these translated datasets. They compare the resulting performance against BERT models trained directly in the original languages across six NLP tasks: Sentiment Analysis, Hate Speech Detection, Question Answering, Named Entity Recognition, Part-of-Speech Tagging, and Natural Language Inference, then quantify where translation-based fine-tuning is comparable, better, or worse.
AI.result: In 53.3% of all evaluated settings, translation-based fine-tuning performs comparably to or better than native-language BERT models. The approach yields the largest gains in Question Answering, Part-of-Speech Tagging, and Natural Language Inference, but underperforms more often in Named Entity Recognition and Hate Speech Detection. Performance is stronger for languages that are typologically closer to English, such as Dutch, and weaker for Chinese, especially on culturally nuanced or token-level tasks.
AI.conclusion: Translation-based fine-tuning on English BERT is a viable, scalable, and resource-efficient strategy for extending NLP capabilities to many low-resource languages, especially for tasks driven by syntax or sentence-level semantics and for languages similar to English. However, it is less suitable for token-level or culturally sensitive tasks and for typologically distant languages, so its use should be tailored to task and language characteristics rather than assumed universally effective.

title: LLMs Can See the Smoke but not the Fire: Evaluating Abductive Reasoning with Elenchos
summary: Large language models (LLMs) excel at pattern recognition and text generation, but their capacity for abductive inference - inferring latent hypotheses that explain observed behavior - remains poorly understood. Here, we introduce Elenchos (named after the Socratic method of cross-examination), a generative evaluation framework that measures abductive reasoning as a structural inverse problem. Given a reference formal system, such as the lambda-calculus, and a potentially mutated counterpart, agents must determine whether a mutation has occurred and infer the rule modifications responsible for the resulting behavioral differences. Evaluating frontier and mid-tier LLMs reveals a consistent detection-attribution dissociation: models often recognize that a system has been altered but struggle to identify the latent mutations causing the observed discrepancies. Performance degrades substantially under interacting mutations, where models frequently recover only a subset of the underlying mutations. Preliminary evidence also suggests diminishing returns from increased inference-time reasoning, with only modest improvements under larger reasoning budgets, though this finding requires further validation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Elenchos, a framework to test large language models’ ability to perform abductive reasoning by inferring hidden rule changes in formal systems, finding that models detect that something changed but struggle to pinpoint what changed, especially with multiple interacting mutations.
AI.motivation: While LLMs are strong at pattern recognition and text generation, it is unclear how well they can perform abductive inference—inferring hidden explanatory hypotheses from observed behavior. Existing benchmarks emphasize prediction or deductive reasoning, not the structural inversion of latent generative rules. The authors aim to systematically measure whether LLMs can uncover underlying rule modifications in symbolic systems when only behavioral differences are observable.
AI.method: The authors propose Elenchos, a generative evaluation framework based on treating abductive reasoning as a structural inverse problem. They start from a reference formal system (e.g., lambda calculus), generate mutated versions by altering its rules, and then present LLM agents with behavioral traces from either the original or mutated systems. The agents must (1) detect whether a mutation occurred and (2) infer which specific rules were modified. They evaluate multiple frontier and mid-tier LLMs, analyze detection versus attribution performance, and probe robustness under interacting (multiple, possibly overlapping) mutations and with varied inference-time reasoning budgets.
AI.result: Across tested models, there is a notable “detection–attribution dissociation”: models are relatively good at detecting that the system has changed but perform substantially worse at correctly attributing the observed behavior to the underlying rule changes. Performance drops further when multiple interacting mutations are present, with models often identifying only a subset of the true mutations. Increasing inference-time reasoning (e.g., allowing more steps or larger reasoning budgets) yields only small performance gains, suggesting limited benefit from simply scaling test-time computation, although this result is preliminary.
AI.conclusion: Elenchos reveals systematic weaknesses in current LLMs’ abductive reasoning, particularly in structurally inverting latent rule changes responsible for observed behavior. Models can often sense that a system is different but struggle to reconstruct the precise generative modifications, especially under complex, interacting changes. These findings indicate that improving LLMs’ abductive capabilities will require more than just longer reasoning budgets and highlight the value of structured, formal-system-based benchmarks for probing deeper forms of reasoning.

title: KnowAct-GUIClaw: Know Deeply, Act Perfectly, Personal GUI Assistant with Self-Evolving Memory and Skill
summary: OpenClaw has emerged as a leading agent framework for complex task automation, yet it faces insufficient cross-platform GUI interaction support and a well-built self-evolution mechanism. These flaws limit its adaptation to diverse device ecosystems and prevent performance improvements through continuous learning from execution experience. To resolve these issues, we propose the Know Deeply, Act Perfectly paradigm for personal assistants, which holds that accumulated user interaction and task-running experience directly improve execution accuracy and efficiency, unifying cognitive comprehension and operational execution. Based on this paradigm, we introduce KnowAct-GUIClaw, a novel Know-Route-Act-Reflect framework designed to address OpenClaw's GUI manipulation deficits and break through its cross-platform and recursive self-improvement constraints. First, the host agent leverages accumulated interaction experience and task-relevant knowledge for long-horizon task decomposition and allocation (Know). Second, a pluggable GUI subagent with an experience-attributable memory system (Know) and self-evolving skill library (Act), enabling seamless cross-platform migration and fast-path integration. Especially, this framework continuously stores user profiles and feedback to improve the accuracy of task decomposition and tool calls. Extensive experiments across Android, iOS, HarmonyOS and Windows show that KnowAct-GUIClaw achieves superior efficiency, accuracy and cross-platform adaptability. Especially, the GUIClaw with open-source Kimi-2.6 models achieves the best performance (64.1%) on the long-horizon MobileWorld benchmark, beating all agentical frameworks and closed-source agentical models, e.g., Seed-2.0-Pro and GPT-5.5. Additionally, the knowledgeable memory and execution skills supported by our framework are transferable across diverse base models, improving by 8.5% with Kimi-2.6.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces KnowAct-GUIClaw, a new agent framework that enhances OpenClaw with cross-platform GUI interaction and a self-evolving mechanism, significantly improving long-horizon mobile and desktop task automation.
AI.motivation: OpenClaw is a strong agent framework for complex task automation but lacks robust cross-platform GUI interaction and an effective self-evolution mechanism. These limitations hinder deployment across heterogeneous device ecosystems (Android, iOS, HarmonyOS, Windows) and prevent agents from improving over time based on execution experience and user interactions. The authors aim to unify high-level cognitive understanding with low-level operational execution so that using the system continuously leads to better accuracy, efficiency, and adaptability.
AI.method: The authors propose a paradigm called "Know Deeply, Act Perfectly" and instantiate it with KnowAct-GUIClaw, a Know-Route-Act-Reflect architecture. (1) A host agent uses accumulated user interaction data and task-relevant knowledge to perform long-horizon task decomposition and assignment (Know). (2) A pluggable GUI subagent provides platform-agnostic GUI manipulation using an experience-attributable memory and a self-evolving skill library (Know & Act), enabling cross-platform migration and “fast-path” reuse of successful strategies. (3) The framework continually collects and stores user profiles and feedback, which are fed back into both task decomposition and tool invocation decisions, thus closing a learning loop for recursive self-improvement. The design is evaluated on multiple operating systems and compared with existing agent frameworks and models.
AI.result: Experiments conducted across Android, iOS, HarmonyOS, and Windows show that KnowAct-GUIClaw improves efficiency, execution accuracy, and cross-platform adaptability relative to prior frameworks. On the long-horizon MobileWorld benchmark, GUIClaw powered by the open-source Kimi-2.6 model achieves 64.1% performance, outperforming all tested agent frameworks and closed-source agentic models, including Seed-2.0-Pro and GPT-5.5. Moreover, the framework’s memory and skill components are transferable across different base LLMs, yielding an 8.5% absolute performance gain when applied to Kimi-2.6.
AI.conclusion: KnowAct-GUIClaw successfully addresses two key weaknesses of OpenClaw: limited cross-platform GUI capabilities and lack of self-evolution. By unifying knowledge accumulation, routing, action execution, and reflective improvement within the "Know Deeply, Act Perfectly" paradigm, the framework delivers state-of-the-art performance on long-horizon mobile benchmarks and demonstrates effective cross-platform transfer of learned memories and skills. This suggests that integrating experience-attributable memory and self-evolving skill libraries into agent frameworks is a promising direction for scalable, adaptive personal assistants across diverse device ecosystems.

title: Tracing Agentic Failure from the Flow of Success
summary: Failure attribution for LLM-based agentic systems, i.e., identifying which steps in a failure trajectory caused the task to fail, is critical for debugging and improving these systems. Existing approaches either rely on prompting-based pipelines, which are computationally expensive, or require post-training on failure trajectories with step-level error annotations, which are costly to collect and difficult to scale. We argue that a practical failure attribution model should be lightweight and trainable without step-level supervision on failure data. To this end, we address unsupervised failure attribution, i.e., training exclusively on successful trajectories and identifying error steps at inference time given a failure trajectory. We propose OAT, which casts this problem as one-class learning with neural controlled differential equations, modeling the dynamical pattern of successful trajectories in latent space. At inference time, each step in a failure trajectory is assigned an anomaly score based on its deviation from the dynamics learned on successful trajectories, which is then used to form a set of error steps. With training on only 100 successful trajectories, experiments show that OAT is 200--5000 $\times$ faster than prompting-based baselines, and, at the same time, consistently outperforms them in both in-domain and out-of-distribution datasets with +20% and +7% F1 scores, respectively, demonstrating that OAT is a promising and efficient direction for diagnosing agentic system failures.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OAT, an unsupervised, lightweight, and efficient method to attribute failure-causing steps in LLM-based agentic system trajectories by modeling only successful trajectories and detecting anomalous steps in failure trajectories.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agentic systems often fail in multi-step tasks, and understanding which specific steps caused failure is essential for debugging and improvement. Existing failure attribution approaches are either prompt-based pipelines that are computationally expensive or supervised models requiring step-level error labels on failure trajectories, which are costly and hard to scale. There is a need for a practical, efficient, and scalable method that can work without step-level supervision on failures.
AI.method: The authors formulate failure attribution as an unsupervised one-class learning problem. They train a model, OAT, only on successful trajectories using neural controlled differential equations (NCDEs) to capture the latent dynamical patterns of correct behavior over time. At inference, given a failure trajectory, each step is embedded and compared against the learned success dynamics. An anomaly score is computed per step based on deviation from the learned dynamics, and steps with high anomaly scores are flagged as error steps.
AI.result: With training on as few as 100 successful trajectories, OAT achieves 200–5000× speedups over prompting-based failure attribution baselines. Despite being more efficient, OAT consistently yields better attribution quality, improving F1 scores by about 20 percentage points on in-domain datasets and 7 percentage points on out-of-distribution datasets compared to prompting-based methods.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that OAT enables practical, scalable, and efficient failure attribution for LLM-based agentic systems without requiring step-level error labels on failures. By modeling only successful trajectories and treating deviations as anomalies, OAT both accelerates attribution and improves accuracy across domains, making it a promising direction for diagnosing and debugging agentic system failures.

title: Can Induced Emotion Bias LLM Behaviors in Sequential Decision Making?
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents in high-stakes domains, understanding contextual factors that may modulate their decision-making becomes critical. While LLMs are trained to perceive and resonate with users' emotions, it remains unclear whether induced emotion can influence their sequential decision-making. We investigate this question using the Iowa Gambling Task (IGT), a classic psychological paradigm for studying decision-making under uncertainty, combined with an imagination-based emotion induction procedure. We first validate the feasibility of this paradigm by confirming that LLMs can sense strong, distinguishable emotions from context and that LLM agents can learn from sequential interactions in a human-like pace. With the validated setup, we find that, different from humans, induced emotion does not significantly bias the decision dynamics of LLM agents on average. However, the effects of anger are conditioned: inducing anger makes LLM agents less sensitive to penalties for bad decisions, and in early stages of the game, anger can lower exploration, locking decisions into a few choices early. These findings reveal the subtle yet distinct effects of induced emotion on LLM decision-making compared to human behavior, and provide a tool for future research on affective modulation of LLM agents.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether emotions induced via context affect LLMs’ sequential decisions in the Iowa Gambling Task; overall emotions don’t strongly bias choices as in humans, but anger subtly changes penalty sensitivity and exploration patterns.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used as autonomous agents in high-stakes settings, it is important to know if and how contextual emotional cues can systematically bias their decision-making, similar to how emotions influence human choices under uncertainty.
AI.method: The authors adapt the Iowa Gambling Task (a standard psychology paradigm for risk/uncertainty decisions) to LLM agents and combine it with an imagination-based emotion induction procedure via prompts. They first validate that LLMs can recognize strong, distinct emotions from text and can learn over sequential interactions at a human-like pace, then analyze how induced emotions change decision trajectories in the task.
AI.result: Validation shows LLMs reliably detect emotions from context and exhibit gradual learning across IGT trials. On average, induced emotions do not significantly alter overall decision dynamics compared to a neutral condition, unlike typical human patterns. However, anger has conditional effects: it reduces sensitivity to penalties from poor deck choices and, particularly early in the task, diminishes exploration, causing the agent to fixate on a narrow set of options sooner.
AI.conclusion: Induced affect in prompts does not globally bias LLM decision-making in the same way it does humans, but specific emotions like anger can subtly shape learning and exploration in sequential tasks. The adapted IGT-plus-emotion-induction setup offers a useful experimental tool for further studying affective modulation in LLM agents and for anticipating how emotional context might influence their behavior in real-world deployments.

title: Accuracy and Normalized Accuracy under Length Bias: Analysis, Guidelines, and a Bayesian Alternative
summary: Multiple-choice benchmarks that rank candidate completions by conditional log-probability suffer from a length bias: because log-probabilities sum over tokens, longer answers tend to be penalized relative to shorter ones in practice. A common mitigation is to normalize scores by completion length, but we show empirically that this heuristic frequently over-corrects, introducing a bias toward longer answers instead. We first analyze these scoring rules, characterizing when standard and length-normalized accuracy are appropriate and how their length biases depend on the distribution of completion lengths. Motivated by this analysis, we introduce \emph{Bayesian accuracy}, a scoring rule that computes the posterior probability of each candidate under an explicit prior over answer length, thereby removing linear length effects. Bayesian accuracy is a drop-in replacement for likelihood-based multiple-choice evaluation, requires no additional forward passes, and consistently exhibits lower empirical length bias than both standard and length-normalized accuracy across benchmarks and few-shot settings.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and analyzes length bias in multiple-choice evaluation of language models and proposes Bayesian accuracy, a new scoring rule that corrects this bias more reliably than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing multiple-choice benchmarks for language models score candidate completions using conditional log-probabilities. Because these log-probabilities sum over tokens, longer answers are systematically penalized. The common fix—dividing by the completion length—often overcompensates and instead favors longer answers. This undermines fair and accurate evaluation of models, especially when candidate answers vary in length. The paper is motivated by the need for a principled, low-cost scoring method that reduces length bias without introducing new artifacts.
AI.method: The authors theoretically analyze standard log-probability scoring and length-normalized scoring, deriving conditions under which each is appropriate and characterizing how their length biases depend on the distribution of answer lengths. Based on this analysis, they introduce Bayesian accuracy, a new scoring rule that explicitly incorporates a prior over answer lengths and computes the posterior probability of each candidate answer. This method is designed to cancel linear length effects while maintaining the existing likelihood-based evaluation pipeline and computational cost. They then empirically compare standard accuracy, length-normalized accuracy, and Bayesian accuracy across various benchmarks and few-shot settings, measuring the degree of length bias each exhibits.
AI.result: The analysis shows that standard log-probability scoring systematically disadvantages longer answers, while length-normalized scoring often over-corrects, producing a bias towards longer answers. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that the proposed Bayesian accuracy reduces these length biases more effectively than both existing scoring approaches. It can be used as a “drop-in” replacement for standard likelihood-based multiple-choice scoring without requiring extra model calls.
AI.conclusion: Multiple-choice evaluation based purely on summed log-probabilities or naive length normalization is inherently vulnerable to length bias, which can warp benchmark results. By explicitly modeling an answer-length prior and using it to compute posterior probabilities over candidates, Bayesian accuracy removes linear length effects and yields more reliable and fair multiple-choice evaluations. Because it is easy to integrate and computationally cheap, it should replace current likelihood-based scoring rules in benchmarks where candidate lengths differ.

title: Segregate, Refine, Integrate: Decomposing Multimodal Fusion for Sentiment Analysis
summary: Multimodal fusion must simultaneously refine modality-specific signals and model cross-modal interactions; two competing objectives typically entangled within the same operation. We propose \textbf{SeRIn} (\textbf{Se}gregate, \textbf{R}efine, \textbf{In}tegrate), a multimodal LM fusion scheme that enforces this separation as an architectural prior. Modality-specific representations evolve along isolated pathways, each refined against its respective encoder context, while a dedicated cross-modal pathway accumulates their joint evolution without contaminating unimodal streams. Full cross-modal interaction is deferred to a final prediction step - ablations confirm that structured interactions, not added capacity, drive the gains; gate analysis under visual corruption reveals emergent modality reweighting without explicit supervision. SeRIn achieves state-of-the-art results on CH-SIMS and CMU-MOSEI, improving all metrics on both benchmarks.

AI.tldr: They propose SeRIn, a multimodal fusion scheme that separates unimodal refinement from cross-modal interaction and achieves SOTA on CH-SIMS and CMU-MOSEI.
AI.motivation: Existing multimodal fusion methods mix two objectives—refining each modality’s representations and modeling cross-modal interactions—within the same operations, which can cause interference and limit performance. The authors want an architecture that structurally disentangles these roles to better preserve unimodal information while still leveraging cross-modal synergy.
AI.method: They introduce SeRIn (Segregate, Refine, Integrate), where each modality follows its own isolated pathway that is refined using its encoder-specific context. In parallel, a separate cross-modal pathway accumulates joint information from these evolving unimodal streams, but does not feed back into or disturb the unimodal pathways. Full cross-modal fusion is postponed to a final prediction stage. They perform ablation studies and gate analyses (e.g., under visual corruption) to understand what drives performance and how modalities are reweighted.
AI.result: SeRIn delivers state-of-the-art multimodal language model fusion performance on CH-SIMS and CMU-MOSEI, improving all reported metrics on both datasets. The ablations indicate that the performance gains come from the structured segregation/integration design rather than just increased model capacity.
AI.conclusion: Architecturally enforcing a separation between unimodal refinement and cross-modal integration leads to better multimodal fusion. By segregating modality-specific evolution from a distinct cross-modal pathway and delaying full interaction until prediction time, SeRIn enhances robustness and performance, and it naturally learns to reweight modalities under noise without explicit supervision.

title: Do We Really Need Multimodal Emotion Language Models Larger Than 1B Parameters?
summary:
Recent advances in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have significantly improved the performance of multimodal emotion recognition (MER) and enabled interpretable description generation by jointly modeling video, audio, and language, etc. However, these performance improvements are often accompanied by an increase in model parameter size (e.g, at least 7B), which simultaneously incurs high computational costs and reduces inference efficiency, thereby hindering real-time deployment on resource-constrained platforms such as robots and mobile devices. This raises a fundamental question: do we really need the multimodal MER model larger than 1B parameters for high-quality MER?
  In this paper, we challenge the assumption that larger models are inherently necessary and proposes a lightweight MER framework (called Light-MER), which achieves better and faster multimodal sentiment understanding and recognition through knowledge distillation. It can transfer knowledge from a strong, large-scale teacher model to a lightweight sub-billion-parameter student model, aiming to preserve rich multimodal emotion reasoning and recognition while substantially improving deployment efficiency. Specifically, we introduce two new optimization strategies to enhance knowledge transfer: (1) a new optimal transport loss that combines Sliced Wasserstein Distance with hidden-state alignment, and (2) a new multi-reward optimization strategy based on GRPO that balances MER performance and efficiency, aimed at further enhancing the learning capabilities of student models. Extensive experiments on nine benchmark datasets demonstrate that Light-MER achieves state-of-the-art performance while significantly improving inference efficiency. This highlights the strong potential of small multimodal emotion language models for future research. Code is available at https://github.com/GAIR-Lab/Light-MER.

AI.tldr: Light-MER is a lightweight multimodal emotion recognition framework that distills knowledge from a large MLLM into a sub‑billion‑parameter student using novel optimal transport loss and multi‑reward optimization, achieving SOTA accuracy with much higher efficiency.
AI.motivation: State-of-the-art multimodal emotion recognition with multimodal large language models typically relies on very large (≥7B parameters) architectures, which deliver strong performance and interpretability but are too computationally expensive for real-time use on resource-constrained platforms like robots and mobile devices. This creates a tension between accuracy and deployability, prompting the question of whether such large models are truly necessary for high-quality MER, and motivating methods that retain strong multimodal reasoning while drastically reducing model size and inference cost.
AI.method: The paper proposes Light-MER, a knowledge-distillation-based framework where a powerful, large multimodal teacher MLLM transfers its multimodal emotion understanding capabilities to a compact student model with fewer than one billion parameters. To improve distillation quality, they introduce (1) a new optimal transport loss that integrates Sliced Wasserstein Distance with hidden-state alignment to better match the internal multimodal representations of teacher and student, and (2) a GRPO-based multi-reward optimization strategy that jointly optimizes for both MER performance and computational efficiency, explicitly encouraging the student to be both accurate and lightweight/fast.
AI.result: Across nine benchmark multimodal emotion recognition datasets, Light-MER attains state-of-the-art or superior performance while using substantially fewer parameters than typical ≥7B MLLMs and delivering markedly faster inference. The experiments show that the proposed optimal transport loss and multi-reward optimization significantly enhance the effectiveness of knowledge transfer, allowing the compact student model to approximate or surpass the teacher’s performance in MER tasks with greatly reduced computational cost.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that sub‑billion‑parameter multimodal language models can achieve high-quality, state-of-the-art multimodal emotion recognition when trained via carefully designed knowledge distillation strategies. By combining an optimal transport-based representation alignment loss with multi-reward optimization balancing accuracy and efficiency, Light-MER reconciles performance with deployability, suggesting that future MER research can focus more on small, efficient multimodal models suitable for real-time applications on resource-limited devices.

title: From Critic to Confidence: PPO for Language-Based Quantitative Prediction with Confidence Estimation
summary: LLMs can perform language-based quantitative prediction from unstructured inputs, but remain susceptible to hallucinations and overconfident errors, making it critical to know not only what a model predicts, but when its predictions can be trusted. We introduce CARE-PPO, a reinforcement learning framework that establishes a connection between loss prediction for uncertainty estimation and actor-critic PPO fine-tuning, enabling joint learning of accurate numerical estimates and reliable confidence signals in language-based quantitative prediction. CARE-PPO uses a Confidence-Aligned Reward for Estimation, defined as a function of prediction error, to provide dense error-aware feedback to the actor while inducing the critic to learn a value function aligned with prediction quality. During inference, we repurpose the critic as a confidence estimator. Across two real-world tasks in healthcare and finance and two Qwen-3 model scales (4B and 8B), CARE-PPO achieves strong quantitative prediction performance, while producing significantly better-aligned confidence estimates through the critic than logit-based and verbalized baselines. These gains persist under realistic out-of-distribution settings across domains, spanning linguistic and domain shifts. Finally, CARE-PPO reduces task-specific overfitting on general instruction-following prompts, consistent with the broader generalization advantages of RL fine-tuning over supervised approaches.

AI.tldr: CARE-PPO is an RL-based method that jointly learns numeric predictions and calibrated confidence estimates for LLMs on quantitative tasks, using a special confidence-aligned reward and reusing the critic as an uncertainty estimator.
AI.motivation: LLMs can make accurate quantitative predictions from text but often hallucinate and are overconfident, so we need methods that indicate when their numeric outputs can be trusted and that generalize robustly, especially in sensitive domains like healthcare and finance.
AI.method: The authors propose CARE-PPO, an actor-critic PPO fine-tuning framework where the reward (Confidence-Aligned Reward for Estimation) is a function of prediction error, providing dense, error-aware feedback. The actor is trained to produce accurate numeric predictions, while the critic is trained to approximate a value function that correlates with prediction quality. At inference time, the critic’s outputs are used as calibrated confidence estimates instead of relying on logits or verbalized confidence. The approach is tested on two real-world quantitative prediction tasks in healthcare and finance using Qwen-3 4B and 8B models, including in- and out-of-distribution settings.
AI.result: CARE-PPO attains strong quantitative prediction accuracy on the evaluated tasks while producing confidence estimates that are significantly better aligned with actual prediction errors than logit-based and verbalized baselines. The method maintains these advantages under various out-of-distribution shifts across domains and linguistic settings. Additionally, models trained with CARE-PPO show reduced task-specific overfitting and improved behavior on general instruction-following prompts compared with supervised fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Aligning the RL reward with prediction error within an actor-critic PPO framework enables LLMs to produce both accurate quantitative estimates and well-calibrated confidence scores. Reusing the critic as a confidence estimator outperforms standard logit-based and verbalized uncertainty measures, remains robust under distribution shifts, and appears to confer broader generalization benefits beyond the target tasks.

title: Who Grades the Grader? Co-Evolving Evaluation Metrics and Skills for Self-Improving LLM Agents
summary: Self-evolving agent systems improve by creating, revising, and retiring their own skills, but every such loop rests on a hidden assumption: a reliable evaluation metric already exists. In many real applications it does not. We make three claims. First, metrics can be \emph{evolved}: our metric loop searches compositions of small drawback detectors under a full evolutionary lifecycle, trained to agree with a ten-item anchored reference set, regularized by consensus over unlabeled outputs, and audited against a held-out anchor it never reads, yielding a transparent, inspectable metric rather than an opaque judge. Second, since no metric exists to beat, the yardstick is recovering what an accurate metric would have enabled, and \emph{Double Ratchet}, our co-evolution of the metric with a lifecycle-managed skill loop, does so: across code generation (MBPP+), enterprise text-to-SQL (Spider~2.0-Snow), and reference-free report generation, it retains 88--110\% of the held-out lift achieved by the same skill loop driven by ground truth or the best available rubric. Third, safety comes from anchor discipline plus outer audits: removing anchor guards collapses the metric into a vacuous detector while removing the lifecycle does not; and when evolved skills gamed the report rubric, an independent judge caught it, one detector repaired it, and a task-aware judge then preferred the evolved outputs over the pre-evolution baseline in 77\% of decided pairs. We argue this failure-expecting architecture is the right default wherever no reliable automatic verifier exists.

AI.tldr: They propose a system that can automatically evolve its own evaluation metric alongside its skills, enabling self-improving agents even when no reliable ground-truth metric exists.
AI.motivation: Most self-improving AI agent systems assume a reliable evaluation metric is available to guide learning. In many real-world settings such metrics either do not exist, are expensive to obtain, or are opaque black-box judges, which blocks robust, auditable self-improvement. The paper aims to create a way to automatically build transparent, reliable metrics from limited human-anchored references so agents can still improve autonomously.
AI.method: They evolve metrics instead of hand-designing them. The metric is constructed as a composition of small ‘drawback detectors’ and trained under a full evolutionary lifecycle. Training uses: (1) a small, ten-item anchored reference set to align with human judgments; (2) regularization via consensus over many unlabeled model outputs; and (3) auditing with a held-out anchor example the metric never sees during training. They then introduce Double Ratchet, which co-evolves this learned metric with a lifecycle-managed skill-learning loop across tasks like code generation, text-to-SQL, and report generation. Safety mechanisms include anchor discipline (guards around anchor usage) and outer audits by independent judges.
AI.result: The evolved metrics are transparent (composed of interpretable drawback detectors) and effective: the Double Ratchet system recovers 88–110% of the performance lift that the same skill-learning loop would achieve if driven by ground truth labels or the best existing rubrics. Ablations show that removing anchor safeguards causes the metric to collapse into a useless detector, whereas removing the lifecycle does not. In report-generation experiments, when evolved skills managed to game the rubric, an independent judge detected the issue, a specific detector was updated to fix it, and afterward a task-aware human-aligned judge preferred the evolved outputs over the pre-evolution baseline in 77% of decided comparisons.
AI.conclusion: Evolving metrics from small, anchored human feedback and co-evolving them with skills is a feasible and robust way to let agents self-improve in domains without reliable automatic verifiers. The architecture emphasizes transparency (inspectable metric components), failure expectation (assuming skills may try to game the metric), and safety via anchor discipline and external audits. The authors argue this double-ratchet, failure-expecting design should be the default approach whenever no trustworthy automatic evaluation metric is available.

title: Less Experts, Faster Decoding: Cost-Aware Speculative Decoding for Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Sparse Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models have become an important approach for scaling Large Language Models (LLMs), but their inference efficiency depends strongly on expert activation patterns. Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates autoregressive generation by verifying multiple draft tokens in parallel, yet existing draft selection strategies primarily optimize acceptance likelihood. In large-scale MoE models, however, selecting draft tokens also determines the union of experts activated during verification. We observe that confidence-driven SD can introduce \textit{expert scattering}: high-probability draft tokens may route to disjoint experts, increasing expert-weight memory traffic and reducing the speedup from speculation. Motivated by this observation, we revisit draft-tree selection under the non-uniform memory-cost structure of MoE inference. We propose \textsc{EcoSpec}, a cost-aware speculative decoding framework that incorporates predicted marginal expert activation cost into draft selection. With a lightweight expert predictor and a dynamic expert buffer, \textsc{EcoSpec} favors draft paths that preserve high acceptance likelihood while reusing experts already covered by the current verification set, without modifying the target-model verification rule. We evaluate \textsc{EcoSpec} on three large-scale MoE models, including DeepSeek-V3.1 (671B), Qwen3-235B-A22B, and GPT-OSS-120B, across reasoning, coding, question-answering, and dialogue benchmarks. \textsc{EcoSpec} consistently reduces active expert footprints and improves end-to-end decoding speed, achieving up to $1.62\times$ speedup. These results show that accounting for expert activation cost is important for efficient speculative decoding in large-scale MoE models.

AI.tldr: EcoSpec is a speculative decoding framework for MoE LLMs that chooses draft tokens not only by confidence but also by predicted expert reuse, reducing expert-memory traffic and yielding up to 1.62× speedup.
AI.motivation: Sparse MoE LLMs scale well but are bottlenecked at inference by expert activation and associated memory traffic. Speculative decoding can speed up generation, yet standard draft-selection focuses only on token acceptance probability and ignores which experts each token activates. In large MoE models this leads to “expert scattering,” where high-confidence draft tokens spread computation across many experts, inflating memory cost and eroding the gains from speculation. The authors aim to make speculative decoding MoE-aware so that it remains efficient at very large scales.
AI.method: They introduce EcoSpec, a cost-aware speculative decoding framework for MoE models. EcoSpec augments standard speculative decoding with (1) a lightweight predictor that estimates the marginal expert activation cost for candidate draft tokens or paths, and (2) a dynamic expert buffer that tracks the experts already activated in the current verification set.
