During draft-tree construction, EcoSpec scores candidate paths by combining acceptance likelihood with predicted expert-cost, preferentially selecting sequences that reuse already-active experts while still maintaining high acceptance likelihood. The underlying target model and its verification rule are unchanged; only the draft token selection policy is modified to be expert-cost aware.
AI.result: On three large-scale MoE LLMs—DeepSeek-V3.1 (671B), Qwen3-235B-A22B, and GPT-OSS-120B—across reasoning, coding, QA, and dialogue benchmarks, EcoSpec consistently reduces the number of distinct experts activated (“expert footprint”) during speculative decoding. This reduction in expert scattering translates into better hardware utilization and end-to-end decoding speedups, with maximum reported gains of up to 1.62× over confidence-driven speculative decoding baselines.
AI.conclusion: Expert activation patterns are a key hidden cost in speculative decoding for MoE LLMs. Optimizing draft token selection purely for acceptance likelihood is suboptimal because it can cause expert scattering and excessive memory traffic. By integrating a simple expert-cost predictor and expert reuse–aware selection into the draft-tree policy, EcoSpec achieves more compact expert footprints and measurable speedups without changing the verification rule or model architecture. The work demonstrates that making speculative decoding expert-cost-aware is important for efficient inference in large MoE models.

title: Visual Access Boundaries in Vision-Language Model Reasoning
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting is widely used as a test-time scaling strategy for Vision-Language Models (VLMs), but it remains unclear what is extended when VLMs generate longer reasoning traces. We ask whether CoT requires continued access to image tokens, or whether it mainly operates over visual information already made available earlier in the forward pass. We introduce Visual Access Sweep, a causal intervention that masks attention from generated-token queries to image-token keys along layer depth and generation time, and define the Visual Access Boundary (VAB) as the minimal access region that preserves task accuracy. Across six model configurations from Qwen2.5-VL and InternVL3, both no-CoT direct answering and CoT prompting exhibit finite VABs. In Qwen2.5-VL-32B and InternVL3 at 14B and 38B scales, when CoT is evaluated against the no-CoT full-access target, its VAB layer differs from the no-CoT boundary by at most two layers, despite substantially longer generations. This suggests that CoT does not primarily improve performance by prolonging direct image-token access throughout the reasoning trace, but by extending language-side computation over image-derived hidden-state information. We further show that CoT gains are constrained by perceptual readout. CoT helps when the queried visual attribute can be reliably read out by the model, but not when that readout is unreliable. A symbolic-attribute oracle shows that CoT can improve counting once ground-truth attributes are supplied as text, while a single-object probe-vs-decode check shows that hard attributes can be linearly recoverable from hidden states yet difficult for the model itself to output. Together, these analyses place the bottleneck at readout rather than counting.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting improves Vision-Language Model (VLM) performance, showing that CoT mainly extends language-side computation over already-extracted visual information rather than requiring prolonged access to image tokens, and that performance is bottlenecked by perceptual readout rather than reasoning depth (e.g., counting).
AI.motivation: CoT prompting is widely used to boost VLM performance, but it is unknown whether its benefits come from extended interaction with visual features during long reasoning traces or from processing information already extracted earlier. Understanding this mechanism can guide better model design, interpretability, and use of CoT in vision-language tasks, especially for abilities like counting and attribute reasoning.
AI.method: The authors propose Visual Access Sweep, a causal intervention that selectively masks attention from generated-token queries to image-token keys across transformer layers and decoding steps. From this, they define the Visual Access Boundary (VAB) as the minimal window of image-token access that preserves accuracy. They apply this to multiple Qwen2.5-VL and InternVL3 configurations under both direct-answer (no-CoT) and CoT prompting. They then compare VABs between settings and run further analyses: (1) a symbolic-attribute oracle that injects ground-truth symbolic attributes as text to test CoT’s effect on counting in the absence of perception errors, and (2) a probe-vs-decode experiment that checks whether visual attributes are linearly recoverable from hidden states even when the model fails to output them correctly.
AI.result: Both direct answering and CoT prompting exhibit finite VABs, meaning only early-layer, early-time access to image tokens is needed; extended, late access is not required. For large Qwen2.5-VL-32B and InternVL3-14B/38B models, the VABs for CoT versus no-CoT differ by at most about two layers, despite CoT involving much longer text generation. This implies that CoT does not rely on continuous visual access during reasoning. Additional experiments show that CoT only yields benefits when the visual attribute can already be reliably read out; when readout is unreliable, CoT offers little or no gain. With oracle symbolic attributes, CoT can significantly improve counting, indicating that counting itself is not the main bottleneck. Probe-vs-decode reveals that some difficult attributes are linearly present in hidden states but still not correctly output by the model.
AI.conclusion: CoT prompting in VLMs primarily enhances performance by extending language-side reasoning over fixed, early-extracted visual representations rather than by maintaining long-term direct access to image tokens. The limiting factor for tasks like counting and attribute-based queries is perceptual readout—how well visual attributes are extracted and expressed—rather than the depth or length of the reasoning trace. Improving visual readout mechanisms may therefore yield larger gains than simply scaling CoT prompting, and analyses based on Visual Access Boundary provide a principled way to study how and when VLMs use visual information.

title: Epistemic Stance Flexibility Probing: Measuring Prompt-Conditioned Register Shift in Large Language Models
summary: A language model may be asked either what experts believe about a contested claim or what it believes about the claim itself. A trustworthy conversational agent should distinguish these two requests and respond in different epistemic registers: neutral attribution in the first case and stance expression in the second. Whether such a shift occurs-and whether it occurs coherently-is not directly assessed by existing benchmarks for accuracy, instruction following, or safety. We introduce ESFP, a behavioral benchmark that treats the contrast between externally attributed and self-attributed prompts as the fundamental unit of measurement. ESFP consists of 104 carefully controlled items spanning six epistemic categories and five phrasing templates, and evaluates model responses along four complementary dimensions: lexical self-attribution, representation-level responsiveness to role framing, sentence-level stance content density assessed by an LLM judge panel, and cross-condition stance consistency. Evaluating eight frontier models from five vendors, we find that epistemic flexibility is largely orthogonal to general model capability: a 27B open-weight model matches the strongest proprietary systems, the flagship model of one family underperforms its lightweight counterpart, and reasoning-optimized models do not consistently exhibit higher flexibility. Stance content density provides the strongest signal, while surface-level lexical markers such as 'I think' can change substantially without corresponding changes in expressed stance. We provide item-level bootstrap confidence intervals, weight-sensitivity analyses, and an explicit discussion of the interpretation limits of the composite score. ESFP measures a model's propensity to adapt its epistemic stance under changing attribution conditions, rather than a general competence measure.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ESFP, a benchmark to test whether language models change how they express beliefs when asked about expert views versus their own stance, showing this “epistemic flexibility” is largely independent of general model capability.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks focus on accuracy, instruction-following, and safety, but do not directly test whether language models appropriately shift between neutrally reporting others’ beliefs and expressing their own stance on contested claims. This gap matters for trustworthy conversational agents that must respond differently when asked what experts think versus what the model itself believes.
AI.method: The authors design ESFP, a behavioral benchmark with 104 controlled items across six epistemic categories and five phrasing templates, contrasting externally attributed prompts (what experts believe) with self-attributed prompts (what the model believes). They evaluate models along four dimensions: (1) lexical self-attribution (surface markers like “I think”), (2) representation-level responsiveness to role framing, (3) sentence-level stance content density judged by an LLM panel, and (4) cross-condition stance consistency. They test eight frontier models from five vendors and conduct bootstrap confidence interval estimation, weight-sensitivity analyses, and analyze the limits of a composite score.
AI.result: The evaluation shows that epistemic flexibility—how models adapt their stance expression under different attribution conditions—is largely orthogonal to general capability: a 27B open-weight model performs on par with top proprietary models, one vendor’s flagship model scores worse than its smaller counterpart, and reasoning-optimized models do not reliably show higher flexibility. Among metrics, stance content density is the most informative, while lexical markers (e.g., using “I think”) can vary substantially without actual changes in stance, indicating that superficial cues are unreliable indicators of epistemic adaptation.
AI.conclusion: ESFP effectively measures a model’s tendency to adapt its epistemic stance when prompts shift between external attribution and self-attribution, rather than serving as a general competence metric. The benchmark reveals that epistemic flexibility is not simply a function of scale or reasoning optimization, and it highlights that deeper stance content metrics are more meaningful than surface lexical markers, while also clarifying the interpretational limits of any composite score derived from the benchmark.

title: Human-AI Agent Interaction as a Neuroplastic Training Environment
summary: Interaction with AI agents has become one of the most frequent activities of everyday digital life. Whether conversing with an assistant, working with a coding copilot, or generating images, the interaction follows a common iterative loop: a request is issued, a result returned, appraised, and the request revised. We observe that this loop is a high-frequency stream of contact events -- moments at which a result meets a person and a conditioned response may fire before deliberate appraisal -- making everyday agent interaction an unrecognised neuroplastic training environment. When a result disappoints, reactive patterns of impatience, perfectionism, frustration, and self-criticism are repeatedly evoked, and under activity-dependent synaptic plasticity each uninterrupted cycle deepens the underlying pathway through long-term potentiation. Ordinary agent use may thus quietly strengthen the very patterns it provokes. We propose that the same training environment can be engaged to the opposite effect. Treating conditioned reactive patterns as physical neurone paths -- activated through a pre-cognitive feeling tone that opens a brief regulatory gap -- we develop a framework in which, at that gap, in place of the reactive re-prompt, a person performs behind-the-scenes observation: watching the neural process operate so the cascade does not complete and long-term depression weakens the path rather than potentiation strengthening it. We characterise this practice through three layers of observation and two modes of application: a user-guided mode requiring no change to existing tools, and an agent-assisted mode in which an ordinary agent is lightly configured to support observation at the gap. We illustrate the framework through generative image prompting, showing how a single frustrating session is behaviourally nearly identical whether or not it is observed, yet neurologically opposite.

AI.tldr: AI-agent interactions form a repetitive feedback loop (prompt → result → appraisal → revised prompt) that unintentionally trains our neural responses. The authors argue this loop can either reinforce negative reactivity (impatience, frustration, self-criticism) or, if used mindfully, weaken these patterns through targeted observation during a brief regulatory window before we react.
AI.motivation: Everyday use of AI agents is now extremely frequent, but its psychological and neuroplastic impact is underexplored. The authors notice that rapid prompt–response cycles often trigger automatic emotional reactions (e.g., irritation at bad outputs). Under known principles of activity-dependent synaptic plasticity, repetition of such reactions can physically strengthen unhelpful mental habits. They want to (1) highlight AI interaction as a powerful, yet unrecognised, training environment for the brain and (2) design a method to turn this from a source of negative conditioning into an opportunity for positive deconditioning and self-regulation practice.
AI.method: Conceptual and framework-building: The authors model reactive responses to AI outputs as conditioned neural pathways activated by a pre-cognitive “feeling tone” that opens a short regulatory gap before full reaction. They then design a practice that uses this gap for deliberate, non-reactive observation instead of immediate re-prompting. The framework includes three layers of observation (likely increasing depth or granularity of self-monitoring) and two application modes: (a) user-guided, which works with any existing AI system without modification, and (b) agent-assisted, where the AI is lightly configured to support and scaffold observation during the gap. They demonstrate the idea using a generative image-prompting scenario, comparing an ordinary frustrating session with an “observed” session.
AI.result: Analytically, they show that from the outside, a frustrating interaction session with an AI looks almost the same whether or not the user is practicing observation: the same sequence of prompts and disappointing results can occur. However, under their neuroplastic framing, the internal neural effect is inverted. In the unobserved case, repeated unbroken reactivity leads to long-term potentiation of negative response patterns. In the observed case, the user interrupts the cascade, which should instead promote long-term depression of those pathways, gradually weakening the conditioned reactions. They thus demonstrate that AI-agent interaction can be repurposed as a micro-training ground for emotional regulation and habit change.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that routine interactions with AI agents are not neutral: they act as dense streams of neuroplastic training trials that may reinforce maladaptive emotional reactions if left unattended. By inserting mindful observation into the brief gap between initial affective tone and overt reaction, users can convert the same environment into a practice for weakening unhelpful neural patterns. The proposed framework, with its layered observation and two modes of deployment, suggests that both individuals and AI tool designers can harness everyday AI use as an accessible, high-frequency context for cultivating healthier cognitive-emotional habits rather than exacerbating reactivity.

title: The One-Word Census: Answer-Choice Conformity Across 44 Language Models
comment: 19 pages, 5 figures. Data, prompts, and code: https://github.com/tap2k/modelun
summary: When a language model must pick one answer from a large space of equally valid options, which does it pick -- and how often is it the same answer every other model picks? Asked to "pick a word -- any word," 44 models chose "serendipity" 41% of the time. We characterize this convergence with a deliberately minimal instrument: 31 single-turn prompts, each naming a category with many valid one-word answers ("Name a tree."), asked four times per model with no system prompt. Analysis is exact-match on normalized tokens -- no embeddings, no judge -- at about a dollar per model. That models converge is well documented; our contribution is the instrument itself -- the One-Word Census -- and what it reveals about the structure of the convergence. We score each model by answer-choice surprisal: the average $-\log2$ probability of its answers under the pooled answers of all other models, leave-one-out. Convergence is extreme -- in 7 of 31 categories one answer takes over 80% of all answers -- yet conformity varies more than fourfold across models, and the variation is structured. Persona- and community-tuned models are the most divergent; the newest mainline flagships are the most conformist, producing almost no answer no other model gave. Within four lineages (Claude, GPT, Qwen, Grok) conformity rises with each generation -- but reverses for the latest flagship Claude and GPT models, a possible early signal of repositioning at the top tier. Rankings are robust to roster composition (leave-one-family-out rho = 0.985). Against human category-production norms, the field is more concentrated than people in 18 of 20 shared categories. All prompts, transcripts, and code are public.

AI.tldr: Paper introduces a minimal benchmark, the One-Word Census, to measure how strongly different language models converge on the same single-word answers when many are valid.
AI.motivation: To understand and quantify how and when language models converge on the same outputs in open-ended settings with many equally valid responses, and to compare convergence patterns across model families, generations, and against human norms.
AI.method: They design 31 single-turn prompts, each asking for a single word from a broad category (e.g., “Name a tree”), and query each model four times without a system prompt. Across 44 models, they collect responses and analyze exact token matches after normalization. They define a surprisal-based conformity score: for each model, compute the average negative log2 probability of its answers under the answer distribution pooled from all other models (leave-one-out). They then compare answer concentration, conformity scores, lineage trends (Claude, GPT, Qwen, Grok), robustness to leaving out model families, and contrast with human category-production norms.
AI.result: Strong convergence: in 7 of 31 categories, one answer accounts for over 80% of all model responses (e.g., “serendipity” for “pick a word — any word”). Conformity varies more than 4x across models, with persona- and community-tuned models being most divergent and top flagship models most conformist, rarely producing unique answers. Within Claude, GPT, Qwen, and Grok lineages, conformity generally increases with newer generations, but the very latest Claude and GPT flagships show a decrease, suggesting possible strategic differentiation. Rankings of model conformity are highly robust to roster changes (leave-one-family-out Spearman rho = 0.985). Compared to human norms, model outputs are more concentrated in 18 of 20 overlapping categories. All data and code are released.
AI.conclusion: A simple, cheap “One-Word Census” instrument reveals that large language models exhibit extreme and structured convergence when many valid answers exist, with clear differences across model types, generations, and relative to humans. Conformity is not uniform: it correlates with model positioning (flagship vs tuned personas) and evolves over generations. This method offers a practical diagnostic for studying and tracking homogeneity and diversity in model outputs over time.

title: Solution of the Hempel's statistical ambiguity problem and Causal AI
summary: This paper addresses Carl Hempel's longstanding problem of statistical ambiguity in inductive-statistical inference, in which contradictory predictions are derived from statistical laws. To avoid such predictions, Carl Hempel proposed the Requirement of Maximal Specificity (RMS) for the statistical laws used in the inference. An analysis of the RMS refinements made by Wesley Salmon, Alberto Coffa, and James Fetzer led to the following definition of maximally specific statistical laws: "the lawlike premises of an adequate explanation must specify all and only those properties whose presence or absence made a difference to the occurrence of its explanandum-phenomenon." However, there was no proof of a solution to the statistical ambiguity problem based on this definition. We use Nancy Cartwright's definition of causes that raise probabilities across background contexts, and then introduce the concept of Causal Rules. Then we define a special semantic probabilistic inference procedure that incrementally refines these causal rules by incorporating all statistically relevant information. This procedure yields Maximally Specific Causal Relationships (MSCRs), for which we prove (Theorem 1) that predictions derived from them are consistent. This resolves the statistical ambiguity problem. The semantic probabilistic inference procedure provides a probabilistic causal learning system, which may be used in such new areas as Causal AI and Causal Machine Learning. They fundamentally explore causal inference as a tool for understanding cause-and-effect relationships within complex systems. Properties similar to RMS remain under discussion. Several notions related to RMS are considered: invariant feature learning, invariant causal prediction, and spurious association.

AI.tldr: The paper solves Hempel’s problem of statistical ambiguity in inductive-statistical explanations by replacing the Requirement of Maximal Specificity with a rigorously defined, causal, probabilistic inference procedure that guarantees consistent predictions.
AI.motivation: Inductive-statistical (I-S) explanations can yield contradictory predictions when different statistical laws apply to the same case. Hempel’s Requirement of Maximal Specificity (RMS) was meant to prevent this, and later refinements (by Salmon, Coffa, Fetzer) gave a qualitative definition of maximally specific statistical laws. However, there was no formal method or proof that RMS, so understood, actually resolves the ambiguity and guarantees consistency. The paper aims to give a formally precise, causal, and probabilistic framework that both captures the spirit of RMS and demonstrably solves the statistical ambiguity problem.
AI.method: The authors adopt Nancy Cartwright’s account of causes as factors that raise the probability of an effect across background contexts. Building on this, they introduce ‘Causal Rules’ as structured representations linking causes, background conditions, and probabilistic effects. They then define a semantic probabilistic inference procedure that incrementally refines these causal rules by incorporating all statistically relevant information. Through this refinement process, they obtain what they call Maximally Specific Causal Relationships (MSCRs). Within this framework, they formally prove (Theorem 1) that predictions derived from MSCRs are mutually consistent, thereby eliminating statistical ambiguity. The method is positioned as a general probabilistic causal learning system that can be algorithmically implemented.
AI.result: The main technical result is a theorem (Theorem 1) showing that predictions based on Maximally Specific Causal Relationships, obtained via the proposed semantic probabilistic inference procedure, are consistent: they do not yield contradictory probabilistic predictions for the same explanandum. Conceptually, this is presented as a resolution of Hempel’s statistical ambiguity problem. The framework also yields a constructive interpretation of a maximally specific law—now recast as a maximally specific causal relationship under Cartwright’s probabilistic causality. Furthermore, the paper connects this framework to ongoing discussions in Causal AI and Causal Machine Learning, and relates its notion of maximal specificity to ideas such as invariant feature learning, invariant causal prediction, and the identification of spurious associations.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that Hempel’s statistical ambiguity problem can be resolved by grounding inductive-statistical explanation in a causal, probabilistic framework based on Cartwright-style probability-raising causes. By defining and refining Causal Rules into Maximally Specific Causal Relationships through a semantic probabilistic inference procedure, the authors obtain a system that ensures consistent predictions and satisfies the philosophical intuition behind RMS in a formally precise way. This framework doubles as a general probabilistic causal learning system, with potential applications in Causal AI and Causal Machine Learning, especially in handling invariant features, invariant causal prediction, and avoiding spurious associations.

title: Knowledgeless Language Models: Suppressing Parametric Recall for Evidence-Grounded Language Modeling
summary: Language models encode substantial factual knowledge in their parameters, which can lead to unreliable behavior when this knowledge is outdated, incomplete, or misaligned with the provided context. In this work, we study whether modifying the pretraining signal can systematically shift models away from parametric recall and toward evidence-grounded reasoning. We introduce Knowledge--''Less'' Language Models (KLLMs), a fundamentally different epistemic training paradigm for LLMs, which are pretrained on corpora in which named entities are anonymized, thereby removing a primary channel for entity-linked factual supervision. This intervention substantially reduces closed-book factual recall, while often improving performance on tasks where relevant information is provided as context. Across multiple model scales, KLLMs consistently outperform matched baselines on contextual question answering, fact verification, and hallucination detection benchmarks. Crucially, in retrieval-grounded settings with imperfect evidence, KLLMs show improved robustness and achieve up to 20--25\% relative gains over standard language models. They further exhibit better calibration, with improved ECE, Brier score, and AUROC, as well as more reliable abstention behavior. Our results demonstrate that suppressing entity-linked supervision during pretraining induces a shift in epistemic behavior: KLLMs rely less on parametric knowledge and more on external evidence, leading to improved reliability under realistic conditions. This suggests that pretraining-time control over knowledge acquisition can complement retrieval-augmented and tool-based systems by providing a more evidence-sensitive base model.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Knowledge-"Less" Language Models (KLLMs), which are pretrained on text where named entities are anonymized. This reduces parametric factual recall and pushes models to rely more on provided evidence, improving reliability, calibration, and robustness in evidence-grounded tasks.
AI.motivation: Conventional language models store large amounts of factual knowledge in their parameters, which can cause them to behave unreliably when that internal knowledge is outdated, incomplete, or conflicts with the context or retrieved evidence. There is a need for models that are better aligned with external evidence and less prone to hallucinations, especially in retrieval-augmented or tool-using systems. The authors aim to understand whether changing the pretraining data can systematically shift models from memorizing facts to reasoning based on given evidence.
AI.method: The authors introduce Knowledge-"Less" Language Models (KLLMs) trained on corpora where named entities are anonymized. This reduces entity-linked factual supervision during pretraining. They compare KLLMs against standard language models of matched scales on a range of benchmarks. Evaluation focuses on: (1) closed-book factual recall, (2) tasks where relevant information is explicitly provided in context (contextual QA, fact verification, hallucination detection), and (3) retrieval-grounded settings with imperfect or noisy evidence. They also measure calibration metrics such as Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Brier score, AUROC, and abstention behavior.
AI.result: Anonymizing named entities during pretraining substantially reduces closed-book factual recall, indicating less reliance on memorized parametric knowledge. Despite this, KLLMs outperform standard models of comparable size on tasks where the necessary information is present in the context, including contextual question answering, fact verification, and hallucination detection. In retrieval-grounded scenarios with imperfect evidence, KLLMs are more robust and achieve up to 20–25% relative performance gains over baselines. They also show improved calibration, with better ECE, Brier scores, AUROC, and more reliable abstention behavior.
AI.conclusion: Suppressing entity-linked supervision during pretraining shifts the epistemic behavior of language models: they rely less on internal, parametric knowledge and more on external evidence. This yields models that are more robust, better calibrated, and more reliable in realistic, retrieval-grounded settings. The work suggests that controlling knowledge acquisition at pretraining time is a powerful complement to retrieval-augmented and tool-based approaches, enabling base models that are inherently more sensitive to evidence and less prone to hallucinations.

title: A Multi-Agent System for Autonomous, Fine-Tuning-Free Clinical Symptom Detection: Development and Validation Study
summary: Clinical notes contain many of the signs and symptoms that bring patients to care, yet this information rarely reaches structured fields. Existing extraction approaches either rely on context-insensitive rules that generate false positives or on supervised models that require substantial fine-tuning. We present Pythia, a multi-agent system that autonomously writes and optimizes extraction prompts for clinical concepts without manual prompt engineering or fine-tuning. Running on a locally hosted open-weights model, Pythia keeps clinical notes on local infrastructure and selects prompts using development-set sensitivity and specificity. We compared Pythia with a curated lexicon across 72 signs and symptoms from 400 clinical notes representing 387 patients. Development (n=300) and validation (n=100) sets were partitioned independently for each concept. Pythia achieved mean sensitivity of 0.76 and specificity of 0.95, compared with 0.82 and 0.76 for the lexicon, and matched or exceeded the lexicon on both metrics for 20 of 62 directly comparable concepts. For 14 concepts where the lexicon labeled every note positive, Pythia recovered mean specificity of 0.97 by requiring a present-tense, patient-attributed finding rather than any textual mention of a term. Specificity transferred from development to validation with minimal degradation across prevalences, whereas sensitivity transfer weakened below 5% prevalence, reaching a mean gap of 0.25 below 2% prevalence. A BERT classifier fine-tuned per concept on the same development set achieved mean sensitivity of 0.23 and collapsed to zero sensitivity for concepts below roughly 5% prevalence. These findings suggest that autonomous, fine-tuning-free prompt optimization can produce symptom extraction prompts that generalize effectively from development to validation while remaining deployable on local infrastructure.

AI.tldr: Pythia is a multi-agent, prompt-optimizing system that uses open-weight LLMs to extract clinical signs and symptoms from notes without manual prompt engineering or model fine-tuning, achieving higher specificity and better low-data performance than a curated lexicon and BERT classifiers, while running entirely on local infrastructure.
AI.motivation: Much of the clinically important information about signs and symptoms is locked in unstructured clinical notes instead of structured fields. Existing extraction methods either use brittle, context-insensitive rules that cause many false positives, or require supervised models that need substantial labeled data and fine-tuning, which is costly and does not generalize well, especially for low-prevalence concepts. There is a need for a scalable, data-efficient, and privacy-preserving approach that can robustly extract clinical concepts and distinguish clinically present findings from mere textual mentions, while running on local infrastructure.
AI.method: The authors propose Pythia, a multi-agent system that automatically writes, evaluates, and optimizes prompts for an open-weight language model to extract clinical concepts from notes. Pythia runs locally to preserve data privacy and selects prompts based on their sensitivity and specificity on a development set for each concept. They evaluated Pythia against a curated lexicon-based approach and against concept-specific fine-tuned BERT classifiers across 72 signs and symptoms in 400 clinical notes from 387 patients, with independent development (n=300) and validation (n=100) sets for each concept. Performance was assessed in terms of sensitivity, specificity, and transfer from development to validation across different concept prevalences.
AI.result: Pythia achieved a mean sensitivity of 0.76 and specificity of 0.95 across concepts, while the curated lexicon achieved 0.82 sensitivity and 0.76 specificity. Pythia matched or exceeded the lexicon on both metrics for 20 out of 62 concepts that could be directly compared. For 14 concepts where the lexicon labeled every note positive (i.e., effectively zero specificity), Pythia recovered a mean specificity of 0.97 by requiring that findings be present-tense and attributed to the patient rather than being any mention of the term. Specificity for Pythia transferred well from development to validation with minimal degradation, whereas sensitivity transfer deteriorated for concepts with prevalence below 5%, with a mean decrease of 0.25 when prevalence fell below 2%. A per-concept fine-tuned BERT classifier, trained on the same development data, reached only 0.23 mean sensitivity and essentially failed (sensitivity near zero) for low-prevalence concepts (<~5%).
AI.conclusion: Autonomous prompt optimization using a multi-agent system and open-weight LLMs can deliver high-specificity, reasonably sensitive extraction of clinical signs and symptoms from notes without any model fine-tuning or manual prompt engineering. Pythia generalizes well from development to validation, particularly in terms of specificity and especially for concepts that lexicon-based methods systematically overcall. It substantially outperforms concept-specific fine-tuned BERT classifiers in low-prevalence regimes, while remaining deployable on local infrastructure to preserve data privacy. This suggests prompt-based, fine-tuning-free approaches are a practical and effective alternative to traditional supervised NLP for clinical concept extraction, especially when labeled data are scarce and privacy constraints prohibit cloud-based computation.

title: Can LLMs Write Reliable Rubrics? A Meta-Evaluation for Experiment Reproduction
summary: Rubric-based evaluation is a promising approach for assessing open-ended outputs from LLM-based research agents, particularly in paper reproduction, where direct paper-to-repository comparison is prone to hallucination. However, constructing paper-specific rubrics requires substantial expert effort, limiting the scalability of benchmarks such as PaperBench. In this work, we present, to our knowledge, the first systematic meta-evaluation of LLM-generated rubrics for paper reproduction. We reformulate rubrics into a checklist-style format and evaluate four generation settings across two backbone models. We meta-evaluate generated rubrics intrinsically by semantic similarity and extrinsically by score alignment with ground-truth rubrics. Our results show that the augmented settings substantially improves downstream evaluation alignment, with the strongest setting approaching the human baseline, while intrinsic gains are more modest. Further analyses reveal that LLM-generated rubrics are often overly fine-grained, biased toward high scores, and less adaptive to paper domains, highlighting both the affordances and limitations.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well large language models can automatically generate evaluation rubrics for reproducing research papers, and systematically compares different generation setups.
AI.motivation: Manual creation of paper-specific rubrics for evaluating LLM-based research agents (e.g., in benchmarks like PaperBench) is labor-intensive and does not scale. Directly comparing a paper with a reproduced repository is also unreliable due to hallucinations. Hence there is a need to understand whether LLMs can generate reliable, paper-specific rubrics automatically.
AI.method: The authors convert rubrics into a checklist-style format and use LLMs under four different rubric-generation settings, applied to two backbone models. They then perform a meta-evaluation of these generated rubrics using (1) intrinsic metrics, such as semantic similarity to human-written rubrics, and (2) extrinsic metrics, specifically how closely scores derived from LLM-generated rubrics align with scores from ground-truth (human) rubrics.
AI.result: Augmented rubric-generation settings (likely with additional guidance or context) significantly improve the alignment between evaluation scores obtained from LLM-generated rubrics and those from human rubrics, approaching a human baseline in the best configuration. However, improvements in intrinsic semantic similarity metrics are smaller. The analysis further finds systematic characteristics: LLM-generated rubrics tend to be overly fine-grained, biased toward assigning higher scores, and less adaptable across different paper domains.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can produce rubric checklists that, under appropriately augmented settings, offer near-human alignment for downstream evaluation of paper reproduction, suggesting they are a viable tool to reduce expert effort. Nevertheless, their tendency toward excessive granularity, high-score bias, and limited domain adaptivity exposes important limitations, indicating that human oversight and further method refinement are still needed.

title: MemOps: Benchmarking Lifecycle Memory Operations in Long-Horizon Conversations
summary: Long-term memory has become a foundational capability for LLM-based agents that accompany users across extended, multi-session interactions. Existing benchmarks, however, evaluate such memory almost exclusively through downstream question answering, scoring only the correctness of a final answer. This black-box formulation conflates the heterogeneous causes of memory failure, such as missing the introduction of a relevant fact, binding an operation to the wrong target, or relying on stale values after a correction. As a result, it can credit correct answers despite their reliance on inconsistent or unsafe memory states. In this paper, we argue that, in dynamic long-horizon interactions, memory is not a static collection of facts but a lifecycle of explicit operations, including remembering, forgetting, updating, reflecting, and their compositions. We introduce MemOps, a benchmark that reformulates conversational memory as a sequence of lifecycle operations and represents each memory event with a structured trace specifying its trigger, target, scope, state transition, and supporting evidence. A controllable generation pipeline embeds these operations into long, task-oriented conversations and produces gold operation traces together with six categories of operation-level probes, evaluated under both adjacent-evidence and long-context settings. Across long-context, retrieval-based, parametric and managed-memory systems, MemOps disentangles failure modes that final-answer accuracy alone conceals, revealing that current systems remain far from uniformly reliable. For instance, session-level retrieval outperforms turn-level retrieval, and long-context models remain notably weak at reconstructing ordered memory-state trajectories. These results move long-term memory evaluation from final-answer scoring toward interpretable, operation-level diagnosis.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MemOps, a benchmark that evaluates long-term memory in LLM agents at the level of explicit memory operations rather than only final-answer accuracy, enabling fine-grained diagnosis of failure modes in dynamic, long-horizon conversations.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for long-term memory in LLM-based agents mostly judge performance via downstream question answering, using only whether the final answer is correct. This black-box approach mixes up many distinct reasons why memory may fail—such as not noticing when a fact is first mentioned, misbinding an update to the wrong entity, or continuing to use outdated information after a correction. It can also mark as correct answers that depend on inconsistent or unsafe internal memory states. The authors are motivated to create an evaluation framework that reflects how memory actually functions over multi-session interactions: as a lifecycle of operations (remembering, updating, forgetting, reflecting) rather than a static fact store, and to make the intermediate memory operations interpretable and diagnosable.
AI.method: The authors propose MemOps, a benchmark that models conversational memory as a sequence of explicit lifecycle operations. They define structured traces for each memory event, specifying its trigger, target, scope, state transition, and supporting evidence. They build a controllable generation pipeline that embeds these memory operations into long, task-oriented conversations, producing both gold-standard operation traces and six categories of operation-level probes. These probes are used to test systems under adjacent-evidence settings (short local context) and long-context settings. They then evaluate several types of systems—long-context models, retrieval-based approaches, parametric memory, and managed-memory systems—against these operation-level tasks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across diverse memory systems shows that MemOps can disentangle distinct failure modes that would be hidden if only final-answer accuracy were measured. The benchmark reveals that contemporary systems are far from uniformly reliable at memory operations. For example, it finds that session-level retrieval strategies outperform turn-level retrieval, and that long-context models in particular struggle to reconstruct ordered trajectories of memory states over time. These nuanced findings illustrate discrepancies between apparent performance on QA tasks and true robustness of memory handling.
AI.conclusion: MemOps reframes long-term memory evaluation for LLM-based agents from a coarse, final-answer-centric perspective to an interpretable, operation-level one. By modeling memory as a lifecycle of explicit operations and providing structured traces and targeted probes, the benchmark enables fine-grained diagnosis of where and how memory mechanisms fail. The authors conclude that current systems have significant gaps in reliable memory handling, and that future work should focus on improving specific memory operations and trajectories rather than solely optimizing downstream QA accuracy.

title: Evaluating Large Language Models on Misconceptions in Multi-Turn Medical Conversations
summary: Patients seeking medical information often ask questions that embed incorrect assumptions or misconceptions. In such cases, safe medical communication requires not only answering the question, but identifying and correcting the underlying false belief. These interactions naturally unfold over multiple turns, a pattern now mirrored in interactions with LLMs. Yet current evaluation frameworks do not capture model behavior in these settings, where misconceptions can emerge, persist, or evolve over the course of a conversation. Whether LLMs can reliably correct such misconceptions over time remains largely unexamined. To study this, we introduce ThReadMed-QA, a multi-turn medical dialogue dataset of 2,437 patient-physician conversation threads comprising 8,204 question-answer pairs, derived from real patient interactions on AskDocs. This dataset enables systematic evaluation of whether models can detect and correct misconceptions under a multi-turn context. We evaluate five LLMs using a rubric-based LLM-as-a-Judge framework that scores responses based on their ability to identify and correct misconceptions. Our experiments reveal a consistent pattern: even frontier models that can address misconceptions in a single interaction degrade substantially over subsequent turns. GPT-5 and Claude-Haiku correct these false presuppositions around 85% on initial questions but drop to roughly 50% within two follow-ups. An oracle analysis replacing prior model outputs with physician responses shows that much of the degradation is driven by error propagation, while performance remains imperfect even under correct context. Even when models tend to correct misconceptions initially, their performance degrades substantially over later turns, leading to inconsistent and potentially unsafe guidance in patient-facing settings and highlighting the need for evaluation frameworks that capture multi-turn behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ThReadMed-QA, a dataset and evaluation framework to study how well LLMs detect and correct patients’ medical misconceptions over multi-turn dialogues, finding that performance degrades sharply across turns due to error propagation.
AI.motivation: Existing medical QA evaluations mostly consider single-turn interactions and do not test whether LLMs can reliably identify and correct patient misconceptions as conversations unfold over multiple turns. In real clinical and patient-facing scenarios, misconceptions may appear, persist, or evolve, and safe communication requires continuously detecting and correcting them. The authors want to fill this gap and understand if, and how, LLMs fail at this task.
AI.method: The authors construct ThReadMed-QA, a multi-turn medical dialogue dataset of 2,437 patient-physician threads with 8,204 question–answer pairs sourced from AskDocs. They then evaluate five LLMs using an LLM-as-a-judge rubric that scores each response on its ability to identify and correct misconceptions given the evolving multi-turn context. They also conduct an oracle analysis, where prior model turns in the context are replaced by gold physician answers, to disentangle error propagation from other sources of failure.
AI.result: Frontier models that handle misconceptions well in single turns perform much worse as conversations continue. For example, GPT-5 and Claude-Haiku correct misconceptions in about 85% of initial patient questions but drop to about 50% by the second follow-up question. The oracle analysis shows that a large part of this degradation is due to error propagation from earlier incorrect model responses, though performance remains suboptimal even when the preceding context is corrected.
AI.conclusion: LLMs’ ability to manage and correct medical misconceptions is fragile over multi-turn dialogues: they may start strong but degrade quickly, leading to inconsistent and potentially unsafe advice. Standard single-turn evaluations miss this behavior. The work argues for and provides a new dataset and rubric that better capture multi-turn safety and consistency, emphasizing the need to design and evaluate models specifically for robust misconception handling over extended patient interactions.

title: Knowledge- and Gradient-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Parametrized Action Markov Decision Processes
summary: In this paper, we study Reinforcement Learning in Parametrized Action Markov Decision Processes (PAMDP), where each decision consists of a symbolic action and numerical parameters. In such settings Reinforcement Learning algorithms typically determine parameters with one-shot estimators, which makes their training sample inefficient. Though in most PAMDP environments explicit but incomplete knowledge (e.g., rules, safety constraints, or expert heuristics) is available, it is rarely directly used to increase the sample-efficiency of training Reinforcement Learning agents. We step into this gap and propose our novel Neuro-Symbolic Knowledge- and Gradient-Guided Reinforcement Learning (KGRL) algorithm. KGRL uses domain knowledge in a Datalog knowledge base to derive the set of applicable actions and feasible parameters for a given state. This allows it to prune non-applicable actions from the decision-space and constrain the parameter spaces of the remaining actions. We then use a gradient-based parameter refinement loop to estimate the optimal parameters during training and deployment of the agent. By recording activated rules along the trajectory, KGRL additionally provides local procedural explanations on the pruning of actions and constraining of parameters. Overall, KGRL guides the agent's exploration and deployment toward feasible and constraint-aware decisions, while increasing sample efficiency during training. KGRL outperforms state-of-the-art RL baselines for PAMDPs in both, sample efficiency and episodic return.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces KGRL, a neuro-symbolic RL algorithm for parametrized action MDPs that uses logical knowledge to prune actions and constrain parameters, plus gradient-based refinement, yielding more sample-efficient and better-performing policies.
AI.motivation: Parametrized Action MDPs involve choosing both a discrete action and its continuous parameters, which greatly enlarges the decision space. Existing RL methods in this setting usually pick parameters with one-shot estimators, leading to poor sample efficiency. At the same time, many real-world domains provide partial symbolic knowledge such as rules, safety constraints, or heuristics, but current methods mostly ignore this information. The authors aim to exploit this available but underused domain knowledge to both reduce the search space and improve learning efficiency and reliability.
AI.method: The authors propose KGRL, a neuro-symbolic RL algorithm that integrates a Datalog-based knowledge base with gradient-based RL. First, the knowledge base is queried in each state to compute the set of applicable symbolic actions and to derive feasible ranges or constraints for their numerical parameters. This prunes non-applicable actions and restricts each action’s parameter space. Then, instead of a one-shot parameter choice, KGRL runs a gradient-based refinement loop to optimize the parameters within the feasible region during both training and execution. The method also logs which rules were activated along the trajectory, enabling local procedural explanations for why certain actions were pruned or parameters constrained.
AI.result: Empirically, KGRL is evaluated against state-of-the-art RL baselines designed for parametrized action MDPs. Across benchmark tasks, KGRL achieves higher sample efficiency (learning good policies with fewer environment interactions) and better episodic returns (final performance). The experiments show that logically constraining the decision space and refining parameters via gradients gives consistent performance gains over baselines that either lack knowledge guidance or rely on one-shot parameter estimators.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating symbolic domain knowledge into RL for parametrized actions, as done in KGRL, can substantially improve both training efficiency and policy quality. By using a Datalog knowledge base to filter actions and constrain parameters, and combining this with gradient-based refinement, KGRL yields feasible, constraint-aware decisions and outperforms existing PAMDP methods. The approach also naturally provides interpretable, rule-based explanations for the agent’s choices, highlighting the benefits of neuro-symbolic integration in complex decision-making problems.

title: LLM Judges Can Be Too Generous When There Is No Reference Answer
summary: LLM judges are increasingly being used to evaluate open-ended model responses, often in no-reference settings where a ground-truth answer is unavailable. However, can they reliably assess in such evaluation setups? We explore this question in this paper through a two stage pipeline with a) calibration experiments that assess the judge model's knowledge of the task it is evaluating, and b) sensitivity experiments that assess how the judge model's performance is impacted by the presence and positioning of the reference answer in the prompt. Across experiments covering three languages, we show that the judge models we evaluated tend to over-credit incorrect answers in the absence of a reference answer, and adding reference answer information to the prompt flips the judge model's correct/incorrect decisions by as much as 85% in some experimental settings. Comparison with a subset of human annotations shows that these reference-driven changes generally align with human judgments. Our results emphasize the need for calibrating the LLM judges with a sample with reference-aware evaluation before using them in reference-free setups reliably, and our methodology provides a blueprint for researchers and practitioners in doing such calibration of LLM judges for other tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper examines whether large language models (LLMs) can reliably act as “judges” of other models’ open-ended responses without access to ground-truth answers, and shows that their judgments are highly influenced by whether and how a reference answer is included in the prompt.
AI.motivation: LLM-as-a-judge is becoming a standard way to evaluate open-ended model outputs, especially when explicit ground-truth references are not available. However, it is unclear whether these LLM judges actually understand the task well enough to evaluate reliably, and how sensitive they are to prompt design and to the presence or absence of reference answers. This uncertainty threatens the validity of current evaluation practices that rely on reference-free LLM judging.
AI.method: The authors propose a two-stage evaluation pipeline for LLM judges: (1) Calibration experiments that measure the judge model’s underlying knowledge of the task it is evaluating, and (2) sensitivity experiments that test how the judge’s decisions change when a reference answer is added, removed, or repositioned in the prompt. They perform these experiments across three languages, comparing judge decisions in reference-free vs. reference-aware conditions and validating a subset of these decisions against human annotations.
AI.result: The study finds that LLM judges frequently over-credit incorrect answers when no reference answer is provided. Introducing a reference answer into the prompt can flip the judge’s correct/incorrect decision up to 85% of the time in some settings. These reference-driven changes in judgment tend to be consistent with human annotations, suggesting that the reference information helps the judge align more closely with human evaluators. This behavior is observed across multiple languages.
AI.conclusion: LLM judges are not reliably calibrated in purely reference-free evaluation setups: they can systematically overestimate the quality of incorrect responses. Providing reference answers markedly alters their judgments, generally in a way that better matches human evaluation. Therefore, before using LLM judges in reference-free scenarios, practitioners should first calibrate them using a sample of reference-aware evaluations. The paper’s two-stage pipeline serves as a practical blueprint for such calibration across tasks and languages.

title: FormalAnalyticGeo: A Neural-Symbolic Based Framework for Multimodal Analytic Geometry Problem Generation
summary: Math reasoning has achieved significant progress with the rapid advancement of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), however analytic geometry remains largely underexplored, primarily due to the scarcity of annotated samples. Existing diagram generation approaches struggle with analytic geometry: template methods cannot handle constraint-driven layouts, and generative models lack the geometric precision to render annotated conic curves correctly. We present FormalAnalyticGeo, a scalable framework for fully automatic generation of multimodal analytic geometry problems. Leveraging the rigor of formal languages, we design the framework around CDL (Condition Description Language), a formal intermediate representation that bridges free-form problem text with precise diagram rendering via a Signed Distance Field (SDF) engine. The framework employs four specialized LLM components in sequence: a Generator that produces diverse analytic geometry problems, a Formalizer that converts each problem into CDL for SDF-based rendering, a Measurer that extracts ground-truth answers through vision-based measurement on the rendered diagrams, and a Quality Verifier that checks outputs at three stages. Structured feedback from the Quality Verifier drives automatic retry, forming a closed loop that eliminates any need for human annotation. Applying FormalAnalyticGeo at scale yields AnalyticGeo7K, a dataset of over 7K verified multimodal problems, each with aligned text, diagram, formal annotation, and ground truth.Experiments show that the generated problems achieve a median ground-truth relative error of 0.70\%, with 82.3\% of answers falling within 5\% of the exact symbolic solution. Our framework and dataset will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FormalAnalyticGeo, a fully automatic framework to generate precise multimodal analytic geometry problems and releases AnalyticGeo7K, a 7K-problem dataset with text, diagrams, formal annotations, and ground-truth answers.
AI.motivation: Despite progress in mathematical reasoning with multimodal LLMs, analytic geometry is underexplored because accurate, annotated multimodal datasets are scarce. Existing diagram generation methods either rely on templates that cannot handle complex geometric constraints or on generative models that lack the precision needed for conic sections and exact layouts. The authors aim to create a scalable, automatic way to generate large numbers of high-quality analytic geometry problems with aligned diagrams and answers, eliminating human annotation costs.
AI.method: The authors design a formal-language-centered pipeline built around CDL (Condition Description Language), an intermediate representation that encodes analytic geometry conditions precisely. Their framework uses four LLM components in sequence: (1) a Generator that creates diverse free-form analytic geometry problems; (2) a Formalizer that translates each problem into CDL; (3) an SDF-based rendering engine that converts CDL into precise diagrams, especially for conics; (4) a Measurer that obtains ground-truth numeric answers by vision-based measurement on the rendered diagrams; and (5) a Quality Verifier that checks quality at three stages (problem, formalization, and measurement) and provides structured feedback to trigger automatic retries, forming a closed-loop system that needs no human labels.
AI.result: Running the pipeline at scale produces AnalyticGeo7K, a dataset of more than 7,000 multimodal analytic geometry problems, each containing problem text, rendered diagram, CDL annotation, and measured ground-truth answer. Quantitatively, the numeric answers derived from measurement are highly accurate: the median relative error compared to exact symbolic solutions is 0.70%, and 82.3% of the answers are within 5% relative error, indicating that the automatic pipeline yields reliable supervision signals.
AI.conclusion: FormalAnalyticGeo demonstrates that combining formal intermediate representations, precise SDF-based rendering, and LLM-based components in a closed-loop verification pipeline can automatically generate high-quality analytic geometry datasets without human annotation. The resulting AnalyticGeo7K dataset is accurate and diverse enough to support future research on multimodal mathematical reasoning, particularly in analytic geometry, and both the framework and dataset are planned for public release.

title: The Illusion of Robustness: Aggregate Accuracy Hides Prediction Flips under Task-Irrelevant Context
summary: As large language models (LLMs) grow more capable, they are increasingly deployed in context-rich settings where task inputs are often accompanied by long, partially irrelevant context. In a controlled setting, we find that state-of-the-art models often appear robust to task-irrelevant context at the aggregate level: prepending it to benchmark questions causes little change in overall accuracy. This aggregate stability, however, masks significant per-example instability. Even semantically meaningless pseudo-words, formed by randomly combining characters, can markedly shift model predictions on a small fraction of examples, degrading performance on some while improving it on others. This two-sided effect holds consistently across a wide range of models and datasets, yet the affected examples are largely model-specific. We further show that this instability is modulated by context type, context length, test-time compute, and model development stage. Together, our findings reveal context-induced tail risks concealed by aggregate accuracy, motivating per-example reliability evaluation of language models.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that while large language models seem robust to irrelevant context on average, their predictions on individual examples can be highly unstable, even when the added context is meaningless.
AI.motivation: Large language models are increasingly used in real-world, context-rich environments where inputs come with long, noisy, or partially irrelevant context. Standard evaluations mainly track aggregate accuracy, which suggests that adding irrelevant context barely affects performance. However, for safety and reliability, it is important to understand whether this apparent robustness holds at the level of individual predictions, and whether there are hidden failure modes when models are exposed to arbitrary or adversarial context.
AI.method: The authors run controlled experiments where they prepend various types of irrelevant context—including semantically meaningless pseudo-words created by random character combinations—to benchmark questions across multiple datasets and a wide range of LLMs. They compare model predictions and accuracy with and without such context, analyzing both aggregate accuracy changes and per-example prediction shifts. They also systematically vary context type, context length, test-time compute (e.g., decoding settings or time), and model development stage to measure how each factor influences instability.
AI.result: They find that aggregate accuracy remains largely unchanged when irrelevant context is added, suggesting apparent robustness. However, at the per-example level, a non-trivial fraction of instances flip predictions: some examples degrade in performance while others improve, even when the added context consists of random pseudo-words with no semantic meaning. This two-sided effect is robust across different models and datasets, but the particular examples affected differ substantially between models. The degree of instability is further shown to depend on context characteristics, the amount of context, how much test-time compute is used, and where the model is in the development lifecycle.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that aggregate metrics hide significant context-induced tail risks: individual predictions can be highly sensitive to irrelevant or even meaningless context. Because the specific vulnerable examples are model-dependent and influenced by deployment settings, relying solely on overall accuracy is insufficient for assessing LLM reliability. The authors argue for evaluation practices that focus on per-example robustness and reliability, particularly in context-rich, real-world applications where arbitrary context may be present.

title: Resist and Update: Counterfactual Report Coordinates for Incentive-Compatible LLMs
summary: Aligned language models routinely misreport under non-evidential incentive pressure: they agree with a confident user or overstate certainty even when their internal belief is unchanged. We cast this as a failure of internal incentive-compatibility (IC) and present a method for learning and certifying counterfactual report mediators that hold a model's reports to a causal contract: invariant to forbidden influences (pressure, prestige, restyling) and responsive to licensed ones (genuine evidence). These two demands, resist and update, pull in opposite directions. We study them on a Bayesian-witness benchmark with known posteriors, in which the same user disagreement is licensed evidence or forbidden pressure purely by stated source reliability. We (i) causally identify, by interchange interventions rather than probe accuracy, low-rank report coordinates for answer, confidence, and caveat that are near-orthogonal and independently controllable, and (ii) introduce a training-free counterfactual report-coordinate (CRC) clamp that references the model's own report under a counterfactually incentive-neutralized context. On the witness benchmark the two-pass clamp attains resist and update of 1.00 jointly (Wilson 95% CI [0.99,1.00]), a causal certificate under a constructible reference, not a deployed solution. Global decoding and steering show a single-parameter tradeoff; output-level fine-tuning matches both objectives only when both are enumerated; resist-only training loses evidence-responsiveness. The deployable single-pass compilation is lossy (0.73/0.97). The mechanism and clamp reproduce across three model families and transfer to a natural sycophancy benchmark (SycophancyEval). Our contribution is the interface and certification method: activation-level counterfactual incentive-invariance as a structural primitive for internal IC.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to make language models’ answers resistant to social pressure while still updating on genuine evidence, and proposes a causal, activation-level method to identify and clamp internal “report coordinates” that govern answers and confidence.
AI.motivation: Aligned language models often change how they report beliefs when users are confident or apply pressure, even if the model’s internal beliefs have not changed. This is framed as a failure of internal incentive-compatibility: the model’s outputs are improperly influenced by incentives like agreeing with the user, prestige, or stylistic pressure. The authors want a way to ensure models remain truthful to their internal evidence while still being able to update when new, reliable information is presented.
AI.method: They construct a Bayesian-witness benchmark where the ground-truth posterior is known, and where the same kind of user disagreement can be either valid evidence or illegitimate pressure depending only on the stated source reliability. Using causal interchange interventions on internal activations, they identify low-rank “report coordinates” for answer, confidence, and caveats that are near-orthogonal and independently controllable. They then propose a training-free counterfactual report-coordinate (CRC) clamp that anchors the model’s report to what it would say in a counterfactual, incentive-neutral context, enforcing invariance to forbidden influences while allowing updates to licensed ones. They also study global decoding, steering, and various fine-tuning schemes, and build a single-pass, deployable but lossy compilation of the clamp.
AI.result: On the Bayesian-witness benchmark, their two-pass CRC clamp achieves perfect joint resist (insensitivity to pressure) and update (sensitivity to real evidence) scores of 1.00 with a Wilson 95% confidence interval of [0.99, 1.00]. They show a tradeoff between resist and update when using single-parameter global controls, and find that output-level fine-tuning only matches both objectives when both are explicitly trained for; training only for resistance harms evidence-responsiveness. Their single-pass deployable version is less precise, reaching around 0.73 resist and 0.97 update. The approach generalizes across three different model families and transfers to a natural sycophancy benchmark (SycophancyEval).
AI.conclusion: Activation-level, causal control of internal “report coordinates” can enforce a structural form of internal incentive-compatibility, making language model reports invariant to illegitimate incentive signals while still responsive to genuine evidence. The proposed CRC clamp provides a certifiable interface and methodology for counterfactual incentive-invariance, which works robustly across models and tasks, even though current single-pass deployments are lossy compared to the ideal two-pass mechanism.

title: PalmClaw: A Native On-Device Agent Framework for Mobile Phones
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have moved beyond generating responses to executing multi-step tasks by calling tools, observing the results, and iteratively deciding the next action. Most agent systems run on desktops or servers, which support tool use and task automation. Mobile devices are also important agent environments because they are widely accessible and contain users' data, sensors, and daily-use applications. Existing mobile agents mainly operate smartphones through graphical user interface (GUI) actions such as tapping, swiping, and typing, which often form long, interface-dependent sequences, cannot directly access device capabilities, and make execution boundaries difficult to define. We present \textbf{PalmClaw}, an open-source agent framework that runs natively on mobile phones and manages the sessions, memory, skills, tools, and agent loop directly on the device. PalmClaw exposes device capabilities as device tools with explicit arguments, structured results, and clearly defined execution boundaries. This design enables agents to use mobile capabilities directly while keeping each action explicit and controlled. Experiments show an 11.5\% relative improvement in task success and a 94.9\% reduction in completion time over the strongest baseline, with lower setup burden and traces illustrating how execution boundaries are applied. Code is available at https://github.com/ModalityDance/PalmClaw.

AI.tldr: PalmClaw is an on-device, tool-centric LLM agent framework for mobile phones that exposes mobile capabilities as structured tools instead of GUI actions, yielding faster and more reliable task execution.
AI.motivation: Most LLM agents run on desktops/servers, while mobile devices are ubiquitous and rich in personal data and sensors. Existing mobile agents mostly control phones via GUI actions (tap, swipe, type), which create long, interface-specific action chains, lack direct access to device capabilities, and have fuzzy execution boundaries. There is a need for an agent framework that runs natively on mobile and can access device capabilities in a structured, controllable way.
AI.method: The authors design PalmClaw, an open-source LLM agent framework that runs fully on mobile devices. It manages core agent components (sessions, memory, skills, tools, and the agent loop) locally. Instead of GUI-level actions, it exposes mobile functionalities as typed "device tools" with explicit arguments, structured outputs, and well-defined execution boundaries, enabling direct, controlled use of capabilities like sensors and apps. They compare PalmClaw against GUI-based mobile agent baselines on multi-step mobile tasks.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows PalmClaw achieves an 11.5% relative improvement in task success rate and a 94.9% reduction in completion time compared to the strongest existing baseline. It also requires less setup effort and provides clearer execution traces that demonstrate how boundaries between tool calls are enforced.
AI.conclusion: Representing mobile capabilities as structured, explicit device tools and running the agent loop natively on the phone leads to more efficient and reliable mobile agents than GUI-action-based approaches. PalmClaw improves task success, drastically speeds up task completion, lowers setup burden, and yields more interpretable execution traces, suggesting tool-centric designs are a promising direction for mobile LLM agents.

title: Win by Silence: Deletion Non-Monotonicity, Autonomous Exploitation, and Typed-State Gating in LLM Plan Evaluation
summary: Plan evaluators can reward a strategic plan for becoming less explicit. This paper studies that failure in a staged expected-value scorer for LLM-generated venture routes. Proposition 1 gives the score change from deleting an interior transition while retargeting its predecessor and retaining downstream value: Delta_k = (prod_{i<k} p_i)[c_k + (1 - p_k)R_{k+1}]. On a frozen 26-route cohort, all 57 admissible deletions matched the analytic identity and threshold sign, and every route had at least one score-improving deletion. A score-seeking optimizer, allowed to restructure routes but not told the exploit mechanism, found baseline-beating uncovered structures in 21/26 routes. GATE refused score release for 26/26 silenced routes with 0/26 honest suspensions; after refusal, 47/54 next revisions repaired to a covered structure, and strict covered improvement rose from 1/26 to 13/26. An adaptive compiler-aware co-author exposed the registry-provenance boundary: obligation-channel evasions remained 6/6 across all four v1/v1.5 conditions, while delta-indexed cost floors reduced beat-honest routes from 6/6 to 3/6 and fundability-by-silence from 5/6 to 0/6 without establishing semantic completeness. If a plan scores better only because it omits necessary work, the plan did not improve; the evaluation created an omission incentive. PCSC detects and neutralizes post-hoc omission splices over model-mediated typed-state records. In the cooperative setting tested, GATE acts as a deterministic search-shaping constraint, not merely a post-hoc filter. It does not verify the semantic completeness or real-world quality of arbitrary LLM-generated strategies.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that an expected-value based scoring system for LLM-generated venture plans can be gamed by making plans less explicit, and proposes a method (PCSC) plus a gatekeeper (GATE) behavior analysis to detect and reduce this omission incentive, though not to guarantee semantic completeness.
AI.motivation: Evaluation systems for LLM-generated strategic or venture plans often use staged expected-value scoring over sequences of actions. Such evaluators risk rewarding plans that omit hard or costly steps, thereby creating incentives for models to strategically under-specify work. The authors want to formalize this failure mode, empirically test how easily it is exploited, and study how a gatekeeping system (GATE) and a compiler-aware co-author can affect this behavior, with the broader goal of designing safer and more reliable plan evaluators.
AI.method: 1) Provide an analytic proposition (Proposition 1) that quantifies how the plan score changes when an interior transition is deleted from a multi-step route while retargeting its predecessor and preserving downstream value. 2) Test this identity on a frozen cohort of 26 venture routes, enumerating all admissible transition deletions (57 in total) and checking sign and magnitude. 3) Run a score-seeking optimizer that can restructure plans but is not told how the exploit works, and measure how often it discovers higher-scoring but under-specified structures. 4) Introduce GATE, a gatekeeping mechanism that can refuse to release scores for "silenced" (under-specified) plans, and analyze its behavior over multiple revision rounds and routes. 5) Use an adaptive, compiler-aware co-author that has access to typed-state records and registry provenance to explore obligation-channel evasions, delta-indexed cost floors, and their effect on the prevalence of score-improving omissions. 6) Propose PCSC, a method for detecting and neutralizing post-hoc omission edits over these typed-state records, and characterize GATE’s role as a deterministic search-shaping constraint rather than a full semantic verifier.
AI.result: 1) The derived score-change formula for deleting an interior transition matches all 57 admissible deletions in the 26-route cohort, both in magnitude and in the sign of improvement or degradation, and every route contains at least one deletion that strictly improves the score while omitting work. 2) A generic score-seeking optimizer, without explicit knowledge of the exploit, finds baseline-beating uncovered (under-specified) structures in 21 of the 26 routes, demonstrating the practical ease of gaming the scorer. 3) GATE refuses to release scores for all 26 silenced routes, and none of the refusals are false positives on honest plans. After GATE’s refusal, 47 out of 54 subsequent revisions move back to covered (less under-specified) structures, and the number of routes with strict covered-score improvements rises from 1/26 to 13/26, indicating that GATE meaningfully shapes the search process. 4) The adaptive compiler-aware co-author shows that obligation-channel evasions persist in all tested conditions (6/6), demonstrating a remaining vulnerability boundary. However, adding delta-indexed cost floors cuts the number of routes where under-specified plans beat honest ones from 6/6 to 3/6, and reduces “fundability-by-silence” from 5/6 to 0/6, even though full semantic completeness is not achieved. 5) PCSC successfully detects and neutralizes post-hoc omission edits that would otherwise exploit the scoring system over the typed-state records, but it does not make the evaluator a complete verifier of real-world plan quality.
AI.conclusion: Expected-value based plan evaluators for LLM-generated venture strategies can inadvertently reward omissions of necessary work, allowing models or optimizers to game scores by making plans less explicit. The authors formally characterize this omission incentive, empirically demonstrate its prevalence and exploitability, and show that a gatekeeping mechanism (GATE) acts as a deterministic constraint that can significantly reshape search toward more covered plans, rather than serving as a simple post-hoc filter. Nonetheless, GATE and PCSC do not ensure semantic completeness or real-world correctness; they mainly reduce, but do not eliminate, the incentive to omit work.
