The paper argues that if a plan scores better only by leaving out necessary steps, the issue lies with the evaluation design, and it proposes PCSC and cost-floor techniques as partial mitigations while highlighting the need for more robust evaluators that directly align scores with genuine plan quality.

title: Dynamic Resource Allocation for Ensemble Determinization MCTS
summary: Simulation-based algorithms are especially suited for high-uncertainty environments such as adversarial board games with significant elements of randomness and hidden information. In particular, several Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) variants are commonly used in such domains. In this paper, we propose a series of enhancements for Ensemble Determinization MCTS, introducing two axes for dynamic resource allocation. First, Dynamic Number of Determinizations, increases or decreases the number of currently used determinization trees depending on the behavior of so-far search. Second, Dynamic Simulation Allocation, splits the simulation budget nonuniformly across the determinization trees, using simulation-to-simulation decisions to choose the tree with potentially the best knowledge gain. As benchmark domains, we used three popular tabletop games: Jaipur, Lost Cities, and Splendor. Testing our proposed enhancements in iteration- and time-based settings showed that particular configurations yield a statistically significant increase in the algorithm's strength.

AI.tldr: They improve Ensemble Determinization MCTS for stochastic, imperfect-information games by dynamically deciding how many determinizations to use and how to distribute simulations among them, achieving statistically stronger play in several tabletop games.
AI.motivation: Simulation-based search methods like MCTS work well in games with randomness and hidden information, but standard determinization-based approaches often waste computation by using a fixed number of determinizations and distributing simulations uniformly, regardless of how informative each determinization is. The authors want to make better use of limited search budget in such uncertain environments.
AI.method: They enhance Ensemble Determinization MCTS with two dynamic mechanisms: (1) Dynamic Number of Determinizations, which adapts the size of the ensemble by adding or removing determinization trees based on online feedback from the search; and (2) Dynamic Simulation Allocation, which adaptively assigns more rollouts to the determinization trees expected to yield higher information gain, using decisions made on a per-simulation basis. They empirically evaluate various configurations of these mechanisms in both iteration-limited and time-limited settings.
AI.result: On three popular stochastic, imperfect-information tabletop games—Jaipur, Lost Cities, and Splendor—certain configurations of their dynamic mechanisms produce a statistically significant improvement in playing strength compared to baseline Ensemble Determinization MCTS.
AI.conclusion: Dynamically adjusting both the number of determinizations and the distribution of simulations among them makes Ensemble Determinization MCTS more effective in high-uncertainty tabletop games, and careful configuration of these mechanisms can yield reliably stronger play within the same computational budget.

title: Audio-Native Speech Recognition with a Frozen Discrete-Diffusion Language Model
summary:
Automatic speech recognition is dominated by autoregressive decoders that emit one token at a time. We ask whether a discrete diffusion language model can transcribe speech instead, refining a whole transcript in parallel over a small number of denoising steps. We train an audio-native interface for DiffusionGemma, a 26B mixture-of-experts model that generates text by uniform, random-token discrete diffusion rather than the absorbing-mask scheme common to recent diffusion language models. A frozen Whisper encoder supplies acoustic features, a lightweight projector maps them into the model embedding space, and low-rank adapters let the frozen backbone attend to the new modality.
  About 42M parameters are trained, which is 0.16 percent of the backbone. We find that the natural training objectives fail to ground the audio because their gradient reaches the projector only through attention that has already dismissed it. A connectionist temporal classification loss applied through the frozen output head breaks this deadlock. The resulting model reaches 6.6 percent word error rate on LibriSpeech test-clean, transcribes in roughly eight parallel steps regardless of utterance length, and uses a single adapter trained on six languages, which we evaluate here on English, Hindi, and Mandarin.

AI.tldr: They build a speech recognizer using a discrete diffusion language model (DiffusionGemma) with a frozen Whisper encoder and small adapters, achieving competitive word error rate with parallel (non-autoregressive) decoding.
AI.motivation: Most ASR systems rely on autoregressive decoders that predict tokens one by one, which can be slow and inherently sequential. The authors want to know if a discrete diffusion language model, which refines entire sequences in parallel over a few steps, can serve as an efficient and accurate alternative for speech transcription, and how to effectively connect audio features to such a model.
AI.method: They connect a frozen Whisper encoder (for acoustic features) to DiffusionGemma, a 26B MoE discrete diffusion LM that uses uniform random-token diffusion. A lightweight projector maps Whisper features into the LM’s embedding space, and low-rank adapters let the frozen LM backbone attend to audio. They initially try natural diffusion-style training objectives but find that gradients to the projector are ineffective; they then introduce a CTC loss applied through the frozen output head to properly ground the audio. Only ~42M new parameters (projector + adapters) are trained while keeping the large backbone frozen.
AI.result: With the CTC-based training setup, the model attains a 6.6% WER on LibriSpeech test-clean, using about eight diffusion denoising steps for transcription regardless of utterance length. A single adapter setup trained on six languages works across multiple languages, and they report results specifically for English, Hindi, and Mandarin.
AI.conclusion: A large discrete diffusion language model, when equipped with a frozen speech encoder and small trainable adapters, can perform competitive ASR in a fully parallel fashion with a constant number of refinement steps. However, naïve training objectives fail to properly couple audio and text; adding a CTC loss through the frozen output head is crucial to make the audio grounding work. This approach suggests diffusion LMs can be practical non-autoregressive backbones for multilingual ASR with minimal task-specific parameters.

title: Do AI Agents Know When a Task Is Simple? Toward Complexity-Aware Reasoning and Execution
comment: 27 pages, 8 figures, 8 tables. Code and benchmark: https://github.com/eejyin/Do-AI-Agents-Know-When-a-Task-Is-Simple-Toward-Complexity-Aware-Reasoning-and-Execution
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly automate multi-step engineering and informatics workflows, yet they rarely ask how much effort a task actually requires. They often follow a maximum-context-first strategy--re-reading files and dependencies they have already seen--turning a one-line edit into a small code-base audit. We argue the missing capability is task-aware execution-scope estimation: judging a task's difficulty, the information it truly needs, and the shortest reliable path before committing budget. We formalize minimum-sufficient execution and the Agent Cognitive Redundancy Ratio (ACRR), and propose E3 (Estimate, Execute, Expand): the agent estimates an initial operating point, executes a minimum viable path, and expands scope only when verification fails. On MSE-Bench--a deterministic benchmark of 121 edits in a capability-controlled simulator--E3 matches the strongest baseline's 100% success while cutting cost by 85%, tokens by 91%, and inspected files by 92%, and further beats a strong adaptive retrieval baseline by 16%; the gains survive held-out instruction wording and essentially every cost weighting. A companion real-model harness (LLM-Case) corroborates the effect on a live gpt-4o agent editing a real open-source library, with every candidate patch graded by actually running the project's real pytest suite against a measured oracle: the over-reading is milder but real, and E3 is the leanest and fastest policy at comparable task success--its one shortfall a provider rate-limit, not a wrong edit. We frame this as a controlled probe of execution redundancy, not a measurement of any deployed agent, and position task-aware execution as a step toward engineering-grounded AI (EGAI)--agents whose effort is anchored in the engineering reality of the task. We release the framework and benchmark.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces E3, a task-aware execution framework for LLM agents that minimizes redundant work while preserving reliability in code-editing workflows.
AI.motivation: LLM agents performing multi-step engineering tasks tend to over-read and re-process large portions of codebases, treating small edits as large audits. This leads to unnecessary token usage, higher computational cost, and slower execution, with agents lacking an explicit notion of how much context or effort is actually needed for a given task. The authors aim to make agents more efficient and grounded in the true engineering effort required.
AI.method: The authors formalize task-aware execution-scope estimation via two concepts: minimum-sufficient execution and the Agent Cognitive Redundancy Ratio (ACRR), which quantifies redundant cognitive effort. They propose the E3 policy (Estimate, Execute, Expand): (1) Estimate an initial minimal operating scope needed to complete the task, (2) Execute a minimum viable solution within that scope, and (3) Expand the scope only when verification indicates failure. They evaluate this approach on MSE-Bench, a deterministic benchmark of 121 controlled code edits, and in LLM-Case, a live harness where a GPT-4o agent edits a real open-source library with validation via its pytest suite.
AI.result: On MSE-Bench, E3 achieves the same 100% task success rate as the strongest baseline while reducing cost by 85%, token usage by 91%, and the number of inspected files by 92%. It also outperforms a strong adaptive retrieval baseline by 16%. These efficiency gains are robust under variations in instruction phrasing and different cost weightings. In the LLM-Case real-model setting, E3 produces efficient edits with over-reading still present but reduced, and emerges as the leanest and fastest strategy at comparable success levels; the main limitation observed is due to external provider rate limits rather than incorrect edits.
AI.conclusion: Task-aware execution-scope estimation substantially reduces redundant context processing in LLM agents without sacrificing reliability. The E3 framework demonstrates that LLM agents can be made more efficient and better aligned with the true engineering effort of tasks, supporting the broader vision of engineering-grounded AI (EGAI). The authors provide both the conceptual framework and the benchmark suite to enable further study of execution redundancy and more efficient agent design.
